summary: "Add butler API for downloading file artifacts from datastore"
description: """As we move to cloud datastores, people can no longer grab a file from a butler repository directly by browsing the file system.    We therefore need an API that allows a file artifact to be downloaded directly to a user workspace without going through a python object (ie not requiring a butler.get followed by an exposure.writeFits). The actual persisted file being retrieved.    For Butler API something like {{butler.getArtifacts(refs, exportDir, transfer=""""auto"""")}} should be sufficient (which would iterate through each ref, obtaining the URIs and then downloading the artifacts at those URIs).  I'm not so sure about a command line interface.    The simplest option would be for butler query-datasets to have a mode that just writes the URIs and then have a butler get-artifacts that takes URIs as inputs (rather than adding a {{\-\-download}} option to {{query-datasets}}."""
comments: "Don't you need a way of specifying what the output filenames look like, or are they always going to be identical with the path or pseudo-path structure in the URI (and if so are they going to be relative to some particular directory)?" [SEP] "Correct. I missed the exportDir parameter. It would be like we do with butler export. The datastore root is removed and replaced with the exportDir root. The files would be in the same hierarchy though. We could discuss whether to preserve the hierarchy or not since in theory files are meant to be uniquely named." [SEP] "I suppose that since the API uses DatasetRef it does know the DataId and so therefore I could read the templates from a local configuration and change all the file names based on local overrides. Can we leave that for version 2?"

description: """DM-24355 was originally about making Database.sync a context manager to make it compatible with having other operations in the same transaction, but when I did the work on that ticket I thought I could make it transaction-friendly without that kind of API change.    That was only somewhat true - the new Database.sync implementation was safe in terms of rigorously guarding against inconsistencies even with concurrent writers, but instead it was subject to deadlocks.    DM-27217 includes an analysis of the problem and made a quick fix that makes the only practical deadlock problem (that we know of) impossible while making other deadlock problems much less likely.  The remaining work (described on DM-27217) was deferred to this ticket, and that involves making Database.sync a context manager, as originally planned."""

"""The new implementation of Database.sync introduced on DM-24355 is subject to deadlocks, as first reported [here|https://lsstc.slack.com/archives/C2JPT1KB7/p1603061206230500].    This is actually a problem with any code that uses the {{lock}} argument to {{Database.transaction}}, because that acquires a SHARED lock (in PostgreSQL) that can be owned by multiple readers, and when both readers then try to upgrade to a more exclusive lock for writing, we get a deadlock.    A partial, simple, possibly-good-enough-for-now fix is to do at a table level what we already do at a full-database level in SQLite - make explicit transaction locking EXCLUSIVE, and thus force any other processes waiting for a lock to block.  It looks like (according to the [PostgreSQL|https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/explicit-locking.html] docs) this does not prevent concurrent reads from the same table.  This is only a partial fix because I think the exclusive lock probably needs to be acquired at the outermost transaction context in order to force blocking instead of deadlocks.  That's in daf_butler's control in the Datastore case that's causing problems now, but it's in obs_base or skymap code in other concrete {{Database.sync}} usage contexts, and since {{Registry.transaction}} is a public API we can't control usage from spreading to other external code a swell.  I think the only way to express that in terms of interfaces is to go back to the original idea of this ticket, which is to make {{Database.sync}} itself into a context manager that cannot be nested within other transactions, but which starts its own transaction context in which other operations can be nested.    Anyhow, given that the intended usage of {{Database.sync}} is primarily on small, infrequently modified tables (like those that track {{instruments}} and {{Datastore}} s), I don't think that locking is likely to be a problem, except perhaps for our usage of {{sync}} on {{exposure}} in raw ingest (but even there, let's just keep an eye on that and deal with it if it becomes a problem).  Longer-term, I there's an alternate approach to all of this that uses SERIALIZABLE transaction isolation and retries instead of explicit locking, and that may scale better to more concurrency as well as be more database-agnostic.  But that's a lot harder to map to a usable Python interface, because those retries need to include all database calls in the same transaction context, not just those invoked by {{Database.sync}}, and that means we need to remember those calls in the Python layer rather than forward them on to SQLAlchemy immediately.  DM-26302 will probably require interface changes that would be similar to the kinds of interface changes this would require (i.e. having {{Database.transaction}} return a connection-holding object that would be used to make database calls, instead of those happening on the {{Database}} itself).    I am leaning towards just using this ticket for the simple/partial fix, and deferring future API-change work to other tickets, because aside from the Datastore creation race problem (which the simple fix should address), any other problems should be both extremely rare and work-around-able via manual retries.  I'll open other tickets for future work when I close this one, if necessary."""

summary: "Correctly use the `tooManyPeaks` config option in meas_deblender and meas_extensions_scarlet"
description: """The behavior for `deblend_tooManyPeaks` is wrong for both deblenders! In the docs for both config files:    https://github.com/lsst/meas_deblender/blob/master/python/lsst/meas/deblender/sourceDeblendTask.py#L92-L94    {code:python}  maxNumberOfPeaks = pexConfig.Field(dtype=int, default=0,                                         doc=(""""Only deblend the brightest maxNumberOfPeaks peaks in the parent""""                                              """" (<= 0: unlimited)""""))      So there is no actual check to skip this if `config.maxNumberOfPeaks <= 0`! It also appears that I implemented the same logic in mes:    https://github.com/lsst/meas_extensions_scarlet/blob/master/python/lsst/meas/extensions/scarlet/scarletDeblendTask.py#L595-L598    {code:python}  if len(peaks) > self.config.maxNumberOfPeaks:                  src.set(self.tooManyPeaksKey, True)                  msg = 'Parent {0}: Too many peaks, using the first {1} peaks'                  self.log.trace(msg.format(int(src.getId()), self.config.maxNumberOfPeaks))  {code}    So for mes, with the default `config.maxNumberOfPeaks == 0` it flags every parent as having too many peaks (I checked the RC run for 9813 and confirmed that this is the case). But it doesn't actually skip the blend, it just carries on as if nothing happened. It should do the same thing for meas_deblender, except that somewhere in the hsc config files there must be a value set for `maxNumberOfPeaks`, otherwise every parent with any children would be flagged with having too many peaks, just like for mes.    So this ticket is to check that when the user specifies the default (`maxNumberOfPeaks=0`) that the `tooManyPeaksKey` is not set."""

description: """It is currently difficult to match results from meas-deblender and meas_extensions_scarlet. Both deblenders start with the same """"mergeDet"""" catalog of parents, where each parent has a peak table that lists the sources that need to be deblended. Unfortunately the peak ID's are not unique, so propagating them will not help, however the peak coordinates are unique for peaks in the same tract (even across bands). In meas_extensions_scarlet we already propagate the peak x and y positions, so if we do the same for meas_deblender and persist those columns into """"deepCoadd_forced_src"""" then we should be able to make a 1-1 match between sources in meas_extensions_scarlet and meas_deblender."""

comments: "Lessons learned:    * Use [graphql-server|https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-server] instead of aiohttp-graphql (an older version of the same thing). Install `graphql-server[aiohttp]`.  * Asynchronous mode is [broken|https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-server/issues/64], but has a known fix. I filed a [pull request|https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-server/pull/67] which has been merged.  * The self-documenting nature of GraphQL is excellent.  * The need for schemas to validate requests is excellent. description: """When running the PTC analysis on BOT run 12606, many amps failed to return valid PTC curves.  I traced this to saturated images in the flat pairs, which caused NaNs in the mean/variance values.  When the code runs the _getInitialGoodPoints routine, the medianRatio parameter becomes NaN, and then all points fail.  I was able to fix this by changing the medianRatio from np.median to np.nanmedian, and then the PTC curves ran OK, but then the plotPtc.py routine failed to plot the PTCs.  As a workaround, I just eliminated the saturated flat pairs from the input deck, but long term the code needs to be robust to saturated inputs.  FWIW, I swear that this problem was not there a few weeks ago."""
comments: "I replaced {{np.median}} in {{_getInitialGoodPoints}} in {{ptc.py}}, and, similarly,  switched to {{np.nanmin}} and {{np.nanmax}} to calculate limits in the plotting routine (which is what was causing it to fail in this case).      With this, we still keep the NaNs in the {{raw}} vectors. It wasn't happening before because the raw vectors were being filled after the NaNs were discarded.     Commands: ({{w_2020_41}})          Plots:  [^PTC_det36.pdf]     " [SEP] "Small comments on the docs, but great other than that." [SEP] "I really don't understand where these NaNs are coming from.  Eliminating the saturated images removed most of the issue, but there are still afew amps that are returing NaN for no apparent reason.  I whittled it down to this simple command line, which runs very fast:    Then I added these print statements in measureMeanVarCov in ptc.py:    When I run this, amps C02 and C07 return NaN for the mean, even though the images look fine and I can print out the min and max of the array data.

description: """All CameraMappers have a {{FilterDefinitionCollection}} that relates filter and band names, including aliases, for the corresponding instrument. """RFC-730 proposes a new filter system that will support only standardized names for each band or filter, but no aliases. While new aliases will no longer be introduced after DM-27167, old files may still use them in Gen 2 (Gen 3 will internally ensure that all names are standardized). To ensure that algorithmic code can safely assume standardized names in {{FilterLabel}} regardless of whether an input Exposure was loaded from Gen 2 or Gen 3, standardize the filter names in an Exposure when loading them from Gen 2.    Note that there is no standardization problem when using {{Filter}}; the problem will only emerge after {{Exposure}} starts using {{FilterLabel}} as the primary API."""

summary: "Stop using filter alias names in DM stack"
description: """RFC-730 proposes a new filter system that will support only standardized names for each filter, but no aliases. To prepare for the new system, ensure that we are no longer introducing aliases into the codebase (in particular, as the primary name of old-style filters).    Proposed algorithm:  # Go through the filter definition file of every {{obs}} package, and make a list of names that appear in either the {{afw_name}} or {{alias}} field of a {{FilterDefinition}}, along with their corresponding {{physical_filter}}.  # Search the entire {{lsst}} and {{lsst-dm}} for hardcoded references to these names, except for those in {{FilterDefinition}} itself and calls to {{Filter::define}}, {{Filter::defineAlias}}, and {{image.utils.defineFilter}}. Calls to the {{Filter}} constructor *are* covered by the search.  # Replace each reference to an afw_name or alias with a reference to the corresponding physical_filter.    It is possible that step 2 will find no matches."""

summary: "Add task to compute and persist VisitSummary tables"
description: """Currently, the only way to access the individual visit metadata, WCS information, photoCalib, PSF size, etc, is to read in all of the components from over 100 individual {{calexp}} files (for HSC and LSSTCam).  This is a performance bottleneck for {{fgcmcal}}, {{jointcal}}, and {{pipe_analysis/visitAnalysis}} to name a few tasks.  This ticket is to add a task to {{pipe_tasks/postprocess.py}} called {{ConsolidateVisitSummaryTask}} (supporting both Gen2 and Gen3) that will consolidate all of the summary info from the constituent detectors into one {{afw}} {{ExposureCatalog}}.  This task can be more easily parallelized by the workflow manager, while it reduces the i/o burden (and input logic) on the consumers of this product significantly.  Simple tests show a reduction in time from 30-40 seconds per visit on HSC data to less than a second to retrieve the {{VisitSummary}}.  """

description: """I ran a PTC task on BOT run 12606, the first time we have the full LSSTCam.  Six CCDs failed to return results.  I am tracking down the reasons.  CCD R01_S00, detector=0 failed because it failed to create a master dark with the error """"Image contains no Pixels"""". Command string:    Error:  """
comments: "This appears to be a failure mode of the overscan code.  Outliers are found by calculating statistics over the entire overscan region, with discrepant points masked then.  For this camera, the overscan is then collapsed along the serial direction by taking the MEDIAN_PER_ROW.  If all the points in that row have been masked, then the statistic raises. For the image in the example, this was occuring on amp C14.    An updated ip_isr branch catches this failure, and sets the overscan to NaN (which will result in masking downstream, but no other value makes sense here)." [SEP] "Yes, it runs with isr.overscan.fitType=MEDIAN instead of MEDIAN_PER_ROW.  I'll run this way for now." summary: "Usability improvement suggestions for butler collection commands"
description: """I gave {{butler prune-collection}} and {{butler query-collections}} a try for the first time today when prepping for an Ops Bootcamp talk, and found some rough edges we should smooth and some missing functionality we should figure out how to support:   * My first thought was to do """"butler prune-collection REPO <collection>"""", not """"butler prune-collection REPO --collection <collection>"""", and given that the collection option is not optional, I think it probably should be a positional argument   * The error messages printed when trying to remove a RUN-type collection without --purge or passing --purge without --unstore reference the Python Registry API, and hence offer confusing suggestions about how to fix the problem from the command-line.   * It'll be common for people to want to delete either collections matching a pattern or the child collections of a CHAINED collection.  We may want to just make this into """"prune-collections"""" (i.e. plural) and accept the same kind of GLOB and many of the other arguments of query-collections, and start with a query for what to delete.  A catch here is that if the query includes both RUN and non-RUN collections, we need to be careful about passing purge=True down appropriately (and update the command-line docs accordingly).   * It'd be nice to have a way to print collection types in {{query-collections}} (these can be obtained by calling {{Registry.getCollectionType}} on the collection name).   * It'd be nice to have a way to print CHAINED collection definitions (these can be obtained by calling {{Registry.getCollectionChain}} on the collection name).  It'd also be nice to have a way to set them ({{Registry.setCollectionChain}}), too, though, so maybe these should be a new pair of subcommands rather than making the former part of {{query-collections}}.  I'm not sure.   * I don't think we're gaining much from using YAML to format the output of {{query-collections}}, as it's always just a flat list, so the """"collections:"""" heading and the """"-"""" before each line are effectively just noise.  The last two bullets undercut that argument a bit, but I think if printing/setting CHAINED definitions is its own subcommand, optionally printing collection types still doesn't represent enough structure that YAML makes sense."""
comments: "I'm a fan of using yaml.dump or astropy table for dumping the output because it lets us avoid spending any time on formatting of output and lets us focus on the content of that output. It also makes testing easier because you can parse the output in a standard manner. I don't see the ""-"" as a problem." [SEP] "I have no problem with using astropy.table here instead, especially if we can drop the column headers (as there will only be 1-2 columns and their meanings will be pretty clear).  I'd also have no problem using YAML if the outputs were naturally structured, but here it feels like exposing an implementation detail." [SEP] "I'm trying to simplify the output to minimize our coding. yaml.dump and table pretty print succeed in that."

description: """Unlike gen2, gen3 can work with multiple instruments and so therefore generally requires that an instrument be specified in a dataId or where clause when selecting datasets.    This requirement to always specify an """"unnecessary"""" instrument has generated some negative feedback.    The problem with globally declaring that a registry only has one instrument is that if you ever change your mind (say adding LATISS data to an LSSTCam registry) then all the code that assume the instrument would default now breaks.    One proposal is to treat instrument as a special property (see also DM-27152) and allow for instruments to be associated with specific collections. If you are using collection """"HSC/raw/all"""" then it is likely that a single instrument is relevant. If a collection happens to include data from multiple instruments it would not be associated with a defaulted instrument but that would be rare and in most cases would have been deliberately configured to be like that."""
comments: "I have not thought through how we would modify collections to have this additional metadata or what would need to change to make use of this metadata.    Presumably raw ingest and calibration ingest would know how to declare the instrument for the collection (maybe complaining if a different instrument was already registered) and if we create an output collection it would have to be configured with the instrument from all the input collections.    The quantum graph builder and butler.get would have to look up the input collection and add the dataId." [SEP] "I had some vague ideas for trying to do at least the schema-change part of this on DM-26692, involving a way to let dimensions be declared as special ""marker"" dimensions (in the market for a better name) that would let them be associated with collections and used to control which spatial overlaps pairs are materialized.  Instrument and skymap would both be this type." [SEP] "I was separately wondering (when thinking about DM-27147) about making Instrume

description: """The commissioning team refer to individual exposures by the """"dayobs"""", sequence number for the night, and controller (OCS vs camera) but these concepts are not known to butler registry since they are effectively instrument-specific. Butler registry knows either the exposure integer ID or the exposure """"observation identifier"""" string.    Rather than modify registry to have duplicate information (or go down the path of gen2 instrument-specific exposure registries) one option is to have a dataId preprocessor that can convert the instrument-specific concepts to something known by registry.    For the specific case of LSSTCam there is a standard mapping from the dayobs,seqnum,controller tuple to the exposure integer ID so this does not require access to the exposure record.  """
comments: "To expand on the simplest possible option:    * ""instrument"" has a special status in butler  * If ""instrument"" is seen in a dataId *and* that dataId is missing required fields, the instrument record is looked up in registry and the instrument class listed there is imported.  * A class method then runs on that class and it is given the dataId. We could consider also passing in the exposure record as a dict.  * That method returns a modified dataId that is then used for all further registry operations.    The issue is how broadly this preprocessing is applied. Is it more than just butler.get? Are we modifying WHERE strings in pipe_base?" [SEP] "Just to clarify, it's not just  {quote}  The commissioning team refer to individual exposures by the ""dayobs"", sequence number for the night  {quote}  it's a Rubin survey convention that was negotiated.  " [SEP] "It's a convention yes, but it's not required. I see many science pipelines people using the exposure_id integers in gen2. There is another ticket for als

description: """In gen2 people can specify the detector by name in a dataId.    This only seems to work for query expressions at the moment (where you can say things like exposure.name or detector.full_name).    It would be nice if a user could use the detector.full_name=R01_S10 or exposure.name=AT_O_20201002_00043 in a dataId without always requiring the integer forms. Registry does know them and in these two examples they are guaranteed to uniquely define the exposure or detector.    Aside: detector doesn't have detector.name at all so there is an inconsistency there with other dimensions."""

summary: "Add per-camera umbrella collections and levels of indirection for versioned root datasets"
description: """We've talked in the past about adding umbrella CHAINED collections for each instrument to gather raws, calibs, refcats, masks, etc.  It's time to do that now.    We should also make sure any RUN-type collections created by ConvertRepoTask are truly going to be valid forever or hidden behind CHAINED collection links.  For example, it's not appropriate to put the particular version of the HSC bright object masks we find in repos today as """"HSC/masks""""; we probably want them to go in """"HSC/masks/<version>"""" and have an """"HSC/masks"""" CHAINED collection referencing that.    Here's a more complete proposal: https://confluence.lsstcorp.org/display/DM/Standard+Collections+and+Collection+Naming+Conventions"""
comments: "I noticed during ops bootcamp prep that pipetask and writeCuratedCalibrations have different conventions for formatting times into RUN collection names.  This ticket is a good place to standardize that, since it's the point at which we reasonably hope our collection names will become stable."

summary: "Make concrete pipeline definitions usable and hide those that aren't"
description: """When more people start using Gen3, we want them to find pipeline definitions in a long-term stable location, to avoid a lot of bad cargo-culting from the beginning.  At present, the only truly usable pipeline definition (in the sense that it includes all necessary overloads) is in ci_hsc_gen3, while pipe_tasks includes many pipeline definitions that _look_ usable but actually aren't.    Here's one proposal for how to fix that, involving only naming conventions and not new code:   * Move/split ci_hsc_gen3/pipelines/CiHsc.yaml to obs_subaru/pipelines/DataReleaseProduction.yaml   * Create similar definitions in obs_lsst (for at least imSim) and obs_decam   * Rename pipe_tasks/pipelines/DataReleaseProduction.yaml to give it a leading underscore, indicating that it is intended only for inheritance or composition in other pipelines, not direct execution.   * Remove ProcessCcd.yaml, Forced.yaml, Multiband.yaml, and Coaddition.yaml from pipe_tasks (merging them into DataReleaseProduction.yaml), or rename them with an underscore prefixes as well.  Since I don't think we want to _also_ make obs_* versions of these, I would prefer to just remove them.   * Consider renaming any [_]DataReleaseProduction.yaml to [_]DRP.yaml; given how often this will be typed on the command line, I suspect this move would be appreciated.    This of course raises (at least) two questions about some functionality changes:   * Should we (also) have a non-convention way to mark a pipeline definition as """"abstract"""", and not directly runnable, to further prevent devs from thinking they can run a pipe_tasks pipeline just by passing {{--instrument}} to the pipetask invocation?  I think we should at least use an underscore-prefix convention (that's very visible, and visibility is good here), but if we can easily do more than that, it could prevent some cases of silently incorrect processing (in the sense that important configs could be missed), and that's a sufficiently serious issue that we should guard carefully against it.   * Should we have a way to label sub-pipelines within a Pipeline definition?  I think we clearly need one; the original intent of the sub-pipelines in pipe_tasks was good, even if it didn't work out.   * Should we remove the instrument command-line argument from pipetask entirely?  If we are encouraging pipeline yaml files to contain per-instrument config overrides directly _ever_ (and I think [~<USER> is on record saying we should prefer that to config-file overrides in obs_ packages, in the long-term) then it is _never_ safe to assume that just passing {{--instrument}} to pipetask is sufficient.    I am open to solving these problems via functionality changes instead of just moving pipelines around, if it's easy enough, but we need to do something before Nov. 1.     """
comments: "I don't think that we should tell people to never use the pipe_task pipelines. They can be useful when wanting to build your own, or to not bring in any overrides when trying to run something. I do think the _ convention does convey ""use only if you know what you are doing""    What do you have against sub pipelines defined that then get integrated into a final one? I think it will make it MUCH more readable that having long files. I really don't see the advantage to changing this to labels within a longer document. I do not know why we would not make obs_* versions of these too. It is also possible that different obs packages will chose to make them, and some may bundle them differently.    I know I would prefer to drop the instrument label, but I know that some people who have poked at gen3 do like it (and specifying the task with -t) I suspect that is because pipelines need time to become familiar.    I like the name DRP, but in general we were encouraging people to use class name conventions with pipeli

description: """Prepare a set of diagnostics to test bright star subtraction on RC2.    Initial notebook to contain:        * visual inspection of calexp's post-subtraction;   * """"Yuanyuan plot""""   * """"minimum mask radius""""         More in-depth descriptions of the last two will be given in the notebooks. In summary:    The Yuanyuan plot shows the average flux in increasingly large apertures around sky objects. An upward trend is seen in both DES and HSC, and is assumed to come from the halos of the increasing number of bright neighbors. Good bright star subtraction should reduce this slope, while still keeping it slightly positive.         The minimum mask radius is, conversely, determined by looking at average quantities in increasing radii around bright star themselves. In this case, we look at the average number of detections. A certain generation of HSC masks use the radius value where that number converges to the average value far from the bright star centers to define the mask radius. Once again, successful bright star subtraction should reduce this value."""

description: """In DM-27097 we are having difficulties with multiprocessing because in order to pickle RawIngestTask in that context we need to pickle the parent task. In general, ensuring that every parent task is pickleable is hard to do and it turns out that a task only needs its parent task to do some minor adjustments to internal configuration and does not use the parentTask at all other than when pickling.    Change pickling in Task so that it pickles the things it needs from the parent task but does not pickle the parent task."""
comments: "Looking at the code, the wrinkle seems to be that the taskDict attribute in subtasks uses the actual parent's taskDict so that any changes to any of the subtasks must appear in the parent task taskDict. This can't work if the taskDict is persisted independently of the parent." [SEP] "I changed tack a bit. Instead of trying to remove parent task (which seems to be impossible) I've changed how pickling works for Task so that subclasses can add additional keyword arguments to the pickling. This then allows each subclass to add new parameters in turn if needed and doesn't require each subclass to know how to pickle a Task.    I have not updated the pickling code in pipe_drivers because there aren't any tests for it and the code is going to go away with gen2." """In moving towards reading in the """"src""""-equivalent parquet tables now being persisted in {{pipe_tasks}}'s {{postprocessing.py}} script, a performance hit was encountered when trying to apply an external {{photoCalib}} to the visit-level source catalogs.  The correction is being made using {{photoCalib.instFluxToNanojansky}} which, currently, only includes overloads involving {{SourceCatalog}}s, or single entry {{SourceRecord}} or scalar values for the flux[fluxErr][position].  When reading in the parquet tables, we do not have a {{SourceCatalog}} to pass in, so this correction must be done by looping over each source to pass in the individual flux/fluxErr/position values as scalars, which is very slow (and sort of defeats the performance-boosting motivation for reading the parquet tables in the first place).  Having an override of this function that accepts arrays of flux/fluxError/position that is efficient would solve this issue.    The hint in the summary that there may be more methods on {{photoCalib}} that would benefit from having array-based overloads refers to, e.g. {{instFluxToMagnitude}}, {{magnitudeToInstFlux}}, {{calibrateCatalog}}, ....?"""
comments: "Just like the WCS issues, this functionality is actually available from python if you are clever.  Putting a friendly API would be very worthwhile (and wouldn't require any C++ fiddling, though we'd have to use the same {{Array}} method names that we did in DM-26485.    Anyway, here's what I did to get the ""calibration scaling value"" in a numpy-vectorized form that is the key to doing all the conversions between {{instFlux}}, {{magnitude}}, {{nanoJansky}}, etc: https://github.com/LSSTDESC/supreme/blob/master/supreme/patch_mapper.py#L522-L547      {code:python}      def _compute_calib_scale(self, ccd, xy):          """"""          Compute calibration scaling value          Parameters          ----------          ccd : `lsst.afw.table.ExposureRecord`             Exposure metadata for given ccd          xy : `numpy.ndarray`             Nx2 array of x/y positions to compute calibration scale.          Returns          -------          calib_scale : `numpy.ndarray`             Length N array of calibration scal

summary: "Design the API for the OWL service, first version"
description: """Here is a what I implemented on DM-17183 using GraphQL:    add_message:  * Arguments (required unless a default is specified):  ** obs_id  ** instrument  ** message_text  ** user_id  ** user_agent  ** is_human  ** is_new (defaults to False)  ** exposure_flag (defaults to none)  * Execution:  ** Check that the exposure exists in one of the (up to two) butler registries. If is_new is False (the default) then the exposure must exist. If is_new is True and the exposure does not exist, then assume it is new and use the current date to compute day_obs.  ** compute day_obs, and date_added.  ** insert the message in the database.  ** return the message    edit_message:  * Arguments:  ** id (required), the ID of the message to edit, plus and any or all of the following:  ** message_text  ** user_id  ** user_agent  ** is_human  ** exposure_flag  * Execution:  ** get the original (parent) message and use its data as defaults for any fields the user did not specify.  ** Set new message parent_id = original message id.  ** add the message.  ** set is_valid=false and date_is_valid_changed in the original message.  ** return the new message.    delete_messages:  * Arguments:  ** id: a list of message IDs  * Execution:  ** for each id:  **   if the message exists, set is_valid=False and date_is_valid_changed.  ** return the list of messages actually deleted.    find_messages:  * Arguments (all optional):  ** min_id, max_id  ** obs_id: a substring of the message ID  ** instruments: a list of instruments  ** min_day_obs, max_day_obs  ** message_text: a substring of the message text  ** user_ids: a list of user IDs  ** user_agents: a list of user agents  ** is_human  ** is_valid: defaults to true  ** exposure_flags: a list of exposure flags  ** min_date_added, max_date_added  ** has_date_is_valid_changed  ** min_date_is_valid_changed, max_date_is_valid_changed  ** has_parent_id  ** min_parent_id, max_parent_id  ** order_by: a list of fields by which to order the returned data; prefix a field name with - for descending order  * Execution:  ** Search for and return all messages matching the criteria.     All min_ values are inclusive and max_ values are exclusive, like C++ and Python.    Eventually there will be a way to find exposures, but this will not be in the first version."""

The process I was thinking was something like:    * Some user interface displays a list of exposures (or a single known exposure).  * User selects an exposure and selects the ""add comment"" action  * They enter some text/status change and click submit (user id is known)  * System takes that request (which will include the obs_id and instrument since that was known when the UI was built) and submits the new comment.  * System validates the obs_id/instrument combination to ensure that they relate to a real exposure.  * Comment is added.    The ""add comment"" API doesn't need the day_obs and it doesn't need is_valid because is_valid must be true because this is a brand new comment and day_obs can't be allowed to be inconsistent with obsid/instrument (this does though mean that for a new observation this would require a registry query to work out the dayobs or in the LSST-specific case, simply parsing the obsid). summary: "Some metric measurements include the package name and others don't"
description: """It turns out that some measurement tasks are hard coding the metric name {{PA1}} and others take a metric name and insert it into the {{Measurement}} e.g. {{AMx}}.  This means that the metric name passed to {{run}} is ignored sometimes and is not in other cases.    It seems like we are being consistent in that we do not include the package name in any of the measurements when we run the pipeline, but I'm not sure thats the best policy.  It's true that you need to know the package name to get the metric, but I think the verify system depends on the name spacing to do filtering of jobs that have metrics from multiple packages.  We should think about the best policy."""

summary: "Rewrite ndarray CMake build to use more modern idioms"
description: """[ndarray|https://github.com/ndarray/ndarray] is a header-only library that uses CMake to find dependencies, machine-generate some headers (using M4), and run tests (which includes building both C++ binaries and compiled Python modules).  But it uses only older, disfavored (and inconsistent) CMake (<= 2.8) functionality, instead of the more modular approach that is quickly becoming standard (e.g. [https://cliutils.gitlab.io/modern-cmake|https://cliutils.gitlab.io/modern-cmake/], [https://pabloariasal.github.io/2018/02/19/its-time-to-do-cmake-right/]).  CMake 3.18 is what the DM conda stack currently installs, and I think that's a reasonable target.    In particular, it's impossible to use find_package and target_link_libraries downstream to find and build against ndarray because it doesn't export the necessary information).  And I'm sure everything else about the scripts are at least a little messy, just because they're the product of a maintainer who never knew CMake well (me).    Ideally, I'd like to avoid changing the directory structure, but I'm open to that if it's important."""

summary: "Extend the Ingest system to allow pulling table contributions from a remote store"
description: """h1. Goals    Implement the HTTP-based ingest service at workers which would support uploading table contributions from a remote HTTP service using a URL provided by a client. Types of URLs supported by the new ingest service include:    The file-based resources are expected to refer to files residing at filesystems (local or remote, such as GPFS, Luster, etc.) mounted on the worker nodes. Operations with the HTTP-based resources would be equivalent to:  {code:bash}  curl <url> -X <method> -H <header> -d <data>  {code}  The newly added worker REST service would allow passing all four (of which only the {{method}} and {{url}} ones are required) parameters to the service.     h1. Modifications and improvements to the existing code    * extend Configuration service of workers with DNS hostname and port number of the worker's HTTP service  * refactor existing code of the Ingest server to support uploading table data from multiple sources  * refactor Http modules (which are presently used by HttpProcessor) to allow using base functionality of the modules in other contexts  * implement the Http-based worker ingest service  * extent the REST metadata services of the Master Replication Controller to return DNS names and port numbers of the HTTP-based worker ingest services    Other tasks:  * update and extend documentation  * bump the Ingest API version number to 2.  * test the new ingest interface at a scale    Direct links to the relevant documentation pages which hasve been updated, or added:  * API version 2: https://confluence.lsstcorp.org/display/DM/Ingest%3A+4.+Version  * Advanced options for ingesting files: https://confluence.lsstcorp.org/display/DM/Ingest%3A+9.5.+Ingesting+files+directly+from+workers  * REST service for allocating/locating chunks: https://confluence.lsstcorp.org/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=135955129  * REST service for locating regular tables: https://confluence.lsstcorp.org/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=135955132  * Worker's REST service for ingesting a single file: https://confluence.lsstcorp.org/display/DM/Ingest%3A+11.1.7.1.+Ingesting+a+single+file"""

summary: "Reading defects from data packages is very slow"
description: """I just did some profiling of 2to3 conversion and noticed that the defects writing for HSC data is dominated by the normalization step that we don't need.  We can already assume that a defects file in a obs_*_data package is optimal so I don't think we need to normalize again on read.  From a quick test it took 59 seconds to read all 112 defects files but nearly 40 seconds of that was in the normalization.    The problem is knowing when to disable normalization since at the moment the readText method has no way to disable normalization and also the pipe_tasks code for reading from obs data packages would not really want to do an """"if defects add special option""""    One option is to declare that we never normalize when reading from fits or text file, and we only normalize if a Defects is being constructed directly. This would be a two line change to the constructor call in readText and readFits.    I'm inclined to go for that option since otherwise it's impossible to even see exactly what went into a file."""
comments: "Minor change to turn off normalization by default when reading from file. This makes a huge change to the performance of write-curated-calibrations.    It does mean that we aren't using any special knowledge of these files being part of a obs data package. For that we'd need to have some generic flag added to the readText method for all IsrCalib classes along the lines of ""trust the contents of this file"" but I didn't want to get into that here and I'm happy to defer that to your IsrCalib work. If you want to change the name ""normalize_on_init"" to ""retain_contents"" or something then I'm fine to change it here. I'm currently using the name that is used for the Defects constructor for consistency." [SEP] "`fromTable` having a different default is a bit confusing, but I think I understand why it's set that way.  " [SEP] "Yes, the reason I did it like that was to let fromTable match the constructor but we need readText to default to not doing it at all whilst we work out a better plan. My understanding is that if we are passing three parameters to that method, the third should be a flux error.  It looks like flux is being passed in both columns.  I could very easily be mistaken, but it looks like a bug as it is."""
comments: "I totally agree with your assessment.  In fact, if you expect the {{*_instFluxErr}} column to exist (they don't in very rare occasions associated with deblender columns...), I believe it is the overload here you want to use (i.e. indeed leaving out the third parameter):   [https://github.com/lsst/afw/blob/master/include/lsst/afw/image/PhotoCalib.h#L306-L319|https://github.com/lsst/afw/blob/master/include/lsst/afw/image/PhotoCalib.h#L291-L304]   noting:  {code:python}  Keys of the form ""*_instFlux"" and ""*_instFluxErr"" must exist.    So, those keys must exist in your schema and you are actually a-ok (yup, looks like they are added here: https://github.com/lsst-dmsst/metric-pipeline-tasks/blob/master/python/metric_pipeline_utils/matcher.py#L23-L26).  Sorry for the noise...but may be worth a double check!" [SEP] "I concur with Lauren that I was simply mistaken in how that function overload was operating.  Reading the docstring clarifies that it is, in fact, doing what I think it should be doing."

description: """The tangled dependencies and exposed submodules in daf.butler.core have bothered me for a while, and I'd like to use this ticket as a place to discuss a way to untangle them while deferring actually doing that until any higher-priority tickets hanging off DM-27033 have merged.    I'm thinking of something like the following, to start with.   - Move core.utils out of core and into daf.butler itself (or maybe a separate package altogether, possibly with pure-Python components from lsst.utils).   - Move core.named out of core and into daf.butler itself as daf.butler._named.  These could also go outside daf.butler; I'm agnostic on that.   - Move any other modules that are dependencies of the core.dimensions subpackage out of core, into a new daf.butler._base subpackage.  That's at least timespan, time_utils, ddl, and config; it may be a few others.   - Move core.dimensions to daf.butler itself, as daf.butler._dimensions   - Move core.datasets into daf.butler itself, as daf.butler._datasets    At that point I think we can evaluate what's left of core, and see whether it should be split up further.  A lot (but not all) of what remains  after the above moves will be code that is of use to Datastore and its implementations only, and I think we should really split those off from anything Registry also cares about.    I think it's worth trying to do this before Nov. 1, but not necessary, and not something we should try to implement while any other high-priority tickets are in flight.  It may cause some downstream breakage, but of the sort that we want, because it will show us places that downstream packages are depending on daf_butler internal structure (or internal components!) in a way we'd want to discourage."""

summary: "Enable persistence of ""source"" parquet tables in obs_subaru"
description: """In DM-22266, the {{pipe_analysis}} scripts are being converted to have the option of reading in the parquet files now being persisted via {{pipe_tasks}}'s  postprocessing.py script. For the coadds, we are reading in the {{deepCoadd_obj}} datasets (added on DM-13770), which consist of a single parquet file per patch that is a merge of the {{deepCoadd_meas}}, {{deepCoadd_forced_src}} and {{deepCoadd_ref}} tables for all filters for a given dataset. As such, these all follow the same column naming conventions as those in their {{afwTable}} equivalents (i.e. they do not follow the DDPD-ified naming conventions; those are persisted as the {{objectTable}} dataset). For the visit level qa analysis in {{pipe_analysis}}, we would like to follow a similar pattern, but using the parquet equivalent of the {{src}} catalogs. The dataset for these is {{source}} (added as part of DM-24062). However, the default in {{singleFrameDriver.py}} is to NOT persist these nor the DPDD-ified {{sourceTable}} versions. The latter got a config override in {{obs_subaru}}, so they do get persisted for HSC processing. While we may eventually move to only using the DPDD-ified tables, in the interim, having the parquet tables with the original column names is desired (especially for maintaining the ability to read in the {{afwTables}} for older repos that didn’t get the {{postprocessing.py}} step, so have no parquet output).    As such, the {{doSaveWideSourceTable}} config in {{singleFrameDriver.py}}, which currently defaults to {{False}}, will be also be overridden in {{obs_subaru}} to {{True}} so that these visit-level {{src}}-like parquet files will be available for future RC2 processing runs.    It will be for a future decision (and RFC!) whether any of the defaults should be changed in {{singleFrameDriver.py}} itself and/or if we move to using the DPDD-ified versions only."""
comments: "This is just config change right?" [SEP] "Correct.  In fact, just an override being added to {{obs_subaru}}, so only HSC processing will be affected." summary: "Update Functor implementation to use gen3 butler/parquet access"
description: """Gen3 parquet access is fundamentally different from gen2.  In gen2, a {{butler.get}}  call on a parquet dataset call returned a lazy {{ParquetTable}} object that doesn't load any data until requested by {{.getDataFrame}} at which point specific columns can be requested.  However, in gen3, a {{butler.get}} call returns data directly, and specific columns are requested at this time.      The {{Functor}} implementation currently in {{pipe_tasks}} is based on the gen2 {{ParquetTable}} object, as it takes one of those as the input to its {{__call__}} method.  Updating for gen3 butler will need to change this.      A related concern is that one of the reasons for the gen2 {{ParquetTable}} implementation to be the way it was was to not have to read parquet metadata with every data load.  Part of this ticket should be to explore the degree to which this metadata loading affects practical use cases, and if anything needs to be changed in the gen3 parquet-interaction layer."""
comments: "A fundamental difference between gen2 and gen3 is that gen2 is assuming the file is local but in gen3 it might be in an S3 object store and by the time you get the data from it the file itself has been deleted locally.  There *might* be local caching of that file in a future datastore implementation but the caller code can't assume that will be there." [SEP] "I think for [~<USER>'s purposes, he can assume that we will _at least_ cache full parquet files on local disk after we fetch them from S3 - someday.  We're obviously not doing that yet, and I hope that's okay because these use cases aren't S3 yet, but it's obviously doable (in the sense that it's a use case we considered when developing the architecture), and there's just no way we'll be able to make things performant in S3 without it.    And I could see us having to go further - I know even on local (or at least shared FS) use [~<USER> has to rewrite our ""normal"" parquet files to do better partitioning, and I think it's conceivable that we might want

"""Rejected naming is a bit misleading, particularly in M1M3 SS C++ code. The code uses rejected*Events to store pre-clipped values, values after clipping are stored into applied*Events (where * stands for component name, e.g. elevation, accelertion, activeoptics,..). rejected*EVents are send only if rejection shall occur (e.g. values will be clipped), but the rejected might specify the component forces will not be used at all. Values from the Applied*Events are used.    Renaming rejected*EVents into preclipped*Events will fix the disambiguaty, clearly signaling (also in C++ code, if rejection does not occurs) those are preclipped values. This will not break any high level requirements, as it does not specify naming of events. If rejection/clipping occured, that will be also signaled in force component state event."""

summary: "Reprocess and Compare RC2 with Increased Sky Correction bgModel2 Values"
description: """Current image processing runs the {{skyCorrection.py}} routines to produce an improved background map. This process, in brief, 1) adds back on the original {{calexpBackground}} to its {{calexp}} (essentially undoing any visit-level background subtraction); 2) bins all CCDs for a single visit into 8192x8192 pixel superpixels, fits an Akima spline to these clipped-mean values and subtracts this {{bgModel}}; 3) fits and subtracts a sky frame across the entire focal plane; and 4) performs an additional background model subtraction ({{bgModel2}}) as in 2), but using smaller superpixel sizes of 256x256 pixels.    In Aihara et al. 2019, the bin scale of this {{bgModel}} step is given as 1024x1024 pixels. Since then, this step has split into two: an initial large-scale subtraction of 8192x8192 pixels (step 2 above) and a second medium-scale subtraction of 256x256 pixels (step 4 above). This ticket explores the effects of increasing this medium-scale bin size to 1024x1024 pixels as indicated in the Aihara et al. 2019 paper.    All three RC2 tracts have been reprocessed with the following config modifications:  {code:python}  config.bgModel2.xSize=15.36  config.bgModel2.ySize=15.36  {code}    Beginning with w_2020_34/DM-26441 data, these processing steps have been run:  # skyCorrection.py  # coaddDriver.py  # multiBandDriver.py  # coaddAnalysis.py  # compareCoaddAnalysis.py    As a prior reference dataset, I'm again refering to the data processing in w_2020_34/DM-26441.    All outputs are available on lsst-devl at: {{/project/<USER>bgModel2/output}}, on the RSP at: {{/home/<USER>bgModel2}}, or at this web link: {{https://lsst.ncsa.illinois.edu/~<USER>project/bgModel2/output/}}.     PS: for context, two community posts on [sky subtraction|https://community.lsst.org/t/sky-subtraction/2415] and [HSC default sky correction|https://community.lsst.org/t/hsc-defaults-now-require-sky-correction/2631] may be useful."""

description: """We are going to want to ingest a lot of raw files so we should profile raw ingest to ensure there are no obvious slowdowns.    testdata_lsst has 450 files and I have a copy locally so first try with that.    Checksum calculation should be disabled (and possibly I should make that the default)."""
comments: "In my first test I unzipped all the .gz files and replaced them with fpacked files so that we do not get a slow down from gunzipping to read metadata.    With profiling enabled single-threaded ingest of the 450 files (from imsim, latiss, phosim, ts8, and ts3) the run time is about 20 seconds (remember checksum is disabled).    About 6  seconds of that is importing python code so the ingest phase is about 14 seconds (how long {{run}} takes).    Calculating all the ingest metadata takes nearly 10 seconds (much of that seems to be down to calculating AzEl/Airmass for the on sky data).  3 seconds of that is reading the YAML camera (for detector mapping) and nearly 4 seconds is spent in astropy coordinates.    Butler ingest only takes about 1 second.    ie metadata extraction accounts for only 7% of run time. If this holds generally and if we intend to reingest multiple times, it seems like by far the best approach is to have a mode where we can read pre-calculated ObservationInfo from a separate file. This would

summary: "Catch sources with negative flux in all bands at their center"
description: """Sometimes the detection algorithm flags sources that have no flux in their center, which according to <USER>can occur when temporary background subtraction and smoothing is used during peak detection. This happens in ~0.1% of blends (not 0.1% of total sources) and is something that we need to catch during initialization to prevent scarlet from crashing.    Since this usually happens with only a single source in a blend, this ticket is to skip the source with no positive flux in its center and deblend the rest of the blend."""
comments: "I should note that this issue has been corrected upstream, this ticket is just created to test that DM-26603 correctly fixes the problem." [SEP] "DM-26603 did not fix this issue, so it still needs to be addressed."

In the case of release builds...well, they're sequential, so maybe we synthesize a release with r20 defined to be released June 1 2020 and six months backwards and forwards from there? summary: "Add focal-plane outlier rejection and focal-plane residual fits to fgcm"
description: """Follow-up on some oddities from per-visit differences from run to run with {{fgcmcal}} on RC2 (thank you [~<USER>!) revealed that there were some problems with outlier rejection in the {{fgcm}} code.  Initial investigation led me to realize I could add some additional """"full focal plane"""" outlier rejection on a per-visit basis that would be very robust, which is implemented on this ticket, and that is certainly helpful.  This then led me to the realization that the final """"gray"""" zeropoint correction could be done much more robustly per visit rather than per ccd.  Finally, some additional oddities in some visits led me to find a bug in {{fgcm}} such that bad reference stars were not always masked out, and could therefore contaminate certain post-fit measurements.    Tests on the {{w_36}} version of RC2 from the changes on this ticket yield significant improvements in the internal repeatability metrics within {{fgcm}}, reducing the bright source repeatability from 5-6 mmag to 3-4 mmag. """
comments: "I've had a look at all 3 PRs and left comments.  Obviously, I didn't go too deep into the {{fcgm}} changes, but I did have a gander and there were a few items that puzzled me, so I left comments in case they are useful.  I've gone ahead and approved all 3 PRs, but it would be nice to have some more validation of this updated behavior (e.g. some plots to compare) before merging." [SEP] "More validation is a good request!    First of all, there is the internal fgcm metrics on repeatability.  As mentioned in the summary, these fixes improve the bright source repeatability from 5-6 mmag to 3-4 mmag per band.  Here is the final i-band histogram for the baseline run:   !fgcmFitCycleRC2_cycle04_sigfgcm_reserved-stars_crunched_i.png|thumbnail!   And here it is for this ticket.  Notice that the histograms are scaled differently:   !fgcmFitCycleRC2-subccd2_cycle04_sigfgcm_reserved-stars_crunched_i.png|thumbnail! summary: "Modify BPS configuration class to allow initialization from a dictionary"
description: """A BPS configuration class can already be initialized with a dictionary. However, this dictionary was implemented to not have the actual config values themselves, but rather provide information about how to construct the config, e.g., location of the config file, a flag whether to get default site values from a database, etc. The goal is to modify the initialization part of the class to allow one to pass in a dictionary of values. It will make testing easier and is a more natural use of passing in a single dictionary.  The """"how to construct the config"""" capability will be removed for now and added later if we ever need it. """

summary: "Update documenteer's doxygen configuration to encompass base configs"
description: """In DM-22698, DM-23094, and DM-22461 we incorporated Doxygen builds for Pipelines docs directly into Documenteer, migrating away from our dependency on sconsUtils and lsstDoxygen. This included consolidating the Doxygen configuration, while still allowing individual packages a limited vocabulary of Doxygen configuration tags to include or exclude specific paths from the documentation.    We've discovered, though, that the {{base}} package actually plays a special role in providing configuration defaults beyond those coded into sconsUtils.    Thus in this ticket we will port base's configurations into documenteer and expand documenteer's Doxygen configuration facility to handle these new tags."""
comments: "1. Port the `base.inc`, `pex_config.inc` and `pex_exceptions.inc` files from their respective packages into a Doxygen default configuration that's included with Documenteer and included in the Doxygen config via `@INCLUDE_PATH` and `@INCLUDE` Doxygen configuration tags.    2. Embed the Doxygen tag file for cppreference.com, which was previously stored in the base package as well.    3. Add `IMAGE_PATH` Doxygen configuration and make it something that can be set from individual packages.    4. Include `src/` directories and `.cc` files in Doxygen inputs as that matches existing usage.    5. Add Doxygen logging configurations to DoxygenConfiguration to enable future dynamic settings.    The net effect is that the Doxygen builds controlled by Documenteer now match the functionality of `sconsUtils`, `lsstDoxygen`, etc. in terms of fully reproducing the `lsst` namespace."

summary: "Forward both abstract and physical filter names to SQuaSH"
description: """The tooling we wrote in DM-25507 only sent on the abstract filter in the {{filter_name}} metadata field.  SQuaSH treats this field as special and assigns the value in that field to the {{filter}} tag which is used by dashboards to filter results.  Existing dashboards assume that tag has the name of the physical filter.  In order to be consistent with that assumption, change the value assigned to the {{filter_name}} field to be the best guess at the physical filter.  It's useful to still send on the abstract filter name since it doesn't cost much."""
comments: "This was reviewed by Keith on the [PR|https://github.com/lsst-dmsst/metric-pipeline-tasks/pull/27]."

summary: "Adapt the Latest File Format of Corner WFS"
description: """Adapt the latest file format of corner WFS. This task should update the *phosim_utils* to repackage the amplifier images by *PhoSim* to mimic the output by camera control system (CCS). The example files can be found in NCSA. The path is {{/project/shared/BOT}}. The {{raw}} data is under the {{_parent}} directory. For example:  !pathOfRawData.png|width=359,height=52,thumbnail!    Notice the above files are the symbolic links (created by the ingestion of {{obs_lsst}}). The file names by CCS are:   !fileNameCCS.png|width=359,height=115,thumbnail!    The image name consists of 4 parts (e.g. *MC_C_20200906_000371*), where *MC*=Main Camera, *C*=CCS (who is in control, CCS or OCS), *20200906* = obsdate, *000371* = sequence number within obsdate. The exposure time is not in the file name, but should be in the primary header(s).    For the difference between *SW0* and *SW1*, need to follow the latest document: {{LCA-13381}}.    In this task, we need to make sure the repackaged images can be ingested by *obs_lsst* and assembled to be two CCDs with the correct geometry compared with the *eimage* in *PhoSim*."""
comments: "I tested to update the policy files of phosim in *obs_lsst*. However, it looks like the setting files will have some update in the generation 3 of data butler. In addition, it looks like DM will do the ingestion of images for us. This means we will use the butler by DM team directly. Therefore, I began to think to update the *ts_wep* instead.    I still think it is better to update the PhoSim mapper:   [https://github.com/teweitsai/obs_lsst/tree/tickets/DM-26836]    We may still need to ingest the corner WFS on the summit instead of going through DM team. Therefore, it should be good that we could mimic the CCS output by PhoSim."

summary: "Allow options-file option to expand environment variables"
description: """The {{-@}} option reads the values from the YAML file and makes those values the new default. This is mostly fine except for cases where we are specifying a file path using an environment variable. This happens a lot in pipetask with command lines such as:        being required.  The YAML file must therefore support environment variable expansion (using {{os.path.expandvars}} since the shell will be bypassed. We have some options:    * Add a callback for specific click options that will call expandvars. This will have to support callback chaining.  * Allow the """"business logic"""" code that accepts the value to do the expansion.  * Add a new option to options_file_option to declare all the values which should be expanded.  * Always call expandvars on every value read from the YAML file.    The first two options run the risk of double expansion if the option was given on the command line and then was expanded but resulted in a new value that also had an environment variable in it. This is probably okay since expandvars only expands variables if the variable exists.    The third option is defined to only run once but requires that we have to explicitly list the options that might use expansion. It may well be the safest option though.  """

summary: "Improve DDS read loop to reduce the danger of filling the DDS read queue"
description: """Read DDS messages in a background thread loop in order to reduce the danger that the read queue will overflow."""
comments: "This is a fairly simple change, though it does make shutdown slightly more complicated. SalInfo now needs an exit handler to make sure the background thread terminates, because ThreadPoolExecutor does not support daemon threads.    I also changed ReadTopic.get so that the default value for flush is False and specifying the argument issues a DeprecationWarning. This is a change in behavior but seems better than the path I had been taking. I know some current code assumes that the queue is not flushed, so although this may break a bit of code I think it equally likely to make other code work more reliably.    Pull request: https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_salobj/pull/147" [SEP] "Reviewed on github" [SEP] "Merged to develop and tagged v6.0.0.rc.1"

summary: "Extend PTC code to use ""proxy flux"" method"
description: """Extend DM-26784 to use [~<USER> """"proxy flux"""" method to deal with (I think?) gaps in photodiode data, or maybe this is to be able to provide corrections when there's no photodiode data at all? Not quite sure, Chris and I can work it out when it's time to do this one.    Also this ticket should work out how to configure the interplay between config options for this and using the photodiode data."""
comments: "Here's an actual description of the proxy flux.  We have two measurements for the linearity, an independent variable (a photodiode charge or an exposure time, called {{X}} below), and a dependent variable (the measured flux, called {{F}} below).   We know/strongly suspect that {{F}} isn't linear in {{X}}, such that {{F = g0 + g1*X + g2*X^2 + ...}}.  When {{X}} is small, we're in a linear regime, so we can ignore {{g2}} and higher order terms.  The DM-26845 linearity code does this by filtering {{F}}, using two config options, such that only values {{minLinearAdu < F < maxLinearAdu}} are retained.  {{minLinearAdu}} should likely be set to zero, but due to the use of neutral density filters, a non-zero value may be necessary (possibly only when {{X}} is the exposure time).    The filtered values, {{f}} and {{x}}, are then fit with a linear fit {{f = h0 + h1*x}}.  With this, the full set of {{X}} can be converted into the proxy flux {{L}}, by calculating {{L = h0 + h1 * X}}.  In this transformed

summary: "PipelineTask test utilities duplicate logic from ctrl_mpexec"
description: """I've discovered some test utility code in pipe_base that essentially duplicates logic in ctrl_mpexec's SingleQuantumExecutor, but doesn't quite do it completely - in particular, DatasetRefs are not resolved prior to calling runQuantum, as is done by {{SingleQuantumExecutor.updateQuantumInputs}}.    That behavior is becoming more important on DM-24432, so for now I'm just copying that method body to a new private function in pipe_base for the test code to use, creating this ticket, and leaving a TODO comment referencing it in pipe_base.    I think the right solution probably involves at least moving SingleQuantumExecutor to pipe_base, though moving the test utility code to ctrl_mpexec might be another option.  It's not clear to whether that would allow us to remove any of the test utility code, or just rewrite it in a way that delegates to SingleQuantumExecutor."""
comments: "Note that the point of the test code was to have the bare minimum needed for running a quantum, i.e. without a lot of the fancier processing {{SingleQuantumExecutor}} does. So I'd be more inclined to see if the test code can be simplified than if it can be replaced with {{SingleQuantumExecutor}}."

description: """pipetask can take many command-line parameters and it can be really annoying to copy and paste them all into a long command line. It would be really helpful if the options could instead be supplied as a configuration file but allow overrides on the command line. This is a common approach taken for complex command lines (for example HandbrakeCLI calls them """"presets"""").    The command line """"presets"""" could be a simple flat YAML file representing a dict where the keys are the long names of options (people could specify array values).    We should do this generically for butler, pipetask, and bps commands using the same infrastructure.  It would be preferably to do this early enough that all the same option validation could happen rather than having to validate in multiple places."""
comments: "Both CmdLineTask and ""old"" pipetask use argparse feature that reads command line options from a file if an argument starts with {{@}} character, the format of that file is just a plain text (https://docs.python.org/3/library/argparse.html#fromfile-prefix-chars). I do not know how widely this is used but it may be worth keeping syntax compatible." [SEP] "I'm completely failing to find a ""click"" equivalent to fromfile_prefix_chars." I'll assign the ticket to me so as not to distract [~<USER>." [SEP] "This seems to be working now. The main issue is the name for the command line option." description: """In the warp-*.fits, DATE-OBS, MJD values are gone (they were used to be wrong).  But they are important.  Do they live in Visitinfo object?"""
comments: "DATE-AVG is in the warp*fits headers, and the same value can be accessed via visitInfo.getDate(). This value is a string that contains the date and time, so is precise enough to recover the MJD.  However, this value is different from the MJD value in the original calexps for the same visit (""typical time"" versus average time of the exposure). Which of these values is wanted for the warp*fits headers or the corresponding butler visitInfo? Also, do we want to have the explicit MJD and DATE-OBS values in addition to the DATE-AVG string?" [SEP] "VisitInfo returns a daf_base DateTime object that can be queried for the MJD.    We generally frown against using FITS headers directly because people start looking for instrument specific ones.    It is meant to be the case though that the output file of a calexp-like dataset should have all the headers that are in the input file (plus any LSST specific additions) so it's a bug that they aren't. See RFC-576."

summary: "Add and modify columns for deblender results"
description: """A few of the columns added to track scarlet processing are not prefixed with the """"deblend"""" keyword, so it isn't obvious downstream which plugin is responsible for generating these. Part of this ticket is to insert the """"deblend"""" prefix to all of those columns so that everything generated by `meas_extensions_scarlet` begins with the """"deblend"""" keyword.    There are also a few columns that should be added to track quantities and metrics useful for debugging/analyzing the scarlet results:    * deblend_nPeaks: currently """"deblend_nChild,"""" the field that keeps track of the number of children in a blend, is only updated after the blend has completed. However, this does not propagate the number of peaks in the footprint of a parent that failed or the number of detected peaks for a footprint (which may or may not have been deblended). So this field will propagate the number of peaks initially in the parent footprint without having to access the footprint data itself.  * deblend_logL: we have discovered that tracking the magnitude of logL can be a very useful way to diagnose a change in scarlet that results in unmodeled (or poorly modeled) flux. Storing this quantity will be a useful regression test of scarlet in difficult blends where the assumptions of the monotonicity operator break down and the model needs to be more flexible."""
comments: "The catalog should also fill the `deblend_peak_center_x` and `deblend_peak_center_y` columns for failed sources to prevent having to load the footprints to get statistics from the catalog." [SEP] "This was fixed as part of DM-26603"

description: """The MultiProFit Parameter class has some inefficiencies like entirely avoidable conditionals in the frequently called get_value and set_value functions; optimizing them could improve performance significantly (particularly for small footprints/galaxies where setup time is comparable or greater than actual model evaluation time)."""
comments: "The main problem was that I had a single Parameter class that supported transformed values with a boolean parameter in the get_value and set_value functions, necessitating avoidable conditionals. I created a new ParameterTransformed class and added get/set_value_transformed functions. This seems to have improved runtime by 10-20% in DC2 and COSMOS HSC, just because the get_value* functions get called so often and the overhead dominates over model evaluation time for a lot of very small footprints.    I'm not entirely happy with the solution and I'm sure it will need more refactoring in the future (particularly with regard to how it interacts with Limits), but I'm setting that aside for now. I suspect the best solution will be to move the relatively simple but frequently called classes and functions to C++ and Pybind them."

summary: "Migrating a configuration system of the Qserv partitioning tools to JSON"
description: """The current implementation of the partitioning tools (Git package [https://github.com/lsst/partition]) is based on a custom DSL. This language is loosely based on JSON, though it's not JSON. Neither it's YAML. The non-standard configuration system poses a number of problems with using the tools in the LSST (and beyond) production environment, such as:  * it's impossible to use standard tools and an libraries for making, editing and validating configurations  * a manual process of making the configurations is error-prone, and has already caused problems with preparing various pre-production catalogs for ingesting into Qserv.    Another problem is that the configuration system allows overriding nearly all configuration file's parameters in the command line. Though such flexibility may be handy for some limited debugging or quick testing scenario, it's highly unsafe in production. In any production scenarios, all essential parameters affecting data transformations must be captured in a source code system for bookkeeping, monitoring and data provenance purposes. These tasks are better addressed if the configuration system is _orthogonal_.    Hence, a goal of this effort is to migrate the configuration system to JSON. This would open a possibility to rely on numerous standard tools for making, editing and validating configurations.    Here is an example of the JSON-based configuration file:  {code:yaml}  {""""mr"""":{    """"num-workers"""":12, """"block-size"""":128, """"pool-size"""":32768   },   """"part"""":{    """"num-stripes"""":340, """"num-sub-stripes"""":3,    """"chunk"""":""""chunkId"""", """"sub-chunk"""":""""subChunkId"""",    """"pos_ra"""":""""ra"""", """"pos_dec"""":""""dec"""",    """"overlap"""":0.01667   },   """"in"""":{    """"null"""":""""\\N"""", """"delimiter"""":""""\t"""", """"escape"""":""""\\"""",    """"field"""":[     """"designation"""",     """"ra"""",     """"dec"""",     """"cntr"""",     """"source_id""""    ]   },   """"out"""": {    """"null"""":""""\\N"""", """"delimiter"""":"""","""", """"escape"""":""""\\"""", """"no-quote"""":false   }  }  {code}"""
comments: "Overall it seems fine. I've made a few suggestions, you can decide what to do with those."

"""The AST library now supports serializing of WCS framesets into YAML format matching the ASDF standard. We need to switch our WCS I/O code so that it no longer writes the WCS in AST Native form but uses ASDF.    Using ASDF has the advantage that other tooling (such as astropy) can understand our WCS whereas at the moment we require they use AST.    We should use a serialization in FITS that matches that used by ASDF for storing WCS in FITS files, rather than using a form that is not directly compatible."""

summary: "Change butler gen3 registry to band rather than abstract filter"
description: """In recent discussions it was suggested that something like """"band"""", """"waveband"""" (or """"energyband"""" -- following CAOM formalism, although that is """"optical"""" vs """"radio"""") could be less confusing than """"abstract filter"""". It was clear that people weren't entirely sure what """"abstract"""" means and we get some people thinking it means a generic filter name such as """"all red filters are called r""""  and others who think it's nothing more than a label (so """"r"""" and """"r2"""" and """"r3"""" are all abstract).  Gen3 is trying to use abstract filter to mean """"you can probably co-add these and get a useful answer that you expect"""".    Consider also renaming physical_filter to just filter if we have band. Physical filter always refers to a specific piece of hardware.    If we wish to make this change to registry and butler then we should change it before we declare schema stability.    This ticket can be marked invalid if we decide against it but I need a placeholder."""
comments: "FWIW, I'd recommend against having {{band}} and {{filter}} coexisting, since to people who *aren't* observers or photometry experts the distinction may be just as confusing as {{abstract_filter}} vs. {{physical_filter}}. Given that (per the Slack discussion) we shouldn't be encouraging the use of {{physical_filter}}, I think letting it keep a long name may actually be a good thing." [SEP] "There are other options: abstract_band?  energy_band? waveband?    I got the impression that using filter for both abstract and physical was part of the problem with the discussion." [SEP] "Yes, I don't see problems with the combination ({{band}}, {{physical_filter}}). Though perhaps others will!" [SEP] "My (slight) preference is for (band, physical_filter), too, of the options presented so far." [SEP] "I am 100% on board with (band, physical_filter).  Yes, ""physical_filter"" is verbose, but (a) as mentioned it is not supposed to be used regularly, and makes you think about what it is that you're referring to, and (b) ban

summary: "Troubleshooting Zookeeper on the Base EFD cluster"
description: """Since Friday Sep, 11 we started seeing session timeouts in Zookeeper and connection errors from the kafka brokers to Zookeeper. That required a few restarts of Kafka over the weekend before we could investigate the issue.    The relevant message from the Zookeeper logs seems to be:        which is present even after a restart.    My interpretation is that this fsync operation might exceed the Zookeeper session timeout. The session timeout is controlled by        which is 2000ms by default and might be causing the timeouts and connection errors we were seeing. I've set this parameter to 8000ms that is used to set the minSessionTimeout and maxSessionTimeout        and looking at the client, we see that the actual negotiated timeout is now 18000 ms:        I'll keep monitoring the cluster, but the initial WARNING message is gone after we applied the new configuration.      Reference:    https://zookeeper.apache.org/doc/r3.3.2/zookeeperAdmin.html#sc_configuration    tickTime: the length of a single tick, which is the basic time unit used by ZooKeeper, as measured in milliseconds. It is used to regulate heartbeats, and timeouts. For example, the minimum session timeout will be two ticks."""
comments: "With the help of [~<USER> we concluded that the problem with the Base EFD was poor I/O due to the {{rbd}} devices installed on the Kueyen nodes.        Josh created a 300G partition for Kafka on the {{nvme}} device on {{/var/lib/local}}. I deployed the ""local-path-provisioner"" and redeployed Kafka.  The retention policies based on size and time are in place to account for the smaller partition.    [~<USER> I think with that we are good to continue the Comcam tests next week. " [SEP] "Control center is available here: https://control-center-base-efd.lsst.codes/clusters"

"""The tagging and versioning used by `setuptools_scm` in scarlet is incompatible with non-version tags like the ones used in the stack (eg. weekly tags). There is also an incompatibility since the stack tags `vX.Y.Z` use the same `v` prefix as scarlet now uses (due to the requirement of `setuptools_scm`).    This ticket aims to solve both problems by  1. Converting scarlet version tags to the more common `X.Y.Z` format (without the `v` prefix)  2. Natively creating the `_version.py` file and removing `setuptools_scm` completely from scarlet."""

This ticket is to quantify the conditions under which the current matcher+solver succeed or fail.  This quantification may be expressed in raw offsets (e.g., in the corners of the CCDs) or in terms of boresight, rotation, distortion (including non-TAN projections).
