summary: "Add support for the secondary index in sph-partition"
description: """h1. 1. Objectives    A goal of this effort is to add support for the _secondary index_ when partitioning data of the _child_ (_dependent_) tables. Here is the extended partitioning """"protocol"""":   # the partitioning is done based on either *{{RA}}*/*{{DEC}}* of a table, or a precomputed map *{{id -> (chunk,sub-chunk)}}*   # the *{{RA}}*/*{{DEC}}* mode is triggered by a presence of the following config or command line parameter *{{part.pos=<ra_column_name>,<dec_column_name>}}*   # to trigger an alternative partitioning method one would have to eliminate *{{part.pos}}* from the configuration and provide *{{part.id=<fk-to-director-table>}}*, where the """"foreign"""" key specified in the last parameter would be is the name of the corresponding column in the child table.   #* the partitioning of a dependent table will fail if an object identifier won't be found in the map   # if both *{{part.pos}}* and *{{part.id}}* are present then the former takes precedence over the later   # the *{{id -> (chunk,sub-chunk)}}* map gets created gets created if both *{{part.pos}}* and *{{part.id}}* are present. The map is stored in a file named *{{index.txt}}* at the same output destination where other partitioning products (chunks, overlaps) are stored.   # the *{{id -> (chunk,sub-chunk)}}* map files produced by the partitioner will be the {{CSV}}-formatted files following an output _dialect_ specified in a collection of parameters *{{part.out_csv.}}**  #* if the file already exist it will be open in the append mode, so that new object entries will be added to the file.   # if only *{{part.id}}* is present (indirect partitioning) then parameter *{{part.id-url=file:<path>/chunk_object_index.txt}}* is required by the partitioner   ** the partitioning of a dependent table will fail if no valid map will be provided   # the overlap tables are generated ONLY in the *{{RA}}*/*{{DEC}}* partitioning mode AND if *{{part.overlap != 0}}*   # the *{{id -> (chunk,sub-chunk)}}* map would *NEVER* be generated for the entries put into the overlap tables   #* that won't be needed because the _child_ (_dependent_) tables do not have overlaps in Qserv.    Other improvements:  * add partitioning option *{{part.disable-chunks={0|1}}}* to disable writing output chunked products. This mode would be used in the _dry run_ mode for evaluating input files, or for generating the _secondary_ index files for object tables which have been partitioned/ingested.    h1. 2. Design considerations and Requirements    In the initial (simplest) file-based implementation, the index files will be the regular *{{CSV}}* text files. Each line of the files will have the following format (assuming the _comma_-separated output formatting):    Where:   * there will be exactly one entry for each object encountered in the _director_ table's data file(s)   * values in each row of the file will be separated by a separator specified in the corresponding configuration parameters    h2. 2.1 Other notes/requirements   * consider using abstract interfaces for fetching data from the output index.   * consider implementing plugins for the abstract interfaces. Each such plugin would be mapped to the corresponding implementation of the persistent (or service-based) secondary index (examples: file-based, MySQL-based, REST API-based, Redis-based, etc.)"""
comments: "Couple of comments to description of this ticket before I look at the code:    {quote}  4. if both part.pos and part.id are present then the former takes precedence over the later  5. the id -> (chunk,sub-chunk) map gets created gets created if both part.pos and part.id are present.  {quote}  Is this not a contradiction?    {{part.id-url=file:<path>/chunk_object_index.txt}} - I think it's better to use configuration names that look like identifiers, e.g. {{id_url}} instead of {{id-url}}, in some dynamic languages these names can get translated into object attributes (depends on library used of course).    " [SEP] "Sort of OK, but see my comments on PR."

"""[kafdrop | https://github.com/obsidiandynamics/kafdrop] is an open-source web UI for kafka and can replace some of the functionalities of Control Center.  """
comments: "Some of the [kafdrop|https://github.com/obsidiandynamics/kafdrop] features:    * View Kafka brokers — topic and partition assignments, and controller status  * View topics — partition count, replication status, and custom configuration  * Browse messages — JSON, plain text and Avro encoding  * View consumer groups — per-partition parked offsets, combined and per-partition lag  * Create new topics    it has integration with the Schema Registry to deserialize Avro messages when displaying them.  I miss a couple of features compared to Control Center among them lack of support to Kafka Connect, ability to edit broker level or topic level properties like add partitions and change retention policy. Also the UI does not refresh you have to reload the app to update the data. But it seems a strong code-base and I would recommend it as a Kafka UI." [SEP] "[PR #17|https://github.com/lsst-sqre/kafka-aggregator/pull/17] adds Kafdrop to the docker-compose setup for evaluation."

summary: "Deploy de kafka-aggregator on the Sandox EFD environment"
description: """We'll deploy the {{kafka-aggregator}} on the Sandbox EFD and evaluate its performance and scalability."""
comments: "Add ArgoCD app to deploy the {{kafka-aggregator}} on the sandbox environment." [SEP] " I left the {{kafka-aggregator}} running overnight processing messages for 100 topics at 10Hz with only one Faust worker running 100 agents and producing aggregated messages at 1Hz. It worked just great, the one worker was able to keep up with the stream. This result was very encouraging. Then in the morning I increased the frequency of the producer to 50Hz. At this regime we start to see the consumers falling behind.  In these tests the source topics were configured with 4 partitions each which means we have 4 Faust consumers consuming messages for each topic, the solution is to increase the number of partitions and run more workers to improve the throughput. I think this is a great first result.  " [SEP] "We see spikes on the throughput graphs and I think I have an explanation for that, it just means the aggregator is idle waiting the window to fill up and processing before producing the aggregated messages - it doesn't

Here are some examples of channels with topics and descriptions that reference relevant Community Forum categories.    *#dm-jointcal topic:* Discussion of the development of Jointcal, which does astrometric and photometric fitting across multiple visits of the same field. | Need tech support? Try here: [https://community.lsst.org/c/support]    *#dm-lsp topic and description:* Support for the LSST Science Platform. Try the forum: [https://community.lsst.org/c/support/lsp]     *#dm-sst description:* Discussions of the DM System Science Team. Looking for user support? Try here: [https://community.lsst.org/c/sci/data]    *#dm-sst topic:* Discussions of the DM System Science Team.  Confluence, including meeting minutes: [http://ls.st/sst]  BJ: [https://bluejeans.com/103664856]  Looking for user support? summary: "Fix the Following Error in the Rotator Simulink Model"
description: """Fix the following error in the rotator Simulink model. There is the problem of physical model in plant that the rotator's position will differ from the related position command gradually until the trigger of *followingError* (the difference >= threshold) and this stops the motion of rotator. According to the observation, this happens if the rotator moves in the negative direction.    In the following figure, there are two targets: (1) position=1.2 deg and velocity=0.01 deg/sec and (2) position=1.5 deg and velocity=-0.01 deg/sec. The total simulation time is 20.1 sec. There is no big difference between the position command and position. The error of *noNewTrackCmdError* after the slew of the second target is triggered as expected.   !trackAdapNoNewTrackCmd.png|thumbnail!    However, if the second target has position=-1.5 deg and velocity=-0.01 deg/sec, the following error is triggered while the rotator turns the direction to slew to the second target:   !trackSolverAdap.png|thumbnail!"""
comments: "[~<USER> found there is the problem of torque in the plant/load dynamics block. He put the scopes in the torque input, *Gravity Compensation*, and *Friction/Damping Compensation*, and noticed that the sum of both Compensations is similar to the maximum torque, and there is no torque left to get a motion started (marked as *TTorque*, see the following figure:    !neg_slew_failed.jpg|thumbnail!    To fix it, he inserted a gain of 0.5 to the *Coulomb Friction* output and the system seems to work fine.     He has no idea what the friction value should be, maybe this is something we need to bring up with MOOG, as the sum of both compensations exceeds the available torque in this particular situation. He contacted Ghavimi, Ali to see he could help this or not.    I asked the following questions and [~<USER> replied:    1. What will happen for the positive point-to-point movement (positionSet 1.5)? It looks like this gain value will affect all control modes globally.    {color:#FF0000}This change should have

summary: "Given the aggregation window size and frequency make sure the aggregator has enough data points to compute statistics"
description: """Example using a window size of 1s and frequency of 1Hz:    """
comments: "[PR #16|https://github.com/lsst-sqre/kafka-aggregator/pull/16] adds the minimum sample size configuration ({{config.min_sample_size}}) to compute statistics.    Given the size of the window and the frequency of incoming messages, the {{config.min_sample_size}} configuration parameter sets the minimum sample size to compute statistics. The Faust tumbling window will always contain at least one message. If the number of messages in the tumbling window is smaller than config.min_sample_size the values of the first message are used instead."

comments: "While [~<USER> and I were running new data through the fakes insertion code we found a few small changes that would improve the code.    1) Add a log message saying which patch, tract and filter the code is currently working on.    2) The code currently expects a string as the value in sourceType. While I originally thought this preferable, as it means you don't have to remember what value means star or galaxy or anything else, some issues have since come up. Depending on what people use to write their fakes input files sometimes the sourceType columns are being written as bytes and sometimes as strings. To deal with this it would probably be better to move to numbers for this column, I am proposing 0 for stars and 1 for galaxies. This needs to be valid for all previously made files as well so it needs to check for strings, bytes and numbers.    3) The fakes code uses galsim to make the fake galaxies. Galsim has restrictions on the range of sersic indices that it can make profiles for. The code has a functio

summary: "make qgraph and run subcommands for pipetask"
description: """Implement {{qgraph}} and {{run}} using click (in the {{pipetask2}} command, which will eventually replace {{pipetask)}}    Add the """"chained"""" subcommands feature, write a Community post about it describing the differences from the existing {{pipetask}} command, request feedback."""
comments: "I ran into a problem chaining the run subcommand after qgraph because it wants to use butler options passed into the qgraph command to create a new butler. I didn't want to make the user enter butler options & values twice (once for qgraph, again with same values for run). I could have made qgraph create the butler and add it to the objects passed to run as a chained command, but there was one option that wasn't used by qgraph (it may have been output, or output-run? I forget, but can look it up tomorrow), and I didn't really want to pass an option to qgraph for a future subcommand that qgraph wasn't using itself. It occurred to me that it might be reasonable for subcommands to ""forward"" their option values to next subcommands in the same command execution (that is, chained subcommands). I added the ""option forwarding"" feature so we can evaluate it and change or remove it as needed. There's documentation about it in the code and in the qgraph and run subcommand help menus. summary: "Set rel=canonical HTTP header on LSST the Docs"
description: """By setting a canonical URL for pages delivered by LSST the Docs, we can ensure that search engines consolidate rankings for the same page on different editions (served by different {{/v}} prefixes.    See https://support.google.com/webmasters/answer/139066?hl=en    The best way to do that is at the CDN level, in Fastly, to set HTTP headers.    Behavior notes:   * The canonical URL should always correspond to the main edition. So strip the {{/v/}} and {{/builds/}} prefixes.   * URLs for directories should end with {{/}} and ideally strip off the {{index.html}}. So it'll be {{https://pipelines.lsst.io/}} rather than {{https://pipelines.lsst.io/index.html}}. This approach is also compatible with how Gatsby apps work. It'll also be compatible with Sphinx's directory-based HTML builder (https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/builders/index.html#sphinx.builders.dirhtml.DirectoryHTMLBuilder)     """

summary: "REST API for building the secondary index in Qserv"
description: """In the current implementation of the new Ingest system the _secondary index_ of a database is normally populated in the end of the _super-transactions_. The algorithm harvests contributions from the corresponding MySQL _partitions_ of the director table's chunks and loads them into the index table at Qserv """"czar""""'s database . Depending on the amount of data ingested into that table during a transaction the index building operation may noticeably slow down the commit phase of the transaction. After gaining the first experience with using the new Ingest system it has become apparent that some workflows may benefit from having the _secondary index_ construction as an explicit stage deferred till a moment when all director table's contributions were loaded into Qserv.    Hence, a goal of the current development is to refine and extend the REST API of the Master Replication Controller to allow more options in building the index. Specifically, the following changes are proposed:  * add an optional parameter *build_secondary_index=<val>* to existing database creation service to indicate an intent to defer the index creation till the very last stage. The default value of the parameter will be 0 which would preserve the current behavior.  * extend the database information structure DatabaseInfo with the corresponding flag.  * modify the transaction commit service not to make contributions into the index if the deferred index construction for the database was requested.  * add a new POST service for constructing indexes as a separate operation to be performed before (or with extra controls) after publishing a database. The service should also allow an optional parameter to allow rebuilding the index from scratch.  * update the documentation on the Ingest System accordingly.  * evaluate if the version of the Ingest API needs to be incremented as well."""

description: """Update how matching is currently run so as to not run on patches with no data. """
comments: "When I initially implemented the code to skip patches without any data, I had to get a couple more things from the repository (the wcs and the bounding box of the source catalog).  Getting the extra data made the time to assemble the inputs 3 times longer.  This is significant because I had not profiled carefully enough.  It turns out that the data access is comparable to the matching time itself.  So making that 3x bigger now meant that the data access was dominating the run time of the matching task, so skipping patches didn’t actually matter any more.  If I make some assumptions, I can get the data access time to only be ~70% larger.  At that point, skipping patches is a win.  For one datapoint the same task went from 3m18.166s to 2m48.650s.  Not the factor of a couple I was hoping for, but it’s something.  Takeaway is that data access is not trivial and we will have to worry about it." description: """ts_salobj 6 (pre-release, at least) will interoperate with ts_salobj 5, but at the cost that the {{identity}} field in {{ackcmd}} topics is empty, instead of being a copy of the {{private_identity}} field of the command.    I am working around this by ignoring the {{identity}} field if it is empty. However, that makes checking that the {{ackcmd}} is really intended for a given {{SalInfo}} instance a bit less reliable because the {{origin}} field must match, but ts_salobj 6 no longer writes or reads the {{private_host}} field so it cannot check the echoed {{host}} field in the {{ackcmd}}.    Once we are using ts_salobj 6 and ts_sal 4.2 everywhere then:  * In {{SalInfo._ackcmd_callback}} remove the code that ignores an empty {{identity}} field.  * In {{ReadTopic}}'s constructor enable the query for {{private_identity}}"""

summary: "Please echo private_identity from commands as identity in ackcmd messages."
description: """I am implementing the authList support in salobj and realized that ackcmd replies from SALPY_Test do not have the {{identity}} field correctly set to the {{private_identity}} field from the command being acknowledged.    If practical it would be a help to have this fixed in SAL 4.2, because it will allow ts_salobj 6 to fully handle authorization and operate with SAL 4.2 (as well as SAL 5). It is likely to also benefit running a combination of SAL 5 and SAL 4.2 (which I strongly suspect will work), though without authorization in the SAL 4.2 CSCs.    All that said, a simple workaround will allow ts_salobj 6 to work without this, so it's not critical. DM-25474."""

Also please make sure {{lsstsal/bin}} is *not* added to $PATH. I find it on my $PATH when I run [~<USER>'s dev Docker image and there is no such directory.    I think this would be an improvement because:  * """"bin/"""" is where all other T&S and DM packages put executable scripts.  * {{lsstsal/scripts}} has 170 or so scripts, most of which are executable. If you prefer to leave {{lsstsal/scripts}} on the PATH then I would like to change that so both are added to PATH."""

description: """The default path template for raw images for obs_lsst instruments is {{raw/%(run)s/%(raftName)s/%(expId)013d-%(raftName)s-%(detectorName)s-det%(detector)03d.fits}}.  This is overridden for LATISS to be {{raw/%(dayObs)s/%(expId)013d-det%(detector)03d.fits}}.    Problems include the presence of {{run}}, which is not usually defined, and the single additional {{raftName}} level of directory hierarchy, which causes large numbers of images to end up in the same directory and related images to be in separate directories.  The LATISS template is better, but following that template for LSSTCam would still lead to hundreds of thousands of files in the same directory.    The action requested here is to come up with a better path that avoids having more than a few thousand entries at any level of the directory hierarchy.  Having it be conducive to subsetting/caching and replication would also be desirable.    I suggest that for ComCam and LSSTCam that both the {{dayObs}} and {{expId}} should be separate parts of the directory hierarchy, in that order.  With that division, {{raftName}} likely does not need to be part of the path.  Gen3 places {{filter}} in the path, but that may make it slightly more difficult to usefully subset the repo for caching purposes, so I would normally avoid that if it weren't for future compatibility."""
comments: "Don't fell like gen3 is constraining you at all here. We can choose a sensible approach for directories. We do require that all possible dimensions end up in the filename itself.  In theory we can have a special raw template in gen3 as well." [SEP] "I propose we should simply solve this for LSSTCam and then apply the same to ComCam.   For LATISS I think it makes sense to leave as is since having a bucket per night fits relatively well.   That said, for LSSTCam the way that leaves the data in a sensible state would be to have the data from a single 24 hour of observing share a common top level directory with a sub-directory for each exposure (i.e. 201 files).  While this is a little wasteful for ComCam... (i.e., each directory containing only 9 files) it at least keeps individual raw sets together.  Any other thoughts?  am I missing something?"

description: """Currently we define components for StorageClasses to be the components that a dataset can be disassembled into or reassembled from.  This is fine for things like masks and wcs but gen2 supports read-only components such as BBox that allow you to efficiently query information about a specific dataset without having to fetch the entire dataset.    Getting this to work is fairly straight forward if there is no disassembly. If we do disassemble then I need to think about how we forward the BBox request to the correct component."""
comments: "I have something simple that works now for Exposure that is not disassembled. I list readComponents separately, register those dataset types and datastore naively passes through the component request to the formatter.    For disassembled datasets things are a bit trickier since the only portable solution would be to pass the component name and a dict mapping disassembled components to their own formatters to the composite Formatter and have that composite formatter work out which components it needs to work out filter or bbox etc. The bit that is a problem is that currently datastore doesn't know what the composite Formatter is because it never bothers to store it (since it disassembled things) -- I think this means that my only option is to always add a row for the composite to posix_datastore_records with a blank filename (or I guess write such a row if the storage class defines read components -- but it probably doesn't hurt to always write).     I don't even know if Image has a fast equivalent of FitsExp

summary: "Insert Fake Sources Provided by the LSST:UK LSB Working Group"
description: """The Rubin Obs DM team are actively collaborating with the LSST:UK LSB working group to develop LSB sky subtraction and source fidelity metrics. This ticket injects several fake source catalogues produced by the LSST:UK LSB WG into the stack, and analyses their outputs.     Resources:   * [Insert Fakes LSST Community link|https://community.lsst.org/t/new-tasks-for-fake-source-insertion/3722]  * [Insert Fakes source code|https://github.com/lsst/pipe_tasks/blob/master/python/lsst/pipe/tasks/insertFakes.py]    A number of input catalogues were provided, namely: {{model_faceon.fits}}, {{model_edgeon.fits}}, {{model_icl.fits}} and {{model_fornax_tab.fits}}. On closer analysis, a number of issues were discovered with these data, necessitating some modifications. Specifically, the requested pointings did not fall within tract 9813, one of our RC2 contuinally processed regions (see the tract figure attached below). To allow us to use these input data, we repositioned each object to move it into the centre of tract 9813. In addition, RA/Dec values were translated into radians, the column {{sourceType}} was added indicating that these sources are {{galaxy}} type, and Sersic indices were clipped to the range 0.3 < n < 6.2.     The modified Fornax dataset was processed using {{insertFakes}}, and then processed with detection turned on using {{multiBandDriver}}. To produce a final output object table, we finished processing using {{writeObjectTable.py}}, {{transformObjectCatalog.py}} and {{consolidateObjectTable.py}}. A trimmed version of this object table is attached to this ticket below.     All working is shown in the attached Jupyter Notebook, available in {{.ipynb}} or {{.pdf}} format below. We also include two example images below showcasing the insertion of a typical Sersic source in all five bands: one prior to final sky subtraction ({{deepCoadd}}) and one after final sky subtraction ({{deepCoadd_calexp}}).     All outputs are available via the LSP in this directory: {{/home/<USER>fakes_lsstuk/outputs/fornaxmod}}"""
comments: "From Aaron Watkins, co-chair of the LSB WG C1 Group:  ""Hi again.  Incoming some tables of model galaxies for injection.  This includes a model Fornax cluster pushed out to something like z=0.1, as well as two things I just want to try out: a large edge-on disk with a thick disk (or at least my best approximation of a thick disk using Sersic profiles), and a smaller but still local Universe LSB face-on disk+bulge.  I used the HSC viewer tool to pick coordinates for these, so they should be in the COSMOS footprint, avoiding the brightest stars (for now; may put something beside a bright star later).""" [SEP] "These data have been processed. Unfortunately, the input format of the tables wasn't quite right. The RA/dec pointings placed them outside tract 9813, which makes continual processing a little tricky. In addition, we needed to modify or add a number of columns (details above). However, once those issues were resolved, we were able to push through the largest fakes catalogue (for Fornax) through the stack

summary: "Remove the History of Trajectory after the Stop Command in the Tracking"
description: """Remove the history of trajectory after issuing the *stop* command in the tracking. The Simulink model uses the *LsstCmdSet* object to get the time, position, and velocity of tracking target. If the user issues the *stop* command before the finish of slew, the rotator stops without the update of *LsstCmdSet*. This means if the user issues the *trackStart* command, the rotator will transition to the *SlewingAndTracking* sub-state and try to track the unfinished target based on *LsstCmdSet*.    In the following figure, there are two targets: (1) position=1.2 deg, velocity=0.01 deg/sec, and (2) position=3.0 deg, velocity=-0.01 deg/sec. The final *track* command is at time=6.05 second. Issue the *stop* command at time=7 second and *trackStart* command at time=9 second. We can see the rotator tries to finish the second target after it is back to the *SlewingAndTracking* sub-state.   !trackStopBeforeSlewDone.png|thumbnail!    To solve this problem, we need to update the *LsstCmdSet* object to set the position to be the rotator's position or position command, and the velocity to be 0 after the stop completes (*Flags_stopComplete* ==1.0) as the following:   !trackWithStopAnd3rdTargetOfPosNowAndRewiteCmdPosAndVel.png|thumbnail!    There are four possible options to do this:   (1) Update the *LsstCmdSet* object in *ts_rotator_controller* once *Flags_stopComplete*==1.0.   (2) Let *ts_rotator* update the *LsstCmdSet* object by sending the new *track* command once *Flags_stopComplete*==1.0. However, we prefer to use the command acknowledgement to know the *stop* command is finished or not in a latter time.   (3) Add a *reset* command or something similar in *ts_rotator* and *ts_rotator_controller* to let the rotator controller updates the position and velocity of *LsstCmdSet*.   (4) Update the Simulink model to add a latch to determine when to use the new *track* command. This will add the complexity to the Simulink model because it is not designed in that way originally. It may not be easy to debug in a latter time. However, this will not affect the *ts_rotator* and *ts_rotator_controller* at all.    I do not prefer the option 1 because there will be multiple places to write the data into the global variable of *LsstCmdSet*. Originally, it is updated by *ts_rotator* only. Or more accurately, the *ts_rotator* issues the *track* command to update the *LsstCmdSet* in *commanding.c* in *ts_rotator_controller*. If the option 1 is chosen, I need to let the *actuator.c* to be able to update the *LsstCmdSet* object. Think more deeply, for the option 3, it is the same thing. The only benefit for option 3 is that the CSC user knows the *LsstCmdSet* is changed. But if the CSC user does not care about this, maybe the option 1, 2, or 4 is better. If we consider the coherence of code, maybe the option 1 or 4 is the best solution."""
comments: "I decided to try the option 4 to hack the rotator Simulink model first.    There is a *reset* block in the *Command Generator Overview* subsystem, which contains the path generator. I tested the *memory* block and realized the value in hold will be reset if the *reset* block is triggered:  !testResetMemory.png|thumbnail!  Therefore, if I want to hold the value of *LsstCmdSet*, I could not do this in the *Command Generator Overview* subsystem." [SEP] "Add the 'switchPosAndVelCmd' subsystem in 'Command Profile' subsystem as a proof of concept. The 'sample and hold' block is used as a latch. In the following figure, the rotator tracks two targets: (1) position=1.2 deg, velocity=0.01 deg/sec, and (2) position=3.0 deg, velocity=-0.01 deg/sec. The final track command is at time=6.05 sec. I stop the rotator at time=7 second, and put back the *SlewingAndTracking* sub-state at time=9 second. The rotator stops at the second target, and put into the Fault state after no new track command error as expected.  !trackSto

summary: "Fix multiple writes into H&S FIFO in LabView Modbus library"
description: """LabView Modbus module (lsst-ts/Common_FPGA_Modbus) contains in RX subVi SCTL blocks where multiple calls into a singe FIFO are possible on timeouts. Those usually don't occurs, as the calls are after failed writes etc. which aren't expected. Still if they do happen, undertermined behaviour will follow - most probably FPGA will crash and communication with the M1M3 will be lost.         A prominent example is below. Update H&S is called to increase counter of received data. If write to RxDataFIFO timeout, update H&S is called to set flag 0x20. If stop bit (0x0200, 10th bit received) isn't true 0x10 is indicated among flags.         !image-2020-06-13-19-06-59-053.png!    It's hard to tell whatever a crash isn't a better solution to those unexpected problems. Hovewer, it will be much better to make it a controlled crash than un-controlled crash cased as side effect of multiple FIFO writes inside a single SCTL.    I hit the issue before while adding another code, realizing that LabView doesn't check writes inside subVis. It will not compile G code with direct (not hidden in subVi) multiple writes into a single FIFO in a single SCTL. LabView doc is full of warnings to not put multiple writes inside a single SCTL - see e.g. [https://forums.ni.com/t5/LabVIEW/FPGA-module-FIFO-arbitration-in-single-cycle-timed-loop/td-p/170803?profile.language=en.] The fact is also clear from how SCTL: works inside FPGA."""

summary: "Please retain the current behavior of private_origin and private_host in ts_sal 4.2"
description: """ts_sal 4.2 pre-release (develop commit 62503396) appears to change how private_origin is set. Please restore the existing behavior (in 4.1) until version 5.0, when we will switch to using private_identity for some of that.    Also for SAL 5.0 we will need some way of differentiating the same user running multiple processes (e.g. multiple Jupyter notebooks or logins) so command acknowledgements can be reliably disambiguated.    The question is whether the distinction between a user on one notebook or another is of interest to the authorization system. My guess is """"no"""" -- all logins are treated the same. In that case the additional information belongs in a separate field -- and I suggest using private_origin the way it is used now, since we already have it. summary: "The MTPtg must be able to send a configurable number of track commands in advance"
description: """Please check that the TMPtg component can be configured to send up to 1 second's worth of tracking commands in advance.    The main telescope motion controller requires that it can always interpolate between two position, velocity, time track target commands. If it cannot interpolate it will stop tracking and halt the axes. Python has somewhat variable performance, due to having a garbage collector, so we need plenty of margin in order to have reliable tracking.    My guess is we will want to run with an advance time of at least 1/2 second."""

summary: "Add support for defining schema version defined by daf_butler"
description: """AS discussed in DM-24803 we need a way to manage schema versions defined in daf_butler. This needs some additional decisions though:  - do we want one global schema version or per-component  - datastore seems to be special case  - dimensions is also very special  - how do we detect schema change  - etc."""
comments: "I have an initial implementation of defining and using versions, however there is a complication that I did not anticipate and I do not know yet how to work around.    The issue is with the initializing registry database. I want to store schema versions in the attributes table and I need to do it exactly once when database schema is initialized. I though that initialization is a controlled process, that is we initialize it once and only once. Registry constructor has a {{create}} argument which, I think, is used to trigger creation of the registry schema, naturally I though I'd use that same argument to trigger population of the attributes with versions. But I discovered that this assumption is not holding, in the unit tests I see cases where that logic breaks:  - many cases when registry is instantiated with {{create=False}} on an empty database, I think this works because sqlite database knows to always set that flag internally for in-memory database  - few cases when Registry is instantiated twice with {{

description: """For schema stability we need a place where we can store identifying information for schema itself and various configuration-related info as well. That will server as a sort of metadata for the rest of the schema and data in registry. To be usable from many releases it itself needs to be super-stable but we also should be be able to store all sorts of metadata in it, exact structure of that is not possible predict today. I think the most generic solution for that is a simple key-value store with arbitrary keys and values encoded as strings. All other structures can be mapped to key/value using reasonable rules, though some care is certainly needed to avoid name collision."""
comments: "I think it is ready for review. The interface is now simplest possible, we may need to extend it later with new methods if needed. What is more important is the schema of the metadata table, I chose reasonably wide string types for both keys and values, I hope this should cover all our needs for now. We do not store any metadata yet, will work on that in next tickets." [SEP] "Switching back In Progress, need to rename the whole thing." [SEP] "Ready for review once again, no traces of metadata left."

description: """The existing Dome {{inPosition}} event has several serious problems:  * It has no boolean flag indicating whether or not the specified device is in position. We need to know """"is in position"""" and """"is not in position"""".  * It has a {{deviceId}} attribute, which badly breaks salobj's ability to automatically output new events only when data has changed. The most recent output is likely to be for a different device! Thus we will get far too many of these events, even when nothing has changed.  * The {{devicePosition}} field is not not useful (especially if you implement DM-25333) and is inadequate for the azimuth (without an associated velocity and time).    I suggest:  * A different inPosition event for each device/axis, e.g.:  * azimuthInPosition(inPosition=True/False)  * elevationInPosition(inPosition=True/False)  * etc.    An alternative is to have one or several events with multiple fields, e.g.:  * inPosition(azimuth=True/False, elevation=True/False, ...)  but it only makes sense to combine data that you get in a single status call. Otherwise you imply information that you don't have!    The same basic problem applies to most other events, including {{motionEnabled}} and {{driveFault}}, {{overTemp}}, {{speedLimitReached}}, {{accelerationLimitReached}} (why do those last two even exist?).  * It would be much better to have a separate event for each device  * We need a flag or other simple way to tell """"this condition is present"""" and """"this condition is absent"""".    Other changes to consider:  * Remove the word {{Change}} from all events that have that suffix. """"Change"""" is redundant. The event is being output *because* the state changed.  * Combine {{lockingPinEngaged}} and {{lockingPinDisengaged}} as {{lockingPin}} with a boolean {{engaged}} flag.  * Combine {{brakeEngaged}} and {{brakeDisengaged}} as {{brake}} with a boolean {{engaged}} flag.  * Combine {{motionEnabled}} and {{driveFault}} into {{driveState}}, with an enum field for enabled/not enabled/fault and another field for the fault code (which is null if enabled/disabled).  * Rename {{interlockAlarm}} field {{interlockAlarmCode}} to {{code}} and look for similar redundancies in other topics. There are a lot of fields that seem needlessly verbose (why """"devicePosition"""" instead of """"position"""", etc.)  """
comments: "With Wouter's kind permission I took this on.    Changes:  * Removed deprecated fields from all topics.  * Added topic-level descriptions to all topics.  * Tightened up existing description tags.  * Overhauled the events.  * Added target events.  * Renamed elRate/azRate to velocity in commands. Yes EIE uses the other terms, but let's not expose that ugliness to the public API.    Telemetry changes:  * Renamed telemetry topics.  * Added a timestamp field to each topic.  * Removed Error fields; they duplicate information.  * Added actual and commanded velocity fields to azimuth and light/wind screen.  * Removed the mysterious event-like {{summary}} topic.  * We still have no idea what the interlocks and thermal topics actually report -- interlocks is especially mysterious due to the field being named ""data"" and the type being float. But we can update that later when we have more information.    Pull requests:  * https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_xml/pull/292  * https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_idl/pull/24" [SEP] "T

summary: "Implement time and size based retention policies in Kafka's NTS deployment"
description: """On the NTS cluster, Kafka has a small ~50G partition to store its logs, which filled up in about 48h with the CSCs running on the NCSA test stand.          We have to fine-tune the Kafka broker retention policy to avoid that."""
comments: "Time retention is configured at the broker level with the following parameters:    we had it initially set to 24h, then 2h and now 1h... since the number of producers running in the NCSA test stand is changing a lot, it is hard to estimate a retention time that would prevent filling up the 50G partition. As a safeguard, we can add a retention configuration based on size:    it is recommended to use 80-90% of the available space.     Note that the value must be quoted in the Helm chart otherwise it gets converted to another format and the configuration fails:    Here's a [good tutorial on retention policy configuration|https://medium.com/@sunny_81705/kafka-log-retention-and-cleanup-policies-c8d9cb7e09f8#:~:text=Default%20retention%20period%20for%20Segments,structure%20for%20a%20Topic%20partition]." summary: "newinstall fails when miniconda path not supplied by user, but already exists"
description: """Jobs that use newinstall are failing when the miniconda already exists.    Previously, when a miniconda path already existed, even if a user was prompted to install it or it was assumed to install it via the {{-b}} option, an installation was not attempted and it was assumed to use that miniconda installation.    Changes from DM-24972 assumed that the miniconda path does not exist if a user asks to install it, either by typing """"yes"""" interactively or using batch mode. """
comments: "To make this more confusing, the tarballs jobs seem like the should be clearing out the directory before running {{bash newinstall.sh -cb}}, but that seems like that is not the case." [SEP] "The new changes do the following:    By default, assume we are not installing.  If a user did not specify the path, use the default generated path.  If the generated path does not exist, mark the path for installation by setting do_install = true    If do_install == false, verify the path (auto-generated or user-supplied) has conda installed by checking for the {{conda}} command. If the command does not exist, newinstall will fail." [SEP] "Looks good to me."

summary: "Change Health & Status data to direct FIFO"
description: """Health & Status data are routed in FPGA through the Request queue. The 64bit data are then squeezed into 4 16bit Response FIFO values. This design is suboptimal for two reasons:   * response queue is also used to send Modbus responses from ILCs, the mix effectively prevents multi-threading on CPU (Host) side to query H&S data and responses at the same time   * as H&S data are mixed with responses, it's hard to tell which data on reposne queue are from H&S and which are from ModBus - that is probably reason why vital H&S collection was implemented in FPGA, but not in C/C++ controller (where it most likely never worked)"""

description: """Remy Joseph noticed that in scarlet residuals there appeared to be a problem fitting the center of galaxies:     !Screen Shot 2020-06-04 at 12.23.24 AM.png|thumbnail!     This ticket tracks work done by myself to assist Remy and <USER>to identify the cause of the behavior and determine if there is something broken in either our constraints or optimization algorithm used in scarlet."""
comments: "It appears that the initial issue that triggered this investigation is now understood, namely that we simply weren't letting scarlet run long enough to fit the centers of bright objects, which take longer to fit than fainter sources. The reason why can be seen in the following gif animation of a single band blend in HSC, initialized with a random morphology for each source in the image and _no constraints_.   This was done to eliminate the possibility that our monotonicity or other constraints could be interfering with the gradients calculated by the adaprox algorithm (optimization):    !test.gif!     We can see the behavior of the fit, where the centers of objects oscillate like ""ripples in a lake"" (as Peter stated) as the gradients slowly work to fit the pixels that are initialized furthest from the truth. So increasing the number of iterations to 200 (our convergence criteria stopped around 80 iterations for the displayed blend) gave the model more time to reach equilibrium with the noise. comments: "A note to begin: since I did not compare sizes/moments with truth, most of my conclusions about structural parameters are based on eyeballing the effective radius vs apparent magnitude distributions in DC2 simulations and in HSC. In all cases, there are many sources - usually false positive or low S/N detections - with unreasonably large sizes for their magnitudes and in many cases overestimated fluxes (I call this the runaway size problem). Photometric accuracy/precision is based on comparing to truth in the DC2 simulations.    A summary is that none of the experiments in this epic provided significant improvements in parameter recovery. However, results do change significantly if a free background level is included. This suggests that source detection and background modelling are limiting galaxy modelling more so than specifics of the galaxy models themselves. summary: "Support the Closed-loop Simulation of Corner Wavefront Sensor in OPD Level"
description: """We need to support the closed-loop simulation for corner wavefront sensor (WFS) in the optical path difference (OPD) level. At this moment, we only support the condition of commissioning camera (ComCam). The code is *[opdCloseLoop.py|https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_phosim/blob/master/bin.src/opdCloseLoop.py]* (ComCam in OPD level). The reason to do this is to simplify the following study/ test of control algorithm with non-zero camera rotation angle. It is noted that the *ts_wep* just tries to calculate the OPD as accurate as possible. Therefore, we could simplify the test of control algorithm by using the OPD directly instead of calculating the wavefront error from donut images.    To play the existed code with PhoSim, please follow: [Use of Active Optics Close Loop Simulation|https://confluence.lsstcorp.org/display/LTS/Use+of+Active+Optics+Close+Loop+Simulation]. The idea of full simulation is [here|https://confluence.lsstcorp.org/display/LTS/AOS+Close+Loop+Simulation+Workflow+Diagrams]. To understand the simulation platform, please follow [PhoSim wiki|https://bitbucket.org/phosim/phosim_release/wiki/Home]. It is noted that we used the aos branch in *[phosim_syseng4|https://github.com/lsst-ts/phosim_syseng4]*.    The sensor names are """"R44_S00"""" (field=31), """"R04_S20"""" (field=32), """"R00_S22"""" (field=33), and """"R40_S02"""" (field=34). This can follow: [sensorNameToFieldIdx.yaml|https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_ofc/blob/master/policy/lsst/sensorNameToFieldIdx.yaml]. The related field positions are at *[OpdMetrology.getDefaultLsstWfsGQ()|https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_phosim/blob/master/python/lsst/ts/phosim/OpdMetrology.py]*. You could use the related set function: *OpdMetrology.setDefaultLsstGQ()* directly. When evaluating the effective PSSN (normalized point source sensitivity), the related OPD are calculated with the field positions defined in *OpdMetrology.setDefaultLsstGQ()*. There are 31 field points in this PSSN evaluation. To have the idea of sensor name and field position for LSST, please follow [mapSensorAndFieldIdx.py|https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_wep/blob/master/examples/mapSensorAndFieldIdx.py]. You may need this to decide the sensor name for each field points (0-30, total=31). To understand the PSSN, please follow the Document-17242 for the details.    To prove the finish of this ticket, need to show the iteration image as in DM-24423. The effective Gaussian quadrature FWHM (full width at half maximum) should be around 0.12 arcsec after the iteration is done. description: """The rotator Simulink model should be able to track multiple targets. The system will fail/ crash if there are targets that have different directions. In addition, sometimes, the simulation will fail even though all targets are in the same direction. This task will dig into the Simulink model to see how is fitting of multiple targets.    The following figure demonstrates the failure. The x-axis is the time in second and the y-axis is the value such as the rotation position in degree. There are two targets with positions of +1.5 and -1.5 degree. The simulation time is 30.1 seconds and the simulation crashes in the middle with zero-crossing error.      !trackErrorForTargetsWithDifferentDirections.png|thumbnail!"""
comments: "By ""multiple targets"" do you mean multiple queued (position, velocity, time) track commands with different times that define the desired profile? In other words do you mean interpolating between pairs of (position, velocity, time) track commands?    I would expect that in normal operation the Rotator will receive all (position, velocity, time) commands in advance so that the Rotator can always interpolate. That gives the most accurate motion profile. If it can't handle this situation, that is indeed a serious bug!    Also note: we almost certainly need to be able to queue up more than 2 position, velocity, time triplets (though the path generator code would only ever be interpolating between two of them). This allows us to have a bigger margin for a slightly late track command. For example if we send track commands at 10Hz and we want 0.5 seconds of margin, we need to be able to send approximately 5 track commands in advance.    For the record: if I understood what Tekniker told me, the TMA requires more t

description: """The general idea is that you can use a Faust agent to process messages from a given Kafka topic and add those messages to a Faust table for persistence. The table is configured as a tumbling window. The window configuration parameters are the window size and the expiration time. When the window expires a callback function is called to process the messages in that window. That's when the aggregation happens and when a new Kafka topic is produced with the aggregated values.    In a previous ticket DM-24600 we created Faust records dynamically which saved us from generating static code for the models in Faust.    After some investigation, we concluded that it would be difficult to create the agent's code at runtime.  We'll try the approach of generating the code for the agents from a template and use Faust auto-discover mechanism to import the corresponding modules."""
comments: "[PR #11 | https://github.com/lsst-sqre/kafka-aggregator/pull/11] implements the Faust agent code generation using a Jinja2 template. Source topics to aggregate are discovered from Kafka based on a regex and a blacklist set in the configuration. This works for the aggregation example too.     The sequence of commands to run the Kafka aggregator looks like:    Initialize the example:         Generate the agents:        This command shows that Faust is able to auto-discover the new agent:         At this point Faust has everything it needs to run the workers, schemas for the source and aggregated topics are registered, Faust Record for the aggregated topic is created at runtime, etc.  Complete instructions on how to run the example can be found in the README." summary: "Put the Rotator into Fault State if no Track Command for a Long Time"
description: """If the rotator does not receive the track command for a long time, the rotator should be put into the Fault state. This could be 0.5 second (by Tiago) or 1 second (by Russell). At this moment, we do see this in the Simulink model by the simulation experiment, but we do not know how that is decided. In addition, we did not see this when we did the test on summit. This task needs to figure out what is the existed logic in model and how to update it.    The following is the figure to show the simulation result (detail is at DM-25243):    !track_v0_1_long_enterFault.png|thumbnail!    The system is at the Fault state around time equals 20.2 seconds (no new track command after time=6 seconds)."""
comments: "As noted on DM-25243 the path generator probably interpolates between track (position, velocity, time) commands. If so, then the system should probably go to FAULT if time > the time of the most recent track command -- in other words as soon as the path generator can no longer interpolate and has to start extrapolating instead.    This is different than I said earlier, but I think it's well worth considering. It means that the margin is given by how early we send our track commands -- in other words the pointing component determines the margin, not the Rotator controller." [SEP] "If there is the communication problem between the pointing component and rotator controller for somehow that no new track command is received, we may still need to put the rotator into the Fault state for the safety." [SEP] "Yes. I agree. I was suggesting that the time be based on the time of the most recent track command -- when current time > the time in that command then the rotator stops and goes into FAULT. In practice that wou

summary: "Fix the Bugs of Trajectory If No Track Command Received In the Tracking"
description: """The path generator will continuously update the demand position even if there is no new track command received in the SlewAndTrack sub-state. The initial suspect is that this comes from the non-zero target velocity in the final received track command. This will continue to update the position command. The initial idea to fix this is to reset the velocity command to be zero if no new track command received.    The following figure (x-axis: time in seconds, y-axis: value such as position in degree) shows this problem. There are two targets (positions = 1.2 degree and 3 degree. velocity = 0.01 deg/sec and -0.01 deg/sec). The track command does not continue after the time equals 6 seconds. We could see the rotator position is at 3 degree when time is at 8 seconds but the demand position is decreasing after time=8 seconds (rotator's position keeps the same) because of the second target has the velocity of -0.01 deg/sec.    !track_v0_1_long_enterFault.png|thumbnail!     """
comments: "If the controller's path generator interpolates between (position, velocity, time) track commands then it probably should not extrapolate at all. I certainly hope that is how the path generator works, as I find it hard to believe it can produce a nice motion profile otherwise.    As per DM-25245 I hope that the low-level controller will go into FAULT state if track commands do not come often enough. I don't know how that relates to this ticket, but I think it crucial that rotator never stop if a track command is late, then start moving again when the track command arrives. That would lead to a silently unacceptable motion profile.    I added more comments on DM-25245" [SEP] "Update the module of ""Track Mod Cmd Gen"" in rotator controller to let the velCmd to be zero if NewCommandReceived==0. However, the result keeps the same and I suspect I forgot to consider the slew and overshoot of posCmd. This is my attempt to codify what I think has been the de factor and/or de jure situation to date, rather than advocating a particular policy: I'd encourage members of the DMCCB to consider whether this is what they _really want to_ approve before we go ahead and merge this.    Note in particular that we should talk about how we want to handle deprecations. The current Dev Guide is pretty lax — “if no one would object, a deprecation can be made without an RFC” — whereas LDM-294 adopts a harder line — “one of the following changes .. API changes to the codebase, including deprecation .. need to be flagged and therefore approved by the DMCCB”.    Finally, I'll add that if we decide to adopt these changes (or some version of them), I suggest we then replace the majority of LDM-294 Appendix C with a referen

summary: "Investigate Control Center instability on NCSA teststand cluster"
description: """Control Center restarted about 50 times in the last 15h          From the logs it looks like the reason was that one of the brokers was down:        And that put Control Center in a state where it was indefinitely trying to rebalance the topics and then restarting        It was not evident from Argo CD that one of the brokers had problems and since we didn't have Control Center to monitor the cluster health it took me some time to realize the real cause of the problem.    Having problems to restart the failing broker:        """
comments: "Also having problems to restart zookeeper:        One of the zookeeper pods seems stuck trying to create a file:            trying to recreate the PVs I saw these errors when deleting them:        That suggests a NFS issue. After recreating the PVs could restart the brokers and zookeeper and the cluter was healthy again.    NOTE: every time we delete the PVs we lose the schemas in the Schema Registry. Before recreating the pvs and restarting Kafka we have to stop the producers otherwise the producers will keep sending messages to the brokers and no schemas will be found. When the producers are restarted the schemas are registered again with the Schema Register.     " [SEP] "The conclusion, from what we are seeing in this cluster is that it is a bad idea to have the Kafka persistent volumes over NFS, we should use local disk for that (and preferentially SSDs) since the NTS cluster nodes have only about 160G of local storage, I think the solution is to reduce the retention period in Kafka to a couple of hours

comments: "Make sure that the boundaries are clear, i.e.    0 <= azimuth <= 2pi vs. 0 <= azimuth < 2pi  0 <= elevation <= pi/2 vs. 0 <= elevation < pi/2" [SEP] "This could be achieved by making use of ts_simactuators." [SEP] "I am also checking the config parameters to make sure that they are not negative in case of the maximum jerk, acceleration and velocity." [SEP] "In the end I couldn’t get the SimActuators working with my code. For the elevation for a reason I really don’t know the stop command wouldn’t work. For the azimuth rotation no SimActuator simulates what EIE will implement. So I ended up writing two of my own. They do not take acceleration into account but do handle the 0/360 azimuth and 0/90 elevation boundaries properly. They also support switching from one current motion (either move or crawl) to another, to stop and to park (the latter in case of azimuth rotation only)." description: """Write a scanner that will determine versions in use for a SQuaRE repository. For the initial implementation, support:    - Helm charts  - Kustomize chart references  - Python make init  - Docker images in Helm charts  - GitHub actions  - pre-commit hooks"""
comments: "https://github.com/renovatebot/renovate also exists and can be run locally. It supports several things that the GitHub dependabot doesn't support, such as Helm charts, Kustomize references, and Docker images configured in {{Dockerfile}}, {{docker-compose.yaml}}, and Helm charts. It is available as a free application for GitHub for public repositories. I've added it to the lsst-sqre organization to experiment.    GitHub Actions and pre-commit hooks aren't supported by either renovatebot or dependabot. Both support Python dependencies and dependabot copes with frozen PIP dependencies (I haven't tested renovatebot). But it doesn't support updating all the dependencies at once with {{make update-deps}}, which we want. renovatebot does support customized actions, so we may be able to make it support that, but we would then need to self-host it.    There is a similar issue with renovatebot with Docker image dependencies in Helm charts. It can update the dependency, but it doesn't update the chart version or regene

description: """Add circular versions of PointToPoint and TrackingActuator to to ts_simactautors. By """"circular"""" I mean an a rotation with no limits and the ability to take the shortest path to a given target or go in the positive or negative direction.    It will always report position in the range [0, 360) degrees but accept any angle for the commanded angle.    Note on implementation: the most naive implementation is to increment or decrement the internal angle as required. By my calculations it would take a millions of wraps before we lose 0.1"""" accuracy due to roundoff errors. One could get fancier and check if the absolute value of position is > 720 and reduce all elements of the path if so."""

summary: "Inspect initial WCS quality for HSC and DECam"
description: """Gen3 relies on our raw WCSs (which are now generally derived from boresight+rotation+cameraGeom, not FITS WCS header keys) to generate the regions for its spatial relationships, even for derived datasets like calexp that may improve on those WCSs.  This has been a problem for DECam in particular, because we think there are some exposures with significant pointing error.  HSC right now probably has the opposite problem: we're probably padding the raw-WCS-derived regions more than we need to, which is bad for database performance.  The goal of this ticket is to do an analysis of initial WCS quality for both instruments by comparing to a later-stage """"truth"""" WCS - calexp.wcs is probably good enough for this most of the time, though jointcal_wcs might be a bit better.  I'd like to come away from with it with a bit of reusable tooling that we could use to repeat the analysis in the future.  It doesn't need to be super polished (even unit tests might be overkill, as we're not going to run it a ton), but I'd like it to actually be committed to a maintained LSST git repo (obs_base, maybe?).  The specific questions I'd like to answer are:  - Are there visits with unusually large (i.e.) outlier-level pointing errors?  If so, are they a consistent offset in any coordinate system?  I would approach this by transforming (0, 0) in focal plane coordinates to sky via a combination of cameraGeom and then initial or truth WCS, and then averaging over CCDs in a visit and looking for outliers in the difference between initial and truth over all visits.  - If we correct for the above pointing errors, are there significant rotation angle errors?  (This is a bit trickier, and I haven't worked out the details of how I would compute it, but I think something analogous to the previous step would work).  We also don't have any evidence of a problem here, yet, so I'm not totally sure it's worth doing...but if we do have a problem, it would totally confuse the next step.  - If we correct for any outlier-level pointing or rotation angle errors, what is the padding (in pixels) we would need to apply to the initial-WCS bounding boxes to ensure that they always contain the truth-WCS bounding boxes on the sky?  I would approach this by using the WCSs to construct sphgeom.ConvexPolygon objects and using those to do relationship tests; you can find examples of this in the Gen3 code that actually constructs regions for us in defineVisits.py in obs_base.  This analysis probably needs to be done with Gen2 butlers right now, because that's the form in which we have the data (HSC PDR2) or could at least easily produce it (various DECam datasets).  The guts of any tooling would hopefully avoid using any butlers and would this be easily transferrable to Gen3 in the future."""

summary: "Redeploy Kafka on the NCSA test stand and investigations after the cluster intervention"
description: """On May 28th there was maintenance on the NCSA test stand cluster to replace weave CNI plugin by flannel.    I have restarted the EFD services on May 28th and it seemed to be working for some CSCs.    After starting all the CSCs we saw errors like:        That means the schema for a given topic was not registered when the producer started, or that it was registered with a different schema ID than the one used in the serialized message for that topic.    As a conseguence the connector tasks could not start properly and failed with the error above.    It looks like Kafka and the Schema Registry got to an inconsistent state and I suspect that happened because of network instability (?) as the producers and the EFD were running during the intervention.    Anyway, to fix that we stoped the producers and manually deleted that topic from Kafka and the corresponding schema from the registry.        Then we saw the problem for another topic, and repetitively to other topics. I only got the EFD working again after a fresh start i.e. redeploying Kafka removing all the previous topics and schemas :(        """

summary: "Have jointcal fail if the chi2 increases dramatically between steps"
description: """[~<USER> reported a [jointcal failure|https://lsstc.slack.com/archives/C2JPXB4HG/p1590742434125100] where the fitter went from {{chi2/ndof~=40}} to {{~1e7}} during a single rejection step. That suggests something went catastrophically wrong with the fitter at that stage. I think the fit loop should probably exit in this case, with an appropriate error message, and not try to write output files.    I'm not sure what a good """"the fit got so much worse we should just stop"""" threshold should be, but probably {{delta-chi2/ndof>1e5}} is a reasonable start? I also haven't looked at the chi2 behavior during enough large data runs to understand whether significant increases in chi2 between runs is a common occurrence or not: that might be worth exploring.    A related question is what to do when the fitter does not converge after {{max_steps}}: currently jointcal logs an error but continues to attempt to write output. We should probably include a test for the chi2 value at the end of {{_iterate_fit}}, like was added in DM-15241 for a """"converged fit""""."""
comments: "I've learned some useful things as part of DM-25779, one of which is that chi2/ndof can increase because ndof decreased dramatically (e.g. due to changing what is being fit during initialization), while the raw chi2 decreases. This seems to happen somewhat frequently during photometric fitting for HSC, with increases of up to ~50% for one step not too uncommon). So an important question here is whether we should be checking against the raw or reduced chi2." This ticket may short circuit most such cases (where the fitter exceeds {{max_steps}}), since from the logs I saw, most cases of high final chi2 are due to catastrophic failures during fitting like this. summary: "Allow non-saluser users to run jobs inside containers made from develop-env"
description: """The problem:    Jenkins runs under jenkins user. When starting a docker image, jenkins user (1003:1003) is hardcoded and passed to the container. There is a lot of workarounds, involving root user etc, all due to fact that /home/saluser is not readable to anybody else than saluser.    Possible solutions (in arbitrary order):   # add jenkins user/user with UID 1003 and give it some basic rights          2. /home directories are designed to hold user's content. What we see there is a project-wide infrastructure being kept in an arbitrary user home directory and used throughout the project. One obvious solution would be to run chmod a+rwX -R /home/saluser as root somewhere during building the container          3. probably better would be to abandon /home/saluser, and replace it for storage with e.g. /lsst, /vro or /opt/lsst or .. _(LBTO uses /lbto to keep project-wide stuff, and although that looks as a brutal attack to the Unix filesystem, it works - no need to store stuff important for us in /opt; probability some UNIX FS standard will in future use /lsst or any of the proposed directories is close to 0; if /lsst is controversial for storing dev repos, make it /lsst_repo, /lsst_dev/, /lsst/dev..)_ Give write rights to everybody in this develop dir, and make available command (with +s to root) to turn those into write protected directories. That way when building and installation of the software is finished, this script can be called, turn everything important into non-writable, making it impossible for users running container to modify its content (_any user can still run container with -u 0:0 if he/she/it would like to modify it's content)._    The fact that we will lose security for a while when building images is for me irrelevant - as noted above, anybody usually can run a container as root user as a workaround. As noted above, a script can be made to turn security back on for production images (and Jenkinsfiles can be configured to check for this after building Docker production Images..).    A note about directory: while /opt is used for third party packages, as noted above, this is proposed for LSST internal installations/containers, stored under lsstts org, and used primarily for LSST work. Once we have the installation process documented (in Dockerfiles building the containers), anybody can modify it to store packages as needed under /opt/mybestproject .. The distinction between /opt/lsst and /lsst is in this sense minimal, and /lsst will save a few bytes and keyboard hits while make the dir easier to notice and remember."""
comments: "I tried to chmod a+rwX -R /home/saluser, which fixed my problem I am encountering (attaching to image as -u 1003:1003):    [I have no name!@1759be2596e3 repos]$ source /home/saluser/repos/ts_sal/lsstsal/salenv.sh   bash: /home/saluser/repos/ts_sal/lsstsal/salenv.sh: Permission denied    after chmod:    [I have no name!@1759be2596e3 ~]$ source /home/saluser/repos/ts_sal/lsstsal/salenv.sh   SAL development environment is configured         I am willing to do the changes as PR once we find consensual solution." [SEP] "A comment: repos are stored and then setup -r  to make them available in Python path. Having task that will install repo somewhere (/lsst/python, /lsst/lib/python) and have this in PYTHONPATH for container would be great. See for workarounds in e.g. ts_MTAOS Jenkinsfile, calling setup -r before running MTAOS tests." [SEP] "The path of ""/opt/lsst"" is used in DM docker image for the scientific pipeline stack." summary: "Gen 2->3 conversion of DECam repositories can give duplicate defects"
description: """If I convert a DECam Gen 2 repository containing defects, the conversion succeeds, but running {{ProcessCcd}} on the result gives an error:    The immediate cause is that one copy of the defects is from the Gen 2 repository, the other copy was ingested during the conversion.    Discussion on [#dm-middleware|https://lsstc.slack.com/archives/C2JPT1KB7/p1590719387081900] suggests that this is due to conflicting responsibilities:  * {{obs.base.Instrument}} has some [hard-coded definitions|https://github.com/lsst/obs_base/blob/a51665517dd0ecacd8d72fbcead186475fcf66d3/python/lsst/obs/base/instrument.py#L38-L45] of curated files, which are processed any time {{ConvertRepoConfig.doWriteCuratedCalibrations}} is set. Defects are included.  * {{ConvertRepoConfig}} also has a [{{curatedCalibrations}} field|https://github.com/lsst/obs_base/blob/7c071951e367c4bef83840b75921f3a2f3af29cb/python/lsst/obs/base/gen2to3/convertRepo.py#L252-L263], which presumably allows other datasets to be handled using {{writeCuratedCalibrations}}. This list does not include defects by default.  * HSC [adds defects to {{ConvertRepoConfig.curatedCalibrations}}|https://github.com/lsst/obs_subaru/blob/c641360ad764cf8db63ecf1d14af6c93660160ff/config/hsc/convertRepo.py#L35-L36]. The bug described above *does not* occur.  * DECam makes no changes to {{curatedCalibrations}}, and its [config override file|https://github.com/lsst/obs_decam/blob/8567ae41add358da7b9af65fc81c39e39078b975/config/convertRepo.py] makes no mention of defects. The bug *does* occur.    The above behavior implies that DECam repositories with defects cannot be converted using the default configs, which goes against our general aim to provide working defaults for all Tasks.    Possible solutions:  * Do away with {{ConvertRepoConfig.curatedCalibrations}}, as proposed by [~<USER>. However, this does raise the question of how to handle observatory-specific curated calibrations.  * Have DECam explicitly register defects in {{curatedCalibrations}}, as HSC does. May be hard to scale to other instruments.  * Duplicate the dataset types hardcoded into {{Instrument}} in the default {{ConvertRepoConfig.curatedCalibrations}}. Won't benefit configs that overwrite this field instead of appending to it."""
comments: "If conversion calls writeCuratedCalibrations at all then we know exactly what datasets will be written for a given instrument. At the moment you can't tell writeCuratedCalibrations to skip certain items or restrict itself to specific calibrations. If you could do that then the config option would make more sense." [SEP] "Does anyone want to object to my plan of removing the config item completely and having the Instrument class report the list of curated calibrations that it is going to write itself?" [SEP] "By ""the config item"", do you mean {{ConvertRepoConfig.curatedCalibrations}}, {{ConvertRepoConfig.datasetIgnorePatterns}}, or both? Removing both would cause trouble for converting some Gen 2 repositories." [SEP] "I mean that the list of curatedCalibrations in a config would no longer be needed.    I think it makes sense for the code that uses datasetIgnorePatterns to ignore the curatedCalibrations known to the instrument automatically. " [SEP] "[~<USER> this should be a small review. No rush for it. I'

I was surprised by the very poor performance of scipy's direct convolution, since I remembered ProFit's direct convolution being faster than FFTs for images up to ~100x100. The main conclusions are in the plot at the end of [this notebook | https://github.com/lsst-dm/modelling_research/blob/tickets/DM-25154/jupyternotebooks/convolution.ipynb] (also attached for convenience) :  - there appears to be something terribly wrong with scipy's direct convolution; it's much slower than ProFit's naive direct convolution for no discernible reason.  - similarly, astropy's fft convolution methods have some inefficiencies/large overhead compared to scarlet  - ProFit's optimized direct convolution (u

summary: "Make it clear that gen3 instrument class paths don't need .instrument"
description: """In DM-23980 I standardized the instrument class names for Gen3. Doing so means that both {{lsst.obs.lsst.LsstCam}} and {{lsst.obs.lsst.instrument.LsstCam}} work. Since we don't want multiple ways of specifying the same instrument and we already know of cases where people think they need to include the {{.instrument}}, rename the internal {{instrument.py}} to {{_instrument.py}} to make it clearer that this name should not be used.    One caveat is that when we register the instrument we call getFullTypeName so if you specify the short name the registry still sees the full name and we therefore have to promise not to move that class later inside the package. One solution to that is for the instrument class to not use getFullTypeName but to """"know"""" the public name of itself."""
comments: "Plan is:    # Modify getFullTypeName such that it strips out the _ components from path  # Move all the instrument.py files to _instrument.py files.in obs packages." [SEP] "[~<USER> Updating getFullTypeName was fairly easy and I added a test. The obs packages simply get renamed instrument.py to _instrument.py so the actual code change is tiny.    One outcomes of this is that we now correctly report that lsst.daf.butler.registry.Registry is lsst.daf.butler.registry.Registry and not lsst.daf.butler.registry._registry.Registry. It can't work out that it's also lsst.daf.butler.Registry."

summary: "Fix the race condition between the RPM daily job and the IDL Conda job"
description: """There is only about a minute between the end of the RPM Daily Build and the start of the IDL_Conda job.  This isn't quite enough time for the yum repo to finish indexing all the new RPMs, so the IDL job often fails as a result.  Figure out a way to have the IDL job wait until the desired RPM is available before trying to install it."""
comments: "Turns out Jenkinsfiles offers a waitUntil function and a timeout function which, in conjuction, offer a for-loop like function that I used to query the Nexus repo for the desired RPM every 15 seconds for up to 5 minutes.  See the PR: https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_idl/pull/22" [SEP] "The IDL job now has a stage to check the repository for the desired RPM before continuing with the build.  After 5 minutes, if the RPM is not found, the job terminates."

description: """In a video chat, [~<USER> pointed out to me that the Gaia DR2 catalog provides position parameter correlations, which can be quite significant for many sources, sometimes even larger than the single parameter errors. We have slots in our refcats for covariances, but no code to do the conversion nor config options to extract those values from an external catalog (see note in the {{IngestIndexedReferenceTask}} class docstring). Now that we have Gaia DR2 testdata for jointcal to process, I noticed that the final chi2 were very low (~1e-3), which may be related to this issue.    At minimum, if we can convert the ra/dec correlations to covariances ([~<USER> said a colleague had code for that), jointcal should be able to use them relatively easily. Making use of the other position covariances (e.g. {{ra_parallax_corr}}, {{dec_pmra_corr}}) would require additional code in jointcal.    How much this affects jointcal's final fit parameters vs. just the fit statistics is an open question. We might want to just wait for Gaia DR3 to be released (originally planned for mid-2021, but now delayed due to COVID-19), as it should have reduced correlations and may have a different format.    Here's the link to the DR2 GAIA_SOURCE catalog datamodel, since I always have to click through a bunch of pages to find it:  https://gea.esac.esa.int/archive/documentation/GDR2/Gaia_archive/chap_datamodel/sec_dm_main_tables/ssec_dm_gaia_source.html"""
comments: "Note for future me, since I just figured this out: Gaia correlation coefficients are {{rho_xy}} such that the off-diagonal element of the covariance matrix is:        where x,y are each of the relevant parameters. So, computing the relevant covariances should be pretty simple in {{IngestGaiaManager}} given the above relationship. However, we probably also have to change {{makeSchema}} and the ingest Config to increase the dimensions of the respective errors. {{makeMinimalSchema}} also would need an update to support e.g. ra_pm covariances.    See ""Error propagation"" here:    https://gea.esac.esa.int/archive/documentation/GDR2/Data_processing/chap_cu3ast/sec_cu3ast_intro/ssec_cu3ast_intro_tansforms.html"

description: """Recent testing on DM-17597 has shown some small inconsistencies in the {{fgcmcal}} solutions on different platforms, particularly in the """"tract"""" mode.  Further investigation has revealed that this is because many internal calculations are performed with 32-bit floats rather than 64-bit floats which yields floating point errors that accumulate on different platforms. comments: "I'm seeing lots of InfluxDB pods getting evicted after it is running for a couple of days. Looks like the memory limit specs are wrong, because of the exit code 137 below. However for the current one that's running I'm seeing:            I'll keep monitoring and check memory usage next time the pod gets evicted." [SEP] "Or perhaps it's just memory pressure as we are running on pretty low resource machines the  {{n1-standard-2}} have only 7GB of memory per node. summary: "SAL's topic hash code generator not consistent"
description: """The code in ts_sal v4.1.2 that generates the has code for DDS topics seems to have a flaw. When I switched the Script SAL component to explicitly list the generic topics it uses (ts_xml tickets/DM-22109), the hash code for {{logevent_checkpoints}} changed (compared to ts_xml develop 133d378) even though {{logevent_checkpoints}} is not a generic topic and did not change at all. I see the same issue in ts_sal v4.1.1    This is quite worrisome for using the new ability to list generic topics as it means the new IDL will not be compatible with the old IDL even though the schemas have not changed.    I have attached the two IDL files.  """
comments: "I believe this was a side effect of a prior change to the whitespace   in the private_ fields. It is not connected with the Generic tags and  will not happen again unless we change the private_ fields again,   which we are about to do, so expect ALL revcodes to change for   SAL 4.2" [SEP] "As noted this was a side effect of a prior change to the whitespace in the idl which should not happen again." [SEP] "Since this is just whitespace, surely there's a way to change the whitespace in the IDL without changing the hash codes? This is a big breaking change. I'd like to avoid it if we can."
