summary: "Implement proper variance plane calculation in the decorrelation afterburner"
description: """In the DM-24371 implementation, we throw away the difference image variance plane as it cannot be afterburned due to unaccounted covariances. However the variance plane calculation from the originals does not consider the effect of the overall (afterburned) convolution of (the original) pixels at the moment which it could do.    For more accurate variance plane calculation, re-do the variance plane operations separately on the science and template image original variance planes using the idea of ZOGY eq. (26)-(27) then add them. This is expected to increase the CPU cost by x4 of the afterburner. Consider keeping this approach optional, especially not doing on the spatially varying case just once for the whole image."""

summary: "Fix non-normalized matching kernel case in the decorrelation afterburner"
description: """In DM-24371, the implemented afterburner correction implicitly assumes that the _matching kernel_ is sum == 1 which is not usually the case, especially without scaling the science and reference image values by their photometric calibration.   * The afterburner correction does not sum to 1, which changes the fluxes of the difference image (or shift its photometric scale).   * The variance plane calculation of the afterburned difference image is scaled to the level of var1+var2 which is incorrect if the matching kernel contains flux rescaling, i.e. not sum == 1.   * The same consideration is required to support a _preconvolution_ kernel, though it needs further investigation to determine the statistical meaning of an _afterburned_ _preconvolved_ image.   * Also, this causes the corrected PSF not to be automatically sum == 1, though this is corrected by the {{KernelPsf}} creation."""

summary: "Streamlined naming convention for the REST API resources in the Qserv Replication/Ingest System"
description: """The current implementation of the REST API provided by the Qserv Replication/Ingest system for exploring and managing Qserv may need to be improved. Presently, the API provides two groups of resources:  {code:}  /replication/v1/<service>  /ingest/v1/<service>    /meta/version    In this case availability of services and their behavior will be completely driven by the current version of the services. Another benefit of this schema is that it would with documenting the REST API since each version of the documentation could refer to specific numbers returned by the proposed metadata service."""
comments: "The proposal of dropping the version string in the resource names sounds fine to me. If we are not planning to support multiple versions in one deployment, I don't see we need the version string.  I assume the APIs will stay quite stable and rarely change. Versioning with the documentations sounds good.  I haven't read https://confluence.lsstcorp.org/display/DM/User+guide+for+the+Qserv+Ingest+system but I wonder if we may want to update (minor tweak of details following code changes) a lot more frequently than the web service APIs.  Even with that I don't see a problem dropping the version string. " [SEP] "PR: https://github.com/lsst/qserv/pull/540" [SEP] "LGTM"

summary: "Review the Control Algorithm of Rotator in Phase 2"
description: """This task will review the rotator Simulink model and try to reproduce the error we had on the summit test that the demand rotator position can be -2000 peak. Another error we have is the peak between tracking targets. We will also investigate the stop profile in model. This is the phase 2 of DM-24799."""
comments: "Based on the state machine in rotator Simulink model, the sub-states of MovingPt2Pt, SlewingAndTracking, and ConstVel will go to ContolledStopping sub-state after issuing the Stop command.    The trackStart command in DDS will send the ""Track"" command in Simulink model actually. The track command in DDS just provides the data of tai, angle, and velocity.    The test report with pointing component is [here|https://jira.lsstcorp.org/secure/Tests.jspa#/testPlayer/LVV-C109] and [SCTR13|https://sctr-13.lsst.io/v/v1.1/index.html].    In my memory, after issuing the Stop command in tracking, the system will be in the SlewingAndTracking sub-state instead of stationary sub-state." [SEP] "Set the rotator initial position to be 0, move to 1.5 degree at time=0.6 second, and stop the motion at time=1.5 second. I got the following figures to prove the rotator stopped in the moving and the systems transitioned into the ControlledStopping sub-state and then, the stationary sub-state.    !moveAndStopRotPos.png|thumbnail!!m

description: """While trying to figure out {{pipetask run}} and a new pipeline simultaneously, I've encountered the following error messages that I believe could be improved:  * If I omit the {{--butler-config}} argument, I get {{sqlite3.OperationalError: no such table: collection}}. It would be more helpful to warn that no Butler repository has been found/selected.  * If I have a mismatch between the dimensions  of a dataset as listed in PipelineTask connections and in the registry, I get {{ValueError: Supplied dataset type \[dataset] inconsistent with registry definition \[dataset]}}. It would help to also mention which task is the problem; the stack trace is all from general-purpose code.  * If I can't account for a particular dataset, I get {{RuntimeError: Expected exactly one instance of input \[dataset]}}. As above, it would help for debugging to know which task is being processed.  * If I omit the {{--register-dataset-types}} argument, I get {{KeyError: """"DatasetType \[dataset] could not be found.""""}}, suggesting that there is a matching problem with the pipeline. Given that this is an extremely common user error, it would be helpful to suggest that it might be a registration problem instead.  * If I forget to provide an {{--input}} when it's mandatory, I get an {{Expected exactly one instance of input}} error with a seemingly arbitrary data ID. This message misleadingly suggests the problem is with the dataset, not the collection.  * An invalid storage class raises a bare {{KeyError}}. It would be more helpful to report which connection and/or which dataset type caused the error.  * If I pass a {{--data-query}} argument that does not match any IDs, I get {{UnboundLocalError: local variable 'n' referenced before assignment}}. An empty query should be handled explicitly by the code.  * If I'm running on a repository with an out-of-date schema, I get {{sqlalchemy.exc.OperationalError: (sqlite3.OperationalError) attempt to write a readonly database}}. It would be more helpful to say that the schema is out of date.  * Debug log messages need a bit of work: ctrl.mpexec.mpGraphExecutor DEBUG: Executing QuantumIterData(index=16, quantum=<lsst.daf.butler.core.quantum.Quantum object at 0x7faeb68908c0>, taskDef=<lsst.pipe.base.pipeline.TaskDef object at 0x7faeb2e05090>, dependencies=frozenset()) to make them usable."""

summary: "Implement the compute() method in the Aggregator class"
description: """Compute summary statistics like {{count}}, {{min}}, {{mean}}, {{stddev}}, {{median}}, and {{max}} for the source fields in a list of incoming messages.    These stats are fixed in the code, for now. I think it won't be difficult to define these operations in the configuration but that is left to the future.    Evaluate if we use the Python statistics module, Numpy, or Pandas for that.        """
comments: "We have at least three options for computing summary statistics in Python that we could use for the Kafka aggregator, the [Python statistics module|https://docs.python.org/3/library/statistics.html], [Numpy|https://numpy.org/] and [Pandas|https://pandas.pydata.org/].    While Numpy is built for speed, Python's statistics module is built for precision, and Pandas has some convenience methods to aggregate time-series data like [pandas.Series.resample()|https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/reference/api/pandas.Series.resample.html].    For the Kafka aggregator, it is not obvious that we need the speed of Numpy or the convenience of Pandas.  With Faust we are aggregating data in real-time operating over lists of a few hundred to a thousand data points on aggregation windows of a few seconds, and we have to wait for the window to accumulate the data before we aggregate anyway.     As long as we don't fall behind we can prioritize accuracy over speed.     In particular, the [{{statistics.mean()}} is known

summary: "RemoteCommand.set_start should not use data from the previous command"
description: """In ts_salobj {{RemoteCommand.set_start}} presently uses data from the previous command as default values for fields. This is dangerous. [~<USER> reports he has been bitten by this a few times.    I propose to fix it by modifying {{RemoteCommand.set}} to create a new sample before setting the specified fields. That will also fix {{RemoteCommand.set_start}} and makes the two methods self-consistent.    I have tried that and found:  * ts_salobj unit tests all pass. Thus the old behavior was not tested, which is unfortunate but it was never intended to be a useful feature. The new behavior definitely deserves to be tested.  * ts_scriptqueue unit tests all pass. Good!  * ts_standard_scripts unit tests: one test fails so that script may need an update  * ts_observatory_control unit tests all pass"""

summary: "Use GCS instead of AWS-S3 for the Butler datastore"
description: """Google Cloud Storage (GCS) provides S3-compatible access via Boto.  For authentication, GCS provides the [HMAC keys|https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/authentication/hmackeys]  which can be the drop-in replacement for AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY.     In this ticket I have a few minor changes to use a GCS bucket as the datastore:    - it needs the GCS endpoint URL   - {{client.upload_file}} doesn't work for files >8MB. In the default bucket setup it would try to use multi-part unload. S3 has a particular brand of multipart upload that GCS doesn't support yet.  A workaround is to switch to {{put_object}}.  - For a non-empty bucket, {{bucket.objects.all().delete()}} works on AWS but not on GCS. I only see it used in {{daf_butler/tests/test_s3utils.py}}.  As the tests use moto it doesn't really matter. But I feel it would be more consistent to use the low-level client in both setUp and tearDown so I rewrote it too. """
comments: "Looks okay. I'd still like the boto3 imports to be protected though." [SEP] "Thank you for your helpful review!     Merged.       BTW in running ci_hsc_gen3 with a AWS S3 bucket I'm seeing     with either w_2020_20 or w_2020_21  (before this merge) . Not sure if that needs further attention.   " [SEP] "The warning has been there forever. It's a boto3 bug and I think there is a GitHub issue for it."

summary: "Flux-associated flag fields in ScienceSourceSelector are incorrect"
description: """When working on DM-24935, I noticed that in {{ScienceSourceSelector}} that when a flux is checked, the associated flag field is also checked with     {code:python}  flagField = self.fluxField + """"_flag""""  {code} https://github.com/lsst/meas_algorithms/blob/b97ba8bf064112940b133beb7b5d8f5936140fec/python/lsst/meas/algorithms/sourceSelector.py#L252    However, the {{fluxField}} is typically something like {{slot_CalibFlux_instFlux}}.  And in that case, the associated flag field should be {{slot_CalibFlux_flag}}, while the source selector is looking for {{slot_CalibFlux_instFlux_flag}}.  The logic of the source selector is to treat missing flag fields as all """"okay"""" so this passes without warning.    Note that this is an issue in {{FluxLimit}}, while {{MagnitudeLimit}} and {{SignalToNoiseLimit}} have a similar problem with {{flux}}.    I'm not sure how much downstream code relies on this; I know that {{fgcmcal}} separately checks for {{slot_CalibFlux_flag}} in the list of bad flags, and therefore should be okay."""

summary: "Modifying published catalogs and tables in Qserv"
description: """The present implementation of the Replication/Ingest system's management system already supports deleting partially ingested catalogs (not tables) using the following resource:  ||method||resource||  |DELETE|/ingest/database/:name|    Unfortunately, the present mechanism leaves behind a number of other use cases which are still important. Hence the *main* objective of the proposed effort is to extend the existing implementation of the system's management interface (the REST API) to allow deleting select tables from the *non*-{{published}} catalogs as well as tables from the {{published}} catalogs, or the whole catalogs. These are examples of catalogs which may need to be deleted:   * test catalogs   * obsolete or wrong (based on the incorrect input) catalogs   * catalogs for which a new (better) version had been ingested   * less important catalogs (to clear disk space in Qserv workers before ingesting more important ones)   * catalogs contributed by users if a users is n longer interested in the catalog    The *second* objective of the effort is to implement a mechanism for *un-{{publishing}}* catalogs. In addition to the above stated scenarios, this one would also allow _extending_ existing catalogs with more tables, _chunks_, adding rows to existing _regular_ or _partitioned_ (_chunked_) tables, removing rows (based on a specific {{super-transaction}}) from existing _regular_ or _partitioned_ (_chunked_) tables. The proposed _bi-directional_ transitions of catalogs between the {{published}} and *non-*{{published}} states would be needed for   * QA-ing large catalogs during their _incremental_ ingests   * fixing problems with catalog ingests which were discovered during QA. This would be done by removing contributions into the catalog made during a specific {{super-transaction}}   * supporting user-level (contributed) catalogs which may be less static by their very nature compared with the production ones (LSST DRP, etc.)    Additional requirements/restrictions:   * all operations affecting *non-*{{published}} catalogs should begin with *un-*{{publishing}} a catalog first.   * the operations affecting the {{published}} catalogs will require adding extra security (management privileges) on top of what was already implemented with the {{auth_key}} based authorization: https://jira.lsstcorp.org/browse/DM-22745. One option here which won't require changing the protocol would be to discriminate different values of the keys to treat some of those as the """"high privilege"""" ones.   * the operation should *not* allow deleting the {{director}} table alone unless the whole database is being deleted.    Deleting a table requires eliminating the following data within Qserv and the Replication system's configuration:   * all instances of the table at workers   * an instance of the table from the {{information_schema}} table at Qserv {{czar}}'s database   * the the corresponding entry from {{CSS}}   * the table entry from the Replication System's Configuration    Deleting a whole catalog (database) requires deleting:   * all instances of the database at workers   * an instance of the database from the {{information_schema}} table at Qserv {{czar}}'s database   * the the corresponding entry from {{CSS}}   * the {{secondary_index}} for the {{director}} table of the database   * the {{empty chunk list}} file or the table.   * the database entry from the Replication System's Configuration    The proposed operation of *un*-{{publishing}} a catalog requires:   * eliminating the catalog from {{CSS}} to prevent Qserv from seeing the catalog   * reinstalling MySQL partitions (or creating the default partition *0* if none existed before) at all tables of the catalog. This step should also affect the {{secondary index}}    The the following resource are to be added:  ||method||resource||role||  |DELETE|/ingest/table/:name|deleting select tables of the *non*-{{published}} catalogs|  |PUT|/replication/database/:name|*un*-{{publish}} a catalog, so that it could be further modified|"""

description: """MultiProFit currently only implements priors that are attached to individual parameters, which are inflexible and impractical. This ticket is to implement some more useful priors aplied to arbitrary transformations of one or more parameters, including:  - a conditional size prior (e.g. on PSF magnitude);  - a shape prior;  - a background level prior."""
comments: "This ended up taking most of June, but I implemented the priors as planned and tested them on DC2 and HSC UDeep/COSMOS. I've attached the PDF slides I went through in team meeting today: [^galaxy_sizes_priors_dc2.pdf] The default settings for the priors are provisional and could certainly use improvement before going live in production use, but that can wait. Some more details on what I did:   * I took the slope of the size prior for all galaxy models by eyeballing the PSF mag - Sersic R_eff relation for isolated galaxies in COSMOS/HST-F814W imaging. I set the uncertainty at a generous 0.2 dex. The intrinsic scatter of the size-mass relation ought to be considerably smaller, but given that these are apparent sizes and magnitudes I thought a conservative value would be wise.   * I set the default size prior for the single Gaussian (PSF-convolved) model to be 0.2 pixels, applied to sigma_x and sigma_y. This should make this model more suitable for point sources, per discussions I had with [~<USER>. I haven't c

description: """It is really nice that the search fills in the URL so it can be copied and shared, but using the URL results in a redirect loop.  This can be triggered by either:  1 doing a search and hitting the reload button in the browser  2 clicking on [this link|https://search-demo.lsst.io/search?query=test]"""
comments: "I think we've cracked this. One change that needed to be be made was in the search app to always include a trailing slash in the path (i.e. {{/search/?query=vault)}}. Gatsby natively prefers trailing slashes. Before we were rewriting the browser URL with just the directory name (i.e. {{/search?query=vault}}), but that actually triggers a 301 redirect on the client.    Second, we've modified the Fastly Varnish configuration for directory redirects to access the {{index.html}} file in the S3 bucket if the path ends with a trailing slash.    Before, the redirect VCL was:    The new redirect VCL is:    So the key parts of this are:   * Test if the *path* ends with ""/"" rather than the whole URL. This lets us be compatible with query strings.   * When setting the new req.url, append ""index.html"" to the path, which excludes the query string.     "

"""When inserting fake sources using {{insertFakes}}, it is necessary to subsequently run {{multiBandDriver}} with {{doDetection=True}}. Currently, the detection options for {{detectCoaddSources}} are not propagated through to {{multiBandDriver}}, resulting in different configuration setups relative to a standard image processing run. Specifically, the {{detectCoaddSources.detection}} config options {{reEstimateBackground}}, {{background.binSize}} and {{tempWideBackground.binSize}} are not set. summary: "Move NamedKeyDict and NamedValueSet out of utils and clean up typing"
description: """An early attempt to do some of DM-24938 on DM-24734 ran afoul of messiness involving regular dictionaries with dimension element names vs. NamedKeyDict objects holding real DimensionElement instances.  This is exactly the sort of stuff mypy is designed to fix, but the type annotations for NamedKeyDict and NamedValueSet are tricky and in some cases incorrect, and need to be fixed first.    We're also regularly using these containers in downstream packages because they're the natural way to pass around and group by DatasetTypes, and that means we should be exporting them at """"lsst.daf.butler"""" level, instead of requiring imports from """"lsst.daf.butler.core.utils"""", because the last two entries in that path should be considered internal daf_butler details."""
comments: "Three things going on in this ticket:  * I've moved NamedValueSet, NamedKeyDict, and IndexedTupleDict out of utils.py and into a new named.py, whose symbols are lifted and exported in lsst.daf.butler.  That's consistent with them being used downstream in pipe_base (previously that had to import from lsst.daf.butler.core, which we don't want).  * I've refactored those classes a bit - adding ABCs and switching from {{\_\_new\_\_}} to {{\_\_init\_\_}}, mostly - to make rigorous typing possible with them (they are a bit too cavalier about typing vs. Mapping/Set inheritance before).  * I've made numerous typing fixes to many other modules in core, to the point where the mypy config now lists the ones _not_ to run on in that subpackage.    There is a trivial PR for pipe_base as well as daf_butler, just to adapt to moving those containers." [SEP] "Looks great."

summary: "Remember which dataset types are in various collections"
description: """We may be able to optimize dataset lookups by remembering which dataset types are present in various collections.    This could happen down in daf_butler, for every collection, via some kind of materialized summary view of the dataset_collection_ tables (which could be managed either by Python code or by database support for materialized joins).  It could also happen by using CHAINED collections more as the """"user visible"""" collections created by the ingest and conversion scripts, because CHAINED collections already permit the dataset types looked up in child collection to be restricted.  The former is more work but seems cleaner long-term (there may be some potential for surprising behavior if we rely only on the latter), and we may want to do the latter as well anyway.    This would probably require schema changes, but probably only additions, and it may be doable in a way that allows the old schema and new schema to both be supported via configuration changes."""
comments: "I am taking a big step towards this on DM-27251, and I think I have a clear plan.  I hope to make the schema changes that would support this functionality (at least) on the DM-27033 integration branch prior to the middleware stable release, but I need to finish the real goal of DM-26692 first.    Because I'm now also planning to rememberthe ""governor"" dimensions of the data IDs in each collection, doing this ticket will then be a step towards DM-27153."

summary: "Add system for pre-fetching, caching, and in-memory joins of dimension information"
description: """DM-24734 revealed that repeated lookups for the same dimension records make up a significant fraction of both Gen3 RC2 bootstrap (~47% in Oracle) and QuantumGraph generation (17% in SQLite).  This could be at least mostly addressed by caching more of those lookups, but we need to define interfaces that allow user code to make it clear when caches can be expired, and it would be even better to be able to do bulk fetches of these records to populate those caches up-front.    At the same time, discussions with [~<USER> about DM-21904 have revealed a very similar use case for pre-fetching and then making simple relationship queries (e.g. """"give me the tracts related to this visit"""") against an in-memory data structure.    I have an idea for how to deal with both of these together, and I think I just need to work out the interfaces in more detail with Nate to ensure it will meet his needs."""
comments: "Current design sketch: https://gist.github.com/TallJimbo/1a2262743e5bfc34a8c1dd6100ea98d9" [SEP] "This has moved away from the original ticket description, in the direction of putting original query results into a temporary table so we can do the follow-up joins and other queries in the database without having to repeat the original big query.  The functional implementation is done, but it needs lots of documentation and commit-cleanup, and I need to check the test coverage (for the most part, the new functionality is covered by one mega-test I added at the end)." [SEP] "I've just finished the first profiles for QuantumGraph generation with this branch.  Let's start by recapping where we were when we started - basically the status at the end of DM-24734, but I've re-run those profiles against the w_2020_25 and am reporting those more recent results to make sure it's a fair comparison for this branch.    Test is building a single-tract quantum graph for the full DRP Gen3 pipeline, with the database loaded w

summary: "salobj test failures involving SALPY that look like delayed late joiner data"
description: """[~<USER> is seeing fairly consistent unit tests failures in ts_salobj on the Tucson test stand: this is only in the tests of communication between SALPY-or-salobj and SALPY-or-salobj. I have appended the log of the most recent failure, which is between SALPY and SALPY.    The way the test works is:  * Runs a simple Test controller/server (in this case using SALPY) as a background job. This controller listens for setLogLevel commands and every time it receives one it echoes the commanded log level using two topics: the {{logLevel}} event and the int0 field of the {{scalars}} telemetry topics. If the commanded level is 0, the controller then waits one second and exits (but that detail does not appear to be relevant to the failures).  * It runs a Test remote/client (again in this case using SALPY) that talks to the controller.  * It reads and checks the initial value of the logLevel event (20)  * It commands several other log levels (the final value being 0). Each time it commands a new log level it waits for the new value to show up in the logLevel event and scalars telemetry.  * The test fails after sending and successfully checking a value or two (so at least one non-20 values has been read). The remote reads a value of 20 when it expects some other value that it just commanded.    This strongly suggests that SALPY is somehow duplicating the initial logLevel sample.    My first though was that {{wait_for_historical_data}} was timing out before the data arrived (the test stand is a bigger environment than my computer and Jenkins so it may take longer for that data to arrive). As you know, if the timeout is too short then late joiner samples will show up after new samples have arrived. I'm not sure if that's a general flaw with DDS or just a misfeature/bug in OpenSplice, but it's a serious problem.    So I asked Adam to set LSST_DDS_HISTORYSYNC=300. But the test still fails (in fact the failure log below had that value of env variable set). So unless SALPY is ignoring that environment variable or the test is taking longer than 300 seconds to run, I think we can rule out that explanation.    Adam sees it almost every time on the Tucson test stand, so that seems like a good place to explore it. I also see it as an intermittent error in the Jenkins job (and I think more often than with SAL 4.0). I never see it on my Mac.    """
comments: "Can you take the Test/python/Test_EventLogger_logLevel.py , adjust it to print out the same data,   then run it in a terminal whilst your test is running ?" [SEP] "That's a good idea, but I'm not sure how to go about it on the Tucson test stand. I'm hoping you can figure that out.    Note that if you run the test with `pytest -s` the sender will print what it is sending (pytest doesn't capture stdout from the subprocess when the test fails)." [SEP] "DM-24932 has a tweak that provides a bit more output, including how long the reader waited for historical data. I plan to include that branch in salobj 5.12" [SEP] "Can you add a sleep immediately after the issueCommand_setLogLevel ?" [SEP] "Done on DM-24931. I also added printout of how long the topics take to create, which is a decent approximation (and upper limit) of the time spent waiting for historical data." summary: "Register aggregated topic schemas to a secondary schema registry"
description: """In the EFD, data replication is done in one direction, copying kafka topics and schemas from a source cluster (e.g. base) to a destination cluster (e.g.  LDF) (see the multi Data Center [active-passive setup | https://docs.confluent.io/current/schema-registry/multidc.html])    The Aggregator is a component that runs on the destination cluster (e.g. LDF) and it creates new schemas for the aggregated topics.  In the active-passive setup, these schemas will exist only at the destination cluster, we don't want them replicated back to the source cluster. That means the schema IDs would no longer be globally unique if we use the same Schema Registry to register the aggregated topic schemas, there may be collisions between schema IDs for schemas created at the source and destination clusters.     For the Aggregator, we'll need *a secondary schema registry* to register the aggregated topic schemas.     In this ticket, we'll investigate this possibility further and configure the {{kafka-aggregator}} to read schemas for the source topics from the primary Schema Registry and write schemas to the secondary one.    """
comments: "I'll avoid the primary/secondary Schema Registy nomenclature as this has a special meaning in a multi-datacenter deployment of Kafka.    From the point of view of the EFD Aggregator, I'm calling the second Schema Registry ""internal Schema Registry"" which is aligned with Faust nomenclature, ""internal topics"" are those managed by Faust and will have their schemas registered in the ""internal Schema Registry"".    I've used docker-compose to show that it is possible to have two Schema Registries running simultaneously and I came with the following docker-compose configuration:    The rationale for this configuration:   * [listeners|https://docs.confluent.io/current/schema-registry/installation/config.html#listeners]    {{SCHEMA_REGISTRY_LISTENERS: [http://0.0.0.0:28081|http://0.0.0.0:28081/]}} runs the {{internal-schema-registry}} on another port.   * [kafkastore.topic|https://docs.confluent.io/current/schema-registry/installation/config.html#kafkastore-topic]    We have to be careful and write the schemas

summary: "Allow Datastore.export and import_ to support disassembled datasets"
description: """In DM-24288 disassembly was moved into datastore. This means that a single dataset_id can now result in multiple files. Currently FileDataset is set up to allow multiple dataset_id per file but has no means for supporting multiple files per dataset_id. Furthermore, FileDataset has no knowledge of component and each component may need a different formatter.    I'm not entirely sure about the best approach to fix this so it needs a bit of thought and experimentation.    """

"""When we started to allow datasets to be disassembled this resulted in a single dataset_id corresdponding to multiple disassembled files. This breaks the paradigm for getUri which assumes a single file.    On this ticket we propose to replace getUri with getURIs that will return a tuple of the URI of the primary dataset and a dict mapping component name to component URIs.    For normal disassembly the primary dataset would be None. In the future for virtual datasets this may no longer be true and the primary dataset could be defined with URIs to override components also being returned.    Predicted file will still assume no disassembly."""
comments: "[~<USER> there are the bulk of the changes in daf_butler but I also had to tweak ctrl_mpexec since it used the old API." [SEP] "Adding clarifying statements from [~<USER> on the dm-middleware channel:  getURIs returns two things:   * if it's not a composite you will get a single URI and a dict that has nothing in it   * if it's a composite you will either get a single URI *or* that will be None and you'll get a dict with Dict[str, ButlerURI]   * In the future for virtual composites you might get a primary URI *and* some overrides in the components dict" [SEP] "A couple comments/questions.   Good to merge."

summary: "Create dataset class for processed bright star stamps"
description: """As part of bright star wings/extended PSF model (DM-23785), we will extract stamps around bright stars from calexps, apply some preprocessing, then save these (along with some metadata) to disk.    The reason this is needed is the code for DM-23785 will rely on coadding a very large number of small cut-outs around bright stars, from different calexps, together. These require some preprocessing steps, such as a normalization step, before being coadded. Since we cannot possibly store all stamps in memory at once, we cannot do this processing on the fly before coaddition, hence the choice to save them to disk.    Longer term, this choice will also enable us to change the way we build the extended PSF (aka bright star wing) models without having to redo the processing. As an example, we might (and likely will) come to realize we want a different extended PSF model for each CCD, or at least per some subregion of the full focal plane. If our initial run had all stars stacked together to build a single, constant model, we could reuse the processed stars saved at this stage to build the new models.    Regarding normalization of the stamps: while this will be carried out in the code for DM-23785, it is worth mentioning here. Since very bright stars are saturated, and otherwise contain various ghosts around their center, we cannot use usual (say, aperture) measurements of their flux. What we will do is instead integrate their flux in an annulus of constant radii around their center, far out enough that most ghosts are contained within the inner radius and do not impact this """"annularFlux"""" measurement. These values (one per star/stamp) are one of the things stored in the dataset introduced by this ticket. These only make sense in relation to one another if the annulus definitions used are the same; the class therefore contains checks that the same annulus radii (both inner and outer) are used, every time new stamps are added to the collection.       This ticket will add the class itself, and the associated info in obs packages.  """
comments: "Thanks for agreeing to review this, Nate!    As my first serious PR, I am afraid there is a chance this one will be ""not quick""."

summary: "Add accurate client IP addresses to Gafawlfawr logs"
description: """The client IPs shown in Gafaelfawr logs for the {{/auth}} endpoint currently point to the NGINX ingress pod instead of the actual client IP.    The problem is that the {{/auth}} request flow is route -> internal handler -> auth route. The internal handler passes the correct remote IP to the auth route, but because it adds another proxy step, the auth route itself sees the IP of the NGINX server as the request IP and sets a new {{X-Forwarded-For}} pointing to NGINX.    We need to reconfigure the ingress to not clear {{X-Forwarded-For}} and instead append to it, which in turn means that Gafaelfawr needs to know how far back to look in the {{X-Forwarded-For}} chain to know what the first external IP is (otherwise you end up trusting random {{X-Forwarded-For}} headers sent by a client, which isn't desirable).    That will affect everything, so all services behind that ingress will get the full {{X-Forwaded-For}} header. Hopefully that won't break anything else; anything that needs to trust that header will need to be configured with the deployment IP range so that it knows what the first external IP is."""

summary: "Improved error reporting in the REST services of the Replication System's Master Controller"
description: """There are a few problems with the current design of the HTTP modules (subclasses of {{lsst::qserv::replica::HttpModule}}):  * it's impossible to return an extended description of errors in addition to a simple string sent back to the services' requestors. It would be useful for many operations to let a client have more insight into origins of problems reported by some services.  * the general design for reporting both results and errors is unsafe, and it leaves a room for mistakes.    In the current model a module's implementation has to use the following mechanism for reporting results/errors:  {code:c++}  class HttpSomeModule: public HttpModule {  ...  protected:      virtual void executeImpl() override {          ...          if (error condition) {              sendError(""""error string"""");              return;          }          ....          json result = ... ;          sendData(result);          return;      }      ....  };  """

summary: "Create a semantic validator for Felis-format TAP_SCHEMA data"
description: """Providers of tables to the LSP-facing database(s) and TAP service(s) need to be able to provide the input to the TAP_SCHEMA metadata required for successful service of these tables.    In the DM design this input is provided in [Felis|https://felis.lsst.io] form.    Currently it's possible to validate that Felis files are _syntactically_ correct, but not that they contain appropriate information for use as TAP_SCHEMA.    The task here is to provide a tool that reads Felis files and applies some rules to give feedback on the semantic validity and usefulness of the information provided, e.g.:   * Are table_index values present, non-zero, and unique within a schema?   * Have a subset of columns been designated as """"principal""""?   * Are the """"description"""" fields non-empty?    The messages could be characterized by severity levels depending on whether they are purely cosmetic issues or ones likely to affect functionality."""

summary: "Update MTMount when Tekniker's code supports enabletracking"
description: """Tekniker's TMA code does not yet support enabling tracking for elevation and azimuth (though it does for the camera cable wrap). They plan to add that (and have even assigned command numbers for it).    At present the mock controller supports it (and requires that tracking be enabled to track), so there is a flag in MTMountCsc that controls whether this command is sent. Once Tekniker's TMA code supports it remove the test and always send the commands.    Until this is implemented it will not be possible to track when runing the mock controller standalone and having the CSC talk to that (though everything else will work). You can still run the CSC with the mock controller, but you have to run it internally, with the {{--simulate}} flag."""

summary: "Investigate alternatives for the Parquet Sink connector"
description: """One of the output formats required for the interoperability of the EFD and the LSP is Parquet. Parquet files have proven to be an efficient columnar data source for large datasets and also work with Dask distributed processing.    There are some other requirements for the Parquet Sink connector.   * We want to use Kafka Connect as for the other EFD connectors   * It must be open-source, if it is provided by Confluent it must be under the [Confluent Community Licence| https://www.confluent.io/confluent-community-license] since we are not paying for the Confluent Enterprise License.   * It must support time-based data partitioning, we want to split the EFD data by observing night.   * Must be able to read from Avro   * Should write to POSIX file system or to an object-store.     """
comments: "There are three Confluent connectors that write to Parquet format under the Confluent Community License.  The HDFS 2.0 Sink connector, the SFTP Sink Connector and the Amazon S3 Sink Connector.    Looking at the docs and discussing this internally with the team we plan on testing the Amazon S3 Sink connector. It can write to [Parquet format|https://docs.confluent.io/current/connect/kafka-connect-s3/index.html#s3-object-formats] and has options for [time base partitioning|https://docs.confluent.io/current/connect/kafka-connect-s3/index.html#partitioning-records-into-s3-objects].  "

summary: "Plan to replace the Confluent replicator connector and other enterprise components of Confluent Kafka"
description: """After negotiating pricing with Confluent the conclusion was that we cannot afford their enterprise license. The license is all-inclusive and it costs 10k per node/yr.      We are currently managing a minimum of 3 nodes * 6 instances of the EFD which means 180k/yr.  Also, they consider each component of the Confluent platform running on a different node, so we cannot deploy the Schema Registry and Zookeeper in the same node to reduce costs, for example.    The decision was to replace the Replicator connector with an opensource alternative, [Mirus| https://github.com/salesforce/mirus] seems like a good option for kafka replication, it also uses Kafka connect and is superior to Mirror Maker, see [this blog post| https://engineering.salesforce.com/open-sourcing-mirus-3ec2c8a38537].    The other enterprise product we were using is the Confluent Control center, its been very useful to monitor and diagnose the Kafka clusters.  An interesting alternative for the control center is the [Lenses dashboards| https://docs.lenses.io/1.1/using-lenses/user-guide/dashboard.html]    We plan on investigating these alternatives in the next epic for the EFD.    """
comments: "Plan for replacing the enterprise components of Confluent Kafka (3-4 weeks of work):   * Get familiar with Strimzi, read docs, k8s deployment broker + zookeeper : 2 days   * k8s deployment broker + Kafka Connect + Schema Registry: 2 days   * Make the InfluxDB Sink connector work with Strimzi Kafka connect, build a kafka connect image with the connector plugin, deploy and test: 2 days   * Refresh Kafka-connect-manager - hopefully, it will just work with Strimzi kafka connect, i.e. I expect it has the same API as the Confluent Kafka connect: 3 days   * Argo CD deployment: broker + zookeeper + kafka connect + kafka connect manager + schema registry: 2 days   * Investigate [Mirus|https://github.com/salesforce/mirus] open source alternative for the Replicator connector, add Mirus plugin to kafka connect and test with Strimzi:  4 days   * Add Mirus to Argo CD deployment and make a full replication test between two kafka clusters: 2 days     "

However, currently a query like:        returns only the last data point, because the previous data points don't have the tract information. This notebook demonstrate that using the aioinflux Python client to input the ""tract"" tag value for previous runs of the HSC RC2 reprocessing.    [https://github.com/lsst-sqre/squash-restful-api/pull/47]     "

description: """<USER>noticed that when dialect and driver are specified in the config but not so in the db-auth.yaml the match will not succeed.    Example, the db key in registry config:         db: """"oracle+cx_oracle://host""""     will not match db-auth.yaml entry         - url: oracle://host...     and vice-versa. Specifying the driver has an impact on functionality of the registry since it changes the functionality of the underlying database, but the driver has no direct impact on DB authentication. The same user should be able to connect with any appropriate driver they have available regardless of whether db-auth entry matches the full dialect+driver string or not.         I think it's most natural to allow, and preserve, specified driver in the butler.yaml, but ignore it in db-auth.yaml. I am wary of making it raise an error when it does, but that can be changed."""

summary: "Please add a method to Job to insert a measurement"
description: """At present when using the verify system one calls {{Job.metrics.insert(measurement)}} to add a measurement. If the measurement's metric was specified as a string there is no checking done to make sure the metric exists (and the MeasurementSet apparently has no way to perform such a check).    My suggestion to make this more robust: add a new {{Job.insert_measurement}} method that does the following:  * If the measurement's metric is a string then look up the metric (which *must* exist) and attach it to the measurement. Yes that modifies the measurement in place.  * Insert the measurement.    I am not sure what to do if the measurement already has a Metric. It might be simplest to check that a Metric by that name exists and raise if not. But that leaves the possibility that the Metrics don't match, so a more picky comparison might be safer."""

summary: "Add background offset tracking to fgcmcal and test on HSC RC2/PDR2"
description: """I have recently developed the ability to use aperture fluxes of stars to track background offset errors as a function of visit, position, and ccd coordinate.  This is a useful way of tracking background errors from the photometry (by looking at violations of the assumption that fluxes in larger apertures should be a purely multiplicative correction from fluxes in smaller apertures).  This allows a way to look at the background at the locations of stars, rather than the typical way of looking at the backgrounds away from stars.    This ticket describes the implementation of this code from {{fgcm}} and into {{fgcmcal}}, as well as example tests on HSC RC2 and PDR2 data."""
comments: "Given a star at a location on the sky, as you measure its flux at larger and larger apertures (the curve-of-growth if you plot the whole curve) then the flux in a larger aperture should simply be a multiple of the flux in the smaller aperture.  This multiplicative constant is a form of aperture correction (from one aperture to another, not to infinity, and not relative to the psf flux which has a very small effective aperture).  Luckily, the aperture correction is roughly constant across a visit (there are variations, but much smaller than the variations from visit-to-visit which depend on the large-scale wings of the PSF).  Therefore, over a visit one would expect that statistically, the as a function of flux level (magnitude) the delta-mag between two largish apertures should be constant, because this multiplicative constant (in flux) yields a constant delta in magnitude.  However, if there is a systematic _background offset_, then this delta-mag will not be constant, and will instead curve down (backgroun

This ticket is a master-ticket to contain multiple tickets related to this work."""

summary: "Understand scientific changes to ap_verify CI as of early May 2020"
description: """In early May 2020, we saw significant changes to our ap_verify runs tracked in Chronograf — the total number of DIAObjects per CCD, and the ratio of DIASources to direct image sources both jumped significantly. This is expected to be due to the decorrelation afterburner updates which happened around this time (DM-24371). Dig in to the data and see if you can confirm that."""
comments: "It looks like the changes in the DIA object counts we saw in Chronograph at the ap-pipe meeting on 5/4/2020 are scientifically valid changes caused by implementing the decorrelation afterburner.         I've attached a few of the relevant plots of DIA object distribution to this ticket, and you can see that the large drop in total DIA objects is due to bad sources along the edge of CCD 56 that are no longer detected. The number of good DIA objects actually increased slightly, and there is no obviously artificial pattern in their distribution. The difference images for each CCD both before the change and after can be seen in the notebook (linked below), as well as the difference of the difference images to highlight the changes.         https://github.com/lsst-dm/ap_pipe-notebooks/blob/tickets/DM-24730/DM-24730-May-AP-verify-changes.ipynb" [SEP] "_Very_ nice. summary: "Investigate poor scarlet performance on crowded fields"
description: """The Rubin Observatory LSST-VTS Blending Task Force has attempted to run scarlet on the globular cluster NGC 6569 to test it's effectiveness on crowded fields. The attached image shows that things did not go so well at all, but possibly because of very poor object detection and/or a poorly modeled PSF. This ticket is to investigate the source of the error and at least ensure that scarlet works as well on this field as can be expected on a first pass without detections on the residuals, which is left for a future scarlet ticket."""
comments: "Below are some general comments about what I found during my analysis. I also created a [notebook|https://github.com/<USER>notebooks/blob/master/ngc_6569.ipynb] that details these findings and does show improved results when running scarlet on data cleaned as described below, although still not ideal.    1. The model generated above didn't have the DECam mask files or variance files, so in some sense their failure isn't that unexpected. The masks and variances are essential in order to model the i and z bands because so many sources are saturated. Without weight maps the saturated pixels dominate the likelihood (since they have the most signal), which goes against scarlet's assumption that an object has the same shape in all bands.    2. The large number of undetected sources has a significant impact on the robustness of the model. In the linked notebook I was able to retrieve about detect the sources using SEP (python version of SExtractor) but it still left a large number of undetected sources. This especi

summary: "Cleanup leftover conda indexes/caches (pkgs directory) for smaller container images"
description: """Moving more packages to conda has meant a larger footprint of the images due to conda image sizes    Conda uses the pkgs directory (effectively """"$(dirname $(dirname $CONDA_EXE))/pkgs"""") as both a cache and a place to download binaries.     {{conda clean -t}} cleans up the tarballs, which is a good first step. The tarballs can unequivocally be cleaned up - they shouldn't really be needed ever again.    {{conda clean -a}} goes a step further and removes some extra things but retains the files for their hard links.    The entire pkgs directory could be wiped out to save 1.5GB, but installing additional environments on top of that environment may end up costing more disk space (no more hardlinks). In the context of a docker container, this might not be likely to happen - I don't believe it's possible to even create your own conda environment on nublado due to permissions, though I might be wrong. summary: "Extend the Replication/Ingest framework and REST API with algorithms and tools for indexing catalogs"
description: """The current implementation of the Replication/Ingest doesn't provide any mechanism for managing (inspecting, creating or dropping) indexes on the data tables within Qserv. Only a few essential (for Qserv to function) indexes get automatically created. A lack of indexes may affect a performance of many user queries. Hence a goal of this development is to add the following capabilities to the system:  * extending the Replication Framework with a set of _request_ and _job_ classes for managing indexes at workers.  * adding a command-line version of the Replication _controller_ tool for managing the indexes  * extending the REST services of the *Master Replication Controller* with the index management services    Other notes:  * the REST services need to be protected with the _auth_key_ mechanism as explained in: https://jira.lsstcorp.org/browse/DM-22745  * it may be necessary to consider putting a cap on the number of indexes which were created by the system due to disk space constraints at a particular Qserv deployment. For example, the system may need to evaluate the amount of the available disk space on the coresponding file system (at Qserv workers) before attempting to create a new index.  """

summary: "Replace confluent_kafka with a python Kafka library that is more robust for alert-stream-simulator"
description: """The `confluent_kafka` library is currently used in alert-stream-simulator for producing to Kafka. It turns out that this library will never actually raise errors of any kind on connection problems. It can be given a timeout, but it will timeout silently with no message. This is not configurable.    There are other ergonomic problems; it is configured only by passing in an enormous dictionary and can be very hard to debug since it is written in C.    I think we should find a better backend library. Some options:   - [kafka-python|https://kafka-python.readthedocs.io/en/master/]   - [aiokafka|https://github.com/aio-libs/aiokafka]   - [pykafka|https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka]"""

summary: "Determine if the Python MTMount CSC can keep up with telemetry from the low level controller"
description: """Figure out if the new Python MTMount CSC could keep up with telemetry from the low level controller: read the data, parse it and output it as SAL while tracking.    Test at the current rate of 20Hz tracking updates and 20Hz telemetry output per topic.  If that works, try speeding things up the telemetry and tracking rates until the system fails, to get a sense of how close to the edge we will be.."""
comments: "Experimental pull request: https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_MTMount/pull/10    Overview    MTMount has 33 telemetry topics with a total of 1026 fields. Assuming each field is double precision (most are) that is 8208 bytes of telemetry in its most efficient form, before serialization. DDS messages are likely sent using binary, but the data must be serialized to be sent from the MCS to the CSC and at present all messages are ASCII, so that is what I tested. If we assume each field is formatted as xxx.xxxx... to full precision, I find that an array of 1026 random doubles is serialized as approximately 20,000 bytes of ASCII text.    When I talk about telemetry rates below this refers to sending all telemetry data (33 topics, 1026 fields). So a telemetry rate of 20Hz is 164,000 bytes/second in its most efficient form, and probably closer to 400,000 bytes/second as ASCII     Test Results    For these first tests: I published telemetry as json (to avoid \n) and made the fields sent by the mock controller identical to

description: """The registry refactor to delegate to polymorphic helper classes should allow us to make many different kinds of changes to the Registry implementation that affect the database schema without breaking our ability to read old repos with new code.  Actually making that happen requires a number of smaller changes, and there are many more that would further improve our backwards compatibility situation:   * copying (and effectively freezing) the Registry config subtrees for dimensions and manager classes into per-repo configs, instead of always pulling defaults from daf_butler;   * starting to increment the version number in the dimensions config;   * recording the dimensions version in the database somewhere, so we can easily check for consistency and raise more helpful exceptions when the config is inconsistent with the database;   * recording the manager classes used in the database (for the same reason), and perhaps adding version numbers for these as well;   * adding dimension version numbers or some other form of stability to the byte-encoded form of DimensionGraph   * recording the names of dynamic tables in the static tables that manage them to reduce reliance on any particular name format   * making it so Manager classes that add foreign key fields can use compound keys, further encapsulating DDL schemas inside the managers.    Note that this ticket is about reducing the need for schema migration (or at least giving us more flexibility about when any particular repo is migrated).  We'll still need to have some way to do actual schema migrations to upgrade old repos when they can't just be thrown away."""
comments: "I started working on this bullet:  {quote}making it so Manager classes that add foreign key fields can use compound keys, further encapsulating DDL schemas inside the managers.  {quote}  on DM-24613 (where I thought it might save work overall, with DM-21794 on the horizon).  After that attempt, I think it's not actually helpful for schema stability and is a distraction from more pressing work.  I've put what I've done so far on the tickets/DM-24660 branch, and I'll revisit what to do with it when I actually get to this ticket."

summary: "Ingest w_2020_14 Object tables of HSC-RC2 into Qserv"
description: """Take the parquet files at  {{/datasets/hsc/repo/rerun/RC/w_2020_14/\*/deepCoadd-results/merged/\*/\*parq}}   as inputs, go through the workflow and ingest into Qserv."""
comments: "Inputs:      " [SEP] "As a prototype, I coded the workflow from the input parquet files to before the data loading into    -github.com/hsinfang/qserv-ingest-hsc-poc- [https://github.com/lsst-dm/qserv-ingest-hsc-poc]   and used it to ingest the inputs into the {{hsc_rc2_w_2020_14_00}} database on qserv -int.    This workflow can be run on Verification Cluster with relatively little manual intervention. It includes steps of rewriting the parquets without nulls and with casted data types, converting them into csv using parquet_tools pq2csv, editing the csv files to get rid of True/False/inf/-inf, partitioning them into chunk files using sph-partition, creating the database and table, starting a super-transaction, and registering the chunks.    Then the outputs of the last jobs have the data-loading commands, which I can use on qserv-master to do the data-loading step. The data-loading is done on qserv-master for now so to use the most recent docker images there. _The data-loading, super-transaction committing,

comments: "Over the last couple of weeks there has been significant effort to look at how `PipelineTask` classes can be used to execute the broad design goals presented in [DMTN-141|https://DMTN-141.lsst.io].  This has required implementing three different tasks:  1) A task to create matched catalogs that can be used by other measurement tasks  2) The actual measurement task  3) And a task to aggregate the individual measurements into a single summary number    My contribution has been in the task to produce matched catalogs and in the very simple pipeline description.    The current pipeline runs in about 1 minute on the r-band visits in the {{ci_hsc_gen3}} repository on a single core.  This can be parallelized and has been shown to run in about 20 seconds on eight cores.    The quantum graph builder identifies 25 patches as quanta for the tasks (the measurements are at the patch level for this prototype).  Though only 9 patches have data since the quantum building is conservative in terms of a boundary around in put

description: """This follows the pattern used for other Registry systems.  After this change, we could consider giving Datastore instances these classes and a manager for the opaque tables instead of a full Registry, would be better for interface segregation. summary: "Assign the OFC state0 in ts_MTAOS by Configuration File "
description: """Assign the OFC (Optical Feedback Control) state0 in ts_MTAOS by configuration file. The *ConfigurableCsc* class in ts_salobj is the basic CSC (Commendable SAL Component) class for the subsystem such as *MtaosCsc* in ts_MTAOS to use. The configuration file is located at MTAOS directory of ts_config_mttcs. The following document explains the details of this: [TSTN-017|https://tstn-017.lsst.io/]. When writing the configuration file, a schema is needed to verify the configuration file based on: [Write a CSC|https://ts-salobj.lsst.io/salobj_cscs.html#writing-a-csc].    OFC is the component that calculates the correction of mirror bending mode and hexapod position. The control algorithm in use can follow [Real time wavefront control system for the Large Synoptic Survey Telescope (LSST)|https://drive.google.com/file/d/1RyMQWeXWRZLtxM8KkSwtB3STzMM9fCvX/view?usp=sharing] and [Control Algorithm in Optical Feedback Control|https://confluence.lsstcorp.org/display/LTS/Control+Algorithm+in+Optical+Feedback+Control]. The state0 (*s*~*0*~ in the [Control Algorithm in Optical Feedback Control|https://confluence.lsstcorp.org/display/LTS/Control+Algorithm+in+Optical+Feedback+Control]) is an intentional parameter used in *x00* reference, which is explained in the above links.    The AOS software contains the simulation code and control code. For the simulation, the user needs to use the repos: ts_wep, ts_ofc, and ts_phosim. For the control code, the user needs to use the repos: ts_wep, ts_ofc, and ts_MTAOS. The ts_phosim is not needed in the control and the ts_MTAOS is not needed in the simulation. In this task, we focus on the control code.    The followings are the steps in this task:   # Update the [default.yaml|https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_config_mttcs/blob/develop/MTAOS/v1/default.yaml] in ts_config_mttcs to contain the state0 information as the following: [state0inDof.yaml|https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_ofc/blob/master/policy/comcam/state0inDof.yaml]. I prefer to have two yaml files (default.yaml + state0inDof.yaml) instead of one (default.yaml) in ts_config_mttcs. However, we need to check ts_salobj can support this or not.   # Provide the related schema: [MTAOS.yaml|https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_MTAOS/blob/master/schema/MTAOS.yaml] in ts_MTAOS/schema directory. Prefer to have two files instead of one. But this depends on the implementation of ts_salobj.   # Update *MtaosCsc* class in ts_MTAOS to read the configuration of state0. *MtaosCsc.configure()* will read the configuration file from SAL. *ConfigByObj* and *ConfigByFile* classes inherit from *ConfigDefault* to read the data. We need to update the related test cases to make sure the *MtaosCsc* can change the state0 by SAL or file.   # Override the default state0 when *OFCCalculation* is instantiated by *OFCCalculationFactory.getCalculator()* in *Model.ofc*. The state0 is stored in the internal data of *Model.ofc.ztaac.optCtrl*. The developer can do *OFCCalculation.getZtaac()* to get the *ztaac* attribute. After this, the developer can use *ZTAAC.setState0()* to set the state0 values. The related *get()* functions are provided for the test. By default, the state0 is set by *OFCCalculation()* --> *OFCCalculation._configZTAAC()* --> *ZTAAC.setState0FromFile()*. It is noted that we will keep the default state0 file in ts_ofc for the simulation (the AOS simulation does not know the ts_MTAOS. The UW team is using the AOS simulation to help the algorithm development)   # Update the test cases and [versionHistory.rst|https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_MTAOS/blob/master/doc/versionHistory.rst] to reflect the change."""
comments: "Just to summe it up:    Make sure state0 (read by OFC routines) is read from configuration file in ts_MTAOS    Making schema would be a bit tricky, as OFC doesn't use a schema - it converts dict loaded from configuration file into nparray, assuming all values are listed in order. I started to create a complex schema description, but has to drop that in favour of a linear schema describing config as used in OFC." [SEP] "This is the proposed schema. I believe I will be able to parse it and transfer it into array inside ofc modifiing OptCtrlDataDecorator accepting second (optional) argument with an array describing order of elements to transfer to array, and default value. That will remove disambiguity caused by the fact order doesn't matter in yaml, so nothing will prevent you to provide DOF file with dX,dY,dZ instead of expected dZ,dX,dY,..         [^telescopedof.yaml]" [SEP] "Please see PR in ts_ofc and ts_MTAOS:    https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_ofc/pull/23    [https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_MTAOS/pull/25]

summary: "Create Faust Records dynamically from Avro schemas"
description: """In {{kafka-aggregator}}, Faust agents aggregate the data stream on tumbling windows of fixed size (see DM-24403). Faust Records describe the data structure for the aggregated topics and with {{faust-avro}} we can serialize that to Avro.    Generally, a field representing a value in the source topic will have the corresponding aggregation fields e.g. min, mean, median, and max in the destination topic.     In DM-24485 we demonstrated that we can parse a Faust Record into an Avro schema with {{faust-avro}} which allows us to register schemas for the aggregated topics in the Schema Registry.    However, we still need to create the Faust Record for the aggregated topic in the first place.    The goal for this ticket is to demonstrate we can create the Faust Record classes dynamically, otherwise, we would have to pre-generate a large amount of static code to create Faust Records for each schema we have in the EFD."""
comments: "Using the {{make_record()}} to create Faust Records classes dynamically seems to be working fine. Surprisingly {{kafkafkaaggregator models}} and {{kafkaaggregator model MODEL}} commands are still working:     !Screen Shot 2020-05-14 at 11.50.56 AM.png|thumbnail! " [SEP] "The {{kafkaaggregator register}} command used to register the corresponding Avro schema also works with dynamic records:     !Screen Shot 2020-05-14 at 11.54.56 AM.png|thumbnail!     Note that thanks to {{faust-avro}}, Avro schemas are created from Faust Records for free.       " [SEP] "Initial implementation of the {{Field}}, {{Topic}}, and {{Aggregator}} classes." summary: "www.lsst.io: Search from homepage search box takes user to advanced search page"
description: """We'd like for the homepage of [www.lsst.io,|http://www.lsst.io,/] the Rubin search site, to have a prominent search box where the user can enter a query. The search will take the user to the Advanced search page where results are shown and the user can make further refinements.    This functionality implies that the Advanced Search page's search state should be reflected in its URL. That way the homepage search can link to the advanced search page with the search terms in its parameters."""
comments: "This PR accomplishes two key things:   # Visually, it adds a {{HeroSearchForm}} to the homepage. The user can type into that box and hit enter: then we use Gatsby's {{navigate}} function to navigate to the advanced search page ({{/search}}) with the query string as a parameter ({{search?query=user query string}}).     # To make that possible, we've reflected the search state into the URL. This way we can link to a search (i.e. from the hero search form). It also lets users ""save"" a search by bookmarking it. The basic recipe for reflecting search state into the URL is from [https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/going-further/routing-urls/react/#basic-urls] To debounce the state update, I ended up using this hook: [https://usehooks.com/useDebounce/]"

In DM-24241 we added a {{contentCategories.lvl0}} and \{\{lvl1}} key that classifies documents in the hierarchy.    Level """"0"""" is """"Documents"""" and levels 1 are document series (e.g. LDM, DMTN)."""

"""DM-22962 introduced a walltime argument to BatchArgumentParser so that BatchCmdLineTask can be run with a total runtime instead of a nebulous time per core per target. This has the unintended side effect of allowing CmdLineTasks tasks with zero targets to run with --walltime, whereas running with --time will cause batchWallTime to return zero and make PBS/Slurm job generation fail. This ticket is to ensure that both --time and --walltime have the same behaviour in corner cases."""
comments: "Note that non-PBS/Slurm jobs (i.e. local SMP) still run with zero walltime because the preamble function does nothing, whereas PBS/Slurm's preamble gives the slightly misleading error message ""Non-positive walltime: %s (did you forget '--time')"".    I think a reasonable behaviour would be to fail in all cases if there are zero targets, which could be accomplished by adding a minTargets argument to BatchArgumentParser with a default of 1 (in case someone really wants to run on zero targets for whatever reason). I'm not sure if this is trivial to implement in every subclass of BatchCmdLineTask, though."

description: """Make sure that the DCS can send configuration parameters to the lower level components."""
comments: "I implemented a basic function to send configuration parameters to the lower level components. The function checks the configuration parameter values against the (hard coded) upper limits for those parameters. It also converts any quantity to radians if necessary since the lower level components expect radians while the configuration parameters are commanded in degrees. If a parameter value is too large or a parameter is unknown, a SAL AckError is thrown.    The function is written based on the assumption that the configuration parameters are provided in a dictionary form which directly can be passed on to the lower level components (apart from unit conversions). This assumption may not be true and will be checked at a later moment." [SEP] "Thanks for the review, [~<USER>."

description: """There have recently been discussions regarding {{Defects}} where new defects are appended to the existing list of defects. This works fine but can result in a situation where lots of tiny bounding boxes could be represented by a few large bounding boxes. Many things become more efficient if the minimal set of bounding boxes is used by downstream processing.    This ticket will add a normalize method to {{Defects}} that will recalculate the defect bounding boxes. One implementation could be to generate a mask from the defects and then recalculate the defects from the mask. This would have the side effect of allowing direct comparison of two different Defects instances."""

Internally you wouldn't need the readFits and then you would replace the internal list of bounding boxes. You do have to decide whether this is a mutable method  or something that returns a new Defects object to the caller." summary: "Try to speed up astropy.time UTC->TAI for scalars"
description: """Based on my timing tests astropy.time is 300 to 400 times slower than my custom code when converting scalar unix UTC time to TAI. Profile the code and see if there is some way to improve this to the point that I could astropy's code for lsst.ts.salobj.tai_from_utc, instead of my custom code."""
comments: "I profiled astropy (and will attach the results) and found no obvious bottleneck -- at least nothing that would get performance into the range where I could use astropy.time to implement lsst.ts.salobj.tai_from_utc (and retain good DDS message reading and writing performance as measured by tests/test_speed.py).    I also timed astropy.time on vectors and found it a bit faster than my code. So the problem is indeed in the way astropy handles scalars.    Finally I tried calling the appropriate ERFA function: astropy._erfa.utctai directly (after being assured that the {{_erfa}} interface was stable, and despite requests, there was no immediate sign of an official public interface, so plenty of people have been using it ""as is""). I found even that was 6.6x slower than my code -- enough to cause a significant speed reduction in tests/test_speed.py (~4400 messages/second instead of ~5500).    I concluded that using astropy.time is not feasible for this purpose and that I could see no way to improve it to the poi

summary: "Incorrect scan rating being assigne to 'SELECT *' queries with no where clause."
description: """'SELECT * FROM table'  queries (lacking a WHERE clause) get a scan rating of 0 and are not flagged as interactive queries. While they should not be interactive, they should have a scan rating higher than 0 that is based on the largest table in the FROM clause.  The rating of 0 keeps the scans from being put on the appropriate shared scan on the workers. 'SELECT COUNT( * )' without a where clause is a special case for testing purposes that is interactive with a scan rating of 0."""

summary: "Define and document ""address space"" for origin IDs"
description: """DM-21794 will split the dataset primary key into a compound key containing and autoincrement field and an """"origin"""" integer that represents _either_ (depending on the dataset type):  * the data repository or (in the future) data repository layer in which the dataset originated (e.g. DBB Chile vs. US endpoint, Bob's personal data repo layer vs. Alice's personal data repo layer in a multi-user multi-layer registry);  * for raws (and perhaps one or two other dataset types), an ID that corresponds to the instrument that produced the dataset, enabling a mangling of the exposure+detector IDs to be used instead of the autoincrement value, making the IDs for these datasets globally consistent.    To make this work, we need to decide how to assign those origin IDs, and that involves some careful thought about how to split up the address space (I'm assuming this will be another uint64, so we've got plenty of space) for both known entities, those we can partially predict (""""we will have per-user layers, but how many?"""") and those we can't predict at all (i.e. just reserve blocks of integers for later use).    We also need to decide how to manage that address space going forward:    - what is the source of truth (in the source code?  In a LDM document?)    - what is the procedure for using making use of address space we've reserved?   - how do we assign addresses to instruments/obs* packages?    I'm expecting the outcome of this ticket to be a document (DMTN or confluence).  If the proposal is to do a lot of it this in code or configuration that lives with code, prototyping that in daf_butler or another git repo would be welcome, even if it doesn't do anything yet."""
comments: "This has come up again in the context of Data Preview 0 in the sense that there is a possibility of the IDF and IN2P3 processing a subset of the DC2 data and we'd presumably like the raw data to share the same dataset_ids (invariant during ingest) for when we try to put the independent output runs back into a single combined registry. Or am I misunderstanding the point of this ticket.    For raw data isn't ""origin"" effectively the instrument name and the exposure_id the unique part that is invariant?  " [SEP] "I don't think this is hard at all for raw data alone. That is probably as simple as determining how to map each instrument name to an integer.    But if we want to use origin values to differentiate users in a multi-user system, we need to decide which origin values are reserved for instrument raws, and which ones are reserved for users, and that seems like something we should at least think a little bit about before assigning some origin values to instruments."

summary: "Please make implicit Linearizer state changes explicit"
description: """Calling {{Linearizer.applyLinearity(detector)}} mutates the state of an “unpopulated” {{Linearizer}} so that it becomes specific to the detector that it was just handed.    This seems intrinsically surprising — why should calling {{applyLinearity}} change the state of my {{Linearizer}}?    It also leaves us open to bizarre situations like the Butler being used to fetch a linearizer for a particular detector, but the linearizer inadvertently being applied to a different detector... which then makes it _invalid_ for use with the detector it was fetched for.    My suggestion is that (as a minimum) {{Linearizer.applyLinearity}} should not call {{self.fromDetector}}; rather, all initialization that cannot be done in {{Linearizer.\_\_init\_\_}} should be done explicitly by the caller.    Stretch goal: when calling {{butler.get(""""linearizer"""", ccdnum=N)}} (or equivalent), the returned {{Linearizer}} should be already populated for the requested detector, and should not require the calling code to do any more work than simply {{apply}}‍ing it. (This is currently an issue in obs_subaru; I've not checked other cameras.)"""

summary: "Support rafts with multiple different detector types"
description: """As implemented, the camera description in {{obs_lsst}} assumes that any raft has detectors that share a common electronic layout.  This is convenient for all the science rafts, but breaks for the corner rafts where the guider and wavefront chips have different geometries.  This forces us to split the the corner physical rafts into two """"virtual"""" rafts, one with the guiders and one with the wavefront sensor chips.    There was a bug related to parsing the special wavefront raft introduced in DM-22605.  I fixed this in DM-24450 and added tests, so hopefully that will keep this from cropping up.  However, when investigating that I noticed that per raft types is not imposed by yaml camera in general, but is specific to how we represent cameras in {{obs_lsst}}.    It would be nice to be able to refer to every raft in the descriptions as the camera team do in headers.  This would remove one layer of translation where mistakes can be introduced.  I've had a quick look at what this would take and per raft detector types is baked into our raft descriptions at the lowest level so that would need some thought."""

summary: "salgenerator operation order can delete required assets"
description: """The salgenerator can delete necessary assets if done in the wrong order.   * *salgenerator <CSC> sal idl* will delete or overwrite certain C++ artifacts.  This results in *sal <CSC> python* build failures   * if two CSCs have similar roots (e.g. ATDome and ATDomeTrajectory), the second CSC build can completely wipe out the first.    Also, *salgenerator <CSC> idl* is not the correct format, but is still allowed.  It should cause an error instead of executing a build."""
comments: "Note to QA: When this is fixed, switch back the order of the IDL and C++ build steps in the salgenerator suites, this will work to validate the fix." [SEP] "Fixed in ts_sal PR #67 https://github.com/lsst-ts/ts_sal/pull/67" [SEP] "The IDL step, by design, runs the Validate steps which clears out the CSC parent directory.  Therefore, it must be run before the language build steps." [SEP] "Running $ salgenerator <CSC> idl now returns an error instead of incorrectly running build steps,    It tells the user they used the wrong command line and the process is correctly invoked by  " [SEP] "The last issue, regarding similarly named CSCs is still a problem.  I created a new ticket, DM-24619, to track that specific issue, so this one could be closed." [SEP] "The IDL step needs to be run before the language build steps, and now gives an error if invoked incorrectly.  The remaining issue, regarding similarly named CSCs now has a separate ticket."

description: """The ATAOS has a set of commands that enable users to set offsets on the LUTs. These commands returns before the offset where actually applied to the components which creates some operational issues. This ticket is to make sure the commands only returns once the offsets where taken or the command is rejected if the offset is not allowed.     We will also implement a command that allow users to specify a value for a axis. The ATAOS will then compute the appropriate offset that needs to be applied on the LUT. """
comments: "Offsets can be sent when the corrections are enabled, and when it's disabled (in which case they get applied when the corrections are re-enabled.   In the case where the loop is enabled, then the command could just return when the offsets are applied.    However, what about in the case where the correction is disabled? " [SEP] "i don't understand what change this ticket is asking for. Changing LUT values is very different than commanding a move. Is it just ATAOS that needs changes?" [SEP] "I think that it the correction is disable the command should be rejected.          [~<USER>, this is to apply offsets to the LUT values. " [SEP] "This change is being incorporated into DM-25150, reviewer should look at PRs on that ticket." [SEP] "If this is being implemented in a different ticket, then let's mark this one as invalid and make it ""is mitigated by"" the parent ticket."
