summary: "Investigate solutions to enabled Avro in a Faust application"
description: """There are at least two packages of interest in PyPI to enable Avro in Faust applications.    I first looked at the [python-schema-registry-client|https://github.com/marcosschroh/python-schema-registry-client] package which implements a synchronous Confluent Schema Registry client and a [codec for Faust|https://github.com/marcosschroh/python-schema-registry-client/blob/master/schema_registry/serializers/faust_serializer.py].    See more about Faust codecs and using different serializers in the [Faust documentation|https://faust.readthedocs.io/en/latest/userguide/models.html#codecs].    The other package out there is [faust-avro|https://github.com/masterysystems/faust-avro] which also implements an Avro codec and Confluent Schema Registry support for Faust.    Looking at the code {{faust-avro}} seems more attractive for its hability to [parse a Faust record into an Avro schema |https://github.com/masterysystems/faust-avro/blob/master/faust_avro/parsers/faust.py] I think that's an important feature missing in the {{python-schema-registry-client}} which assumes the Avro schema is provided in addition to the Faust record (see [here|https://github.com/marcosschroh/python-schema-registry-client/blob/master/schema_registry/serializers/faust_serializer.py#L32]).    In addition, the {{faust-avro}} implementation is asyncio based. A drawback, however, is that it subclasses the Faust base classes like {{faust.app.App}}, {{faust.serializers.codec.Codec}}, {{faust.serializers.schemas.Schemas}}, {{faust.models.record.Record}}, {{faust.types.topic.Topic}}, etc but that also makes using it much easier as we show in this ticket.    Perhaps a hybrid solution would be to use only the parsers implemented in {{faust-avro}} and our own library {{kafkit}} that already implements an async schema registry client.    I think that implies rewriting [faust_avro/serializers.py|https://github.com/masterysystems/faust-avro/blob/master/faust_avro/serializers.py] using kafkit as a [codec for Faust|https://github.com/marcosschroh/python-schema-registry-client/blob/master/schema_registry/serializers/faust_serializer.py] similar to what [python-schema-registry-client|https://github.com/marcosschroh/python-schema-registry-client/blob/master/schema_registry/serializers/faust_serializer.py] does.  However, I don't know if that's possible without changing the {{faust-avro}} source code."""
comments: "[~<USER> this is a very simple PR that shows how to convert the test topic aggregation example to use Avro serialized messages with [faust-avro|https://github.com/masterysystems/faust-avro].    This option is attractive because it worked out of the box, so I think I'll continue using {{faust-avro}} and test it with real-life topics from SAL.    [https://github.com/lsst-sqre/kafka-aggregator/pull/2]" [SEP] "[~<USER> I've added one more commit to the PR, it is ready to review now. " [SEP] "Comments on the PR. I'm not totally sold on faust-avro for the reasons you specify and the fact that faust-avro is pretty new and made by just one organization. That said, it works so it seems good to use it to get going." [SEP] "Thanks for the suggestions!"

description: """On DM-15536, I discovered that the {{pipeline}} target in the ci_hsc_gen3 SConstruct file only depends on the {{raws}} target (and since that ticket, the {{visits}} target), but it should depend on the {{externals}} target as well.  I think this leaves correct execution up to whether SCons happens to schedule things in the right order.  I tried a few (failed) approaches to fixing this quickly that I'll document here:  - Just adding the dependency creates a cycle (or so SCons thinks), because some of the external files get dropped into the output collection/directory (!).  - Not telling SCons about the external files that get put into the output directory prevents {{scons --clean}} from deleting them.  - Moving those files out of the output directory by changing their entries in the YAML file is no good, as that will cause this to break next time we run the script that recreates the YAML file from ci_hsc_gen2 outputs.  I think the right fix has to involve moving those files to a different location in ci_hsc_gen2 as well."""

summary: "Handling mismatches in collections of chunks when ingesting director and dependent tables into Qserv"
description: """h1. Objectives    Both fixes were triggered by: https://jira.lsstcorp.org/browse/DM-24396    h2. Missing chunk contributions    In Qserv all partitioned tables of the same catalog (database) are expected to have _exactly_ the same set of _chunks_. The current implementation of the Replication/Ingest system _generally_ supports this requirement. This is enforced at the final stage of the ingest workflows - when _publishing_ catalogs. Unfortunately, this measure is not sufficient to sanitize user input at earlier stages where the problem could be properly addressed and missing chunks could be easily (and correctly) compensated by the *Ingest* system.  h3. Solution #1  From a technical point, a solution would be to create _chunk_ tables for all _partitioned_ tables each time a new contribution is received by the system for _any_ partitioned table. In this model any chunk touched by a user will result in a full set of all _partitioned_ tables created for that chunk, regardless if some chunks for some tables will be still empty after publishing a catalog.  h3. Solution #2  Unfortunately, the proposed solution only works if a collection of chunks for the subsequently loaded tables is a superset of the ones from the previous loading stages. Otherwise, chunks in the non-empty intersect would still be missing in Qserv. Hence the second solution implemented in the ticket was to add a _fix up_ stage run by the database _publishing_ algorithm to create missing tables. This would ensure all missing chunks of all relevant tables would be created.  h2. Fixing a bug in the {{CSS}} configurations of the _dependent_ tables  As it was reported in https://jira.lsstcorp.org/browse/DM-24396 Qserv fails to process queries involving dependent tables. The problem has been found in an an implementation of an algorithm populating the Qserv's {{CSS}} for the _dependent_ tables.   h2. Other minor fixes and improvements  * reinforced transaction management not to leave """"dead"""" (unnoticed by user's workflow) transaction in case if any problems occurs  during transaction creation  * reinforcing database publishing service to prevent the operation if it's too early to do so  *   """
comments: "Me reviewed. {{reviewers.pop(""<USER>")}}"

description: """DM-22138 broke matchedVisitMetricsTask by neglecting to rename brightSnr to brightSnrMin, which is holding up RC2 reprocessing (DM-24359)."""
comments: "See also DM-24457 for a ticket to add a test to prevent future undetected regressions.    At first I thought the solution would be as simple as finishing replacing brightSnr with brightSnrMin. However, as in DM-22138, the two model_error (astrom/phot) methods were not correctly inheriting the brightSnr parameter and just using the default value of 100. In this case I decided to inherit the value correctly as I don't see a compelling reason to have separate brightSnrMin/Max values for these metrics. This will have the unfortunate side effect of effectively changing the default to 50, but I don't see a way around that.    If someone were to add separate brightSnr cuts for the error models, there would also need to be checks to ensure that they are consistent (i.e. a subset of) the range used to build the matched dataset in the first place." description: """Currently scarlet uses autograd to calculated the gradients used for optimization. This creates an overhead in both memory and runtime, as autograd requires a wrapper for all functions used in the code in order to back-propagate the gradients for each variable.    For scarlet we can calculate almost all of those gradients ourselves, eliminating the need to use autograd. The attached notebook demonstrates how this can be done, giving an outline of the runtime differences between autograd and direct gradients using a simplified modeling algorithm with a single back-propagation. It also demonstrates that gradients can be back-propagated up to 10 times faster per iteration when using gradients that we calculate ourselves, where the exact saving is based on the ratio of the PSF to the full size image (which translates into how much time we are spending on gradient calculation vs convolutions).    DM-23788 implements convolutions in real space, resulting in a model that allows us to calculate all of the gradients directly ourselves. However, it also demonstrates that real space convolutions are slower than those performed in k-space, so we are likely to use fft convolutions in production. We could in principle still use autograd to calculate the FFT convolution gradients for us, however since we only need the gradients for that single operation it will likely be easier to just implement a direct gradient for the FFTs as well (which may be as simple as calling a function in autograd)."""
comments: "I attached  [^direct_gradients.pdf] to give a (very rough) overview of the algorithm and where the expected runtime savings come from." [SEP] "Hi Peter,  Could you please review this ticket for direct gradients?    BTW: you are also the reviewer for DM-23788, which is still in review. Those changes are now included on this branch, so once we've finalized the review would mind also closing that one?    Thank you,  -Fred" [SEP] "A minor clarification. We don't get rid of autograd, we simply tell is how to compute gradients because we know most of these operations exactly. The reason for not giving up on autograd is that it allows us to include models for which we don't know the gradients analytically. It's a lot easier for the user to create a new source model this way.    Even if we give up on autograd, the concept of optimized custom gradient functions is supported by all autodiff implementations I know of." description: """Compute pixel covariance matrices on coadds to check for non-diagonal terms.         This will confirm the hypothesis that some of the noise variance bleeds into covariance when coadding.         Empirically, a scaling  of the variance by a factor of ~1.1-1.2 (with variations with field and filter) was found necessary; these values should be recoverable from the covariance matrices.         This can later be used to check noise correlation (or lack thereof) induced by different coaddition schemes (Kaiser coadds), and whether we ultimately want to propagate some parts of the covariance matrix.              ([https://nbviewer.jupyter.org/github/MorganSchmitz/NoiseCovarianceExploratoryNotebooks/tree/master/] has some early notebooks)"""

summary: "Use obs and camera overrides in make_apdb.py"
description: """{{make_apdb.py}} takes an {{ApPipeConfig}} and uses it to configure the database. At present, it loads config files requested by the user from the command-line, but does not try to load observatory or camera-specific overrides for the data going into the APDB. This caused {{ap_verify}} to run afoul of DM-24435, and may also lead to inconsistent behavior if we ever want to configure the APDB in a per-camera manner (column names for special filters, for example?).    Modify {{make_apdb.py}} to search for and load {{ap_pipe}} override files. This requires that {{make_apdb.py}} know what camera it is working with. I see two options:  * -provide an input Butler repository, and infer the obs package and camera from the mapper. This has the advantage of common syntax with {{ap_pipe.py}}.-  * explicitly name the obs-package and camera on the command line, e.g. {{""""obs_subaru/hsc""""}}. This has the advantage of being less confusing for the user, who might otherwise expect that the _location_ of the repository is somehow relevant."""
comments: "On second thought, an input Butler repository would be a bad idea, since it will make it harder to use {{make_apdb.py}} with Gen 3 before DM-22663. Explicit camera string it is." [SEP] "It turns out that the way {{ConfigFileAction}} and {{ConfigValueAction}} work is very hard to reconcile with also providing the camera through command-line arguments ({{CmdLineTask}} does it by manually hacking the {{input}} command-line argument before passing it to the ""real"" parser).    There are a couple of ways around it in {{make_apdb}}, but all would require a lot of rewriting of Gen 2 code. Given the so far hypothetical benefits, I think it's best to put this capability off until DM-22663, and in the meantime work around DM-24435 by simply not freezing the config."

summary: "Freezing a config locks the registry(ies) of other instances of that config"
description: """Config field instances that correspond to a {{RegistryField}} have an internal member, {{_field}}, pointing back to the original field. {{_field}} points to the same object for all config instances corresponding to the same config. This causes a rare bug where calling {{ConfigChoiceField.freeze}} locks the registry for all config instances using that {{RegistryField}}, including instances that did not exist at the time of the call. See my [post on #dm|https://lsstc.slack.com/archives/C2JPL2DGD/p1586539537027000] for a specific example.    Please change the code so that independently created config instances no longer affect each other. This issue appears to be a side effect of the fix for DM-17757, and may be difficult to fix without introducing still other side effects."""
comments: "I wonder if the solution isn't to, on freezing the config, copy the {{RegistryField}}/{{ConfigChoiceField}} _instead of_ calling its {{freeze()}} method. You'd still have the benefits of frozen registries, but they'd be decoupled from the original, publicly visible {{RegistryField}}." [SEP] "I pushed a pex config branch, can you see if that works for you? If so I will clean it up a bit." [SEP] "Yes, {{ap_verify}} runs and produces the same output." [SEP] "I just want to verify the sign of your comment. This does fix your initial issue, without your work around on ap_pipe?" [SEP] "Yes." [SEP] "A few style comments on the PR.  "

comments: "Deprecating the begin_<command> methods turns out to be impractical because the basic do_<command> methods do useful checking that would be difficult to obtain if subclasses had to override these methods.    However, the end_<command> methods are less important, because the checking has been done by then. Still, if one overrides do_<command> to obtain end-of-state-transition behavior then it is crucial to call super().do_<command> first, or that checking will not be performed. To avoid the risk of getting this wrong it is safer to leave the end_<command> methods in place."

summary: "Add abstractions for spatial indexing to daf.butler.registry.Database"
description: """Our current approach to spatial indexing on the sphere is simply to store the HTM IDs each region overlaps at a particular level, in an extra """"common skypix overlap"""" table for every table that has a region column.  This might be an okay approach for regions of a certain size, but it's definitely not a good approach for regions of arbitrary size, and probably not close to the best approach even for its ideal region size.  Doing better would probably require using special index types built for spatial overlap queries (R trees and variants thereof).  While the databases of interest to us (SQLite, Oracle, and PostgreSQL) do not support indexing regions on the sphere natively, they all do provide ways to index 3-d bounding boxes, and the lsst.sphgeom objects we work with provide ways to get 3-d bounding boxes (for a unit sphere).  The interfaces for the three databases are quite different, however, so the big challenge is probably encapsulating that functionality behind a consistent interface for the rest of our Registry code to use.  We might be able to leverage SQLAlchemy hooks for some of this customization, but I doubt they will cover everything.  While Oracle and PostgreSQL both treat geometries as column types and spatial indexes much like normal indexes, they have different syntax for inserting, querying, and relating them.  SQLite is even more different: its R tree implementation involves creating a special table for every spatial column, in which lower and upper bounds behave like regular (but separate) columms.  Adding at least some of the abstraction to our Database class might be a lot easier, and no less usable, though at present it doesn't do much with SELECT queries (they're just passed through to SQLAlchemy directly).  And I don't think this functionality needs to be totally transparent - just database-generic.  It would be totally reasonable for Registry code that's using our Database interface to work with a spatial column to have to do things it wouldn't have to do with a normal column, like manually extracting bounding boxes from regions for inserts, calling Database methods to get SQLAlchemy expressions that represent overlaps in query join/where clauses, or doing post-query filtering in Python (as we do now).  This ticket is for adding that abstraction layer to some combination of SQLAlchemy and the Database class.  I'll create another one for actually using it in Registry (maybe after the design for this one is further along). """
comments: "[Conversations on slack|https://lsstc.slack.com/archives/C2JPT1KB7/p1586444134256900] revealed that my initial investigation missed some built-in and extension database functionality we should at least investigate first before diving into this scheme.    The most obvious one is that Oracle Spatial [_does_ support expressing geometries on the sphere natively |https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E11882_01/appdev.112/e11830/sdo_cs_concepts.htm#SPATL616] and [~<USER> has a contact at NCSA who has used that successfully in astronomy.  We should check that this includes indexing spatial regions for overlap queries and joins (not just point-in-region queries) before diving in, but if it does, it's probably the way to go there.  There's a slight chance that the Oracle spherical stuff is actually slower than just using the Oracle 3-d stuff directly, because it can also do ellipsoids and heights (a lot of sophistication we _don't_ need), but hopefully the code path that needs spherical but doesn't need those things is exercise

summary: "Investigate Faust windowing feature for data aggregation"
description: """In this ticket we implement an example to demonstrate the Faust [windowing|https://faust.readthedocs.io/en/latest/userguide/tables.html] feature that can be used for aggregation of kafka streams.    The general idea is that you can use a Faust agent to process the stream of events  (messages) from a given Kafka topic and add those messages to a Faust table for persistence. The table is configured as a tumbling window. The window configuration parameters are a size and an expiration time. When the window expires a callback function is called to process the messages in that window. That's when the aggregation happens and a new Kafka topic is produced with the aggregated values.    A docker compose config is provided to start a local Kafka cluster to run the example. The instructions to start the Kafka cluster, the Faust worker and the producer are described in the README.  The docker compose also includes the Confluent Control Center which is handy to inspect the topics, their configurations and the consumer lag for the Faust consumers when running the application.    NOTE: running multiple Faust workers to distribute the application load currently crashes the application, this will be investigated in DM-24473.  """
comments: "See PR https://github.com/lsst-sqre/kafka-aggregator/pull/1" [SEP] "Hey [~<USER> I would appreciate very much your review to help to evaluate if we are on the right path in developing the kafka-aggregator based on Faust." [SEP] "This looks great. I've written up some of my thoughts in that PR and specifically this concluding note: [https://github.com/lsst-sqre/kafka-aggregator/pull/1#pullrequestreview-395677689]    I think we should continue down this path. The real test will be metaprogramming the agents and their aggregation functions to based on schema data alone so that we're not explicitly coding up agents for each SAL topic.    I'm also wondering about how we can align the windows based on the wallclock so that all aggregated streams have the same alignment so that we can do SQL joins conveniently." [SEP] "Thank you [~<USER> for the review and for the insightful comments. I've implemented the suggestions in the PR and opened DM-24528 to investigate the window alignment further.    "

summary: "Handle ""curated"" datasets more generically in Gen 2 ap_verify"

The only way to ingest them in Gen 2 appears to be to call a command-line script, [installTransmissionCurves.py|https://github.com/lsst/obs_subaru/blob/master/bin.src/installTransmissionCurves.py]. It's not obvious how to incorporate this into {{DatasetIngestTask}}, given that it's supposed to be instrument-agnostic.    Find a way to handle transmission curves effectively in Gen 2 without breaking support for other instruments like DECam.    (Transmission curves will not be an issue in Gen 3 if we provide all calibs through a pre-ingested repository, and if not, we can use the {{obs.base.Instrument.writeCuratedCalibrations}} method to ingest them generically.)"""

summary: "HSC defects_visit table has unexpected filter column"
description: """While working on DM-24259, I performed automated Gen 2 ingest of an HSC dataset in the following order:  # Call {{obs.subaru.ingest.HscIngestTask}} on the raws, with the {{obs_subaru}} config overrides  # Call {{pipe.tasks.ingestCalibs.IngestCalibsTask}} on the calibs, with the {{obs_subaru}} config overrides  # Call {{lsst.pipe.tasks.ingestCuratedCalibs.IngestCuratedCalibsTask}} on the defects in {{obs_subaru_data}}, with the {{obs_subaru}} config overrides    This led to the following error on defects ingest:      Investigation revealed that the table had columns for date and filter, but the {{obs_subaru}} config for {{IngestCuratedCalibsTask}} expected the {{defects_visit}} table to only have a date column. [~<USER> speculates that the table was created during calib ingest (which is configured to have its {{*_visit}} tables be (date, filter)). However, if this is the case then it's not clear how ingestion passes when building {{obs_subaru}}; there are no instrument-specific config files for ingestion.    Given that defects should not be filter-dependent, this appears to be a bug in HSC ingestion. Investigate the issue to confirm the cause, and fix. One plausible option is to create the defects-related registry tables only when ingesting defects, not calibs."""
comments: "I was under the impression that the obs_x_data packages force filter to be present. I was a bit surprised by this when I was looking at the Linearizer code that also required it. ingestCuratedCalibs is forcing filter to default to NONE for all curated calibs, possibly because ingestCalibs also forces filter to be used.  If you look at bias entries in a calibration registry you'll also see that filter is present (always set to NONE)." [SEP] "So are you saying that https://github.com/lsst/obs_subaru/blob/cbbde586f25690534e76e3fd2bcdc4ed751fbffb/config/ingestCuratedCalibs.py#L14 should be changed to {{['calibDate', 'filter']}}?" [SEP] "I have never looked at this before but it's likely that since ingestCalibs is doing this and since we are creating a filter for defects just like for bias, then it's possible that we have to be closer to the ingestCalibs.py config here." [SEP] "Does this all mean that obs_subaru has never been switched over to obs_subaru_data?"

summary: "Measure Local-Sky Oversubtraction in HSC PDR1/PDR2/Current Data"
description: """In this notebook we have used statistics generated from sky objects to quantify and qualify sky over-subtraction and under-subtraction within Tract 9697. We performed three custom runs of coaddDriver to re-create HSC-SSP equivalent PDR1, PDR2 and PDR3 data using the current DM stack software. We further performed three custom runs of multiBandDriver using these data, extracting unmasked circular aperture fluxes within radii of 9 pixels and 250 pixels. Sky objects are pseudo-objects placed in regions where no other detections were found, and therefore should act as a measure of sky estimation success or failure across our dataset. Much of the analysis performed here leads on directly from efforts described in DM-23641.    We find significant variations in the sky estimation outputs employed over the course of HSC-SSP processing. PDR1 and PDR3 data visually exhibit significant sky-oversubtraction around bright sources, or in the vicinity of clusters of bright sources. These are likely caused by source flux or 'flux bridges' between bright sources being inadequetly identified at the source detection step, and included in a subsequent definition of a background sky map. Sky oversubtraction surrounding bright sources in PDR1 data gave rise to the term 'divot': the negative halo of flux surrounding bright sources. The radial extent of the divot in PDR1 lies further away from any given bright source than the equivalent divots in PDR3 data.    In PDR2 data, the divot issue appears largely to have been resolved, with minimal evidence for sky oversubtraction around bright sources. However, PDR2 data exhibits a much larger variation in background sky flux in the field, evidenced by sky oversubtraction in the least densely populated areas within this tract. The cause of this remains unclear, yet is likely linked to CCD and imaging artefacts (e.g., ghosts, ghoulies) compromising sky estimation efforts.    In summary, we find that all three datasets exhibit evidence of sky over-subtraction, either in densely populated regions (PDR3), sparsely populated regions (PDR2), or both (PDR1). The varying sky estimation routines employed here and their impact upon these performance metrics should be carefully considered when generating further improvements to our sky estimation routines in the future.    The attached notebook {{skycomp.ipynb}} contains all analysis and code. A PDF version of this notebook ({{skycomp.pdf}}) is also provided. The {{scripts.tar.gz}} file contains stack processing scripts and associated configuration files. All patch postage stamps may be viewed at this link: https://lsst-web.ncsa.illinois.edu/~<USER>skycomp/plots/0009697/"""
comments: "Thank you for reviewing this Dan. The notebook details most of our analyses of sky over-/under-subtraction in PDR1/2/3 data using sky objects generated from three custom runs of coaddDriver/multiBandDriver. Keen to hear your thoughts. " [SEP] "Looks good. It's nice to have the plots for all of the patches. The minor suggestions I have:  - a colorbar would be nice, both to explicitly show which colour is zero and clarify the units  - a clarification on the point about PDR3 looking better visually but oversubtracting all sources; that seems fair, but at the same time it looks like PDR1/2 both had extended positive and negative regions, whereas at least the bias in PDR3 has a consistent sign (if not magnitude)  - point to an example or two of flux bridges in PDR1 and 2 (ideally positive and negative if they both exist). I'm not entirely convinced that there's a systematic there rather than just cases of 'positive interference', for lack of a better term." [SEP] "Thanks for the review. I've made a few modificati

summary: "Store instrument class with gen3 instrument registration and add API"
description: """When designing the butler command line tools it is clear that life would be much simpler if an instrument could be referred to by name rather than by instrument class. It should only be instrument registration that requires the full class name. All other operations should work by specifying the name, looking up the name in registry and retrieving the class name, using doImport on that class, and instantiating the class for downstream usage.    For this to work we need two changes:    # Add the instrument class full name text string to registry.  # Have an API ({{butler.registry.getInstrument}}?) that will return an instantiated gen3 Instrument (they don't need parameters) given the name of the instrument.    """

summary: "afw should warn when FITS headers case may be modified (cfitsio > 3380)"
description: """When using newer versions of CFITSIO (> 3380 aka 3.38), keys written by cfitsio via fits_write_record / ffprec are upper cased. description: """AP pipeline want to have order of visit processing processing to be stable, in particular association steps for one visit needs to wait until the same step for previous visit is finished. Adding this sort of dependency for quantum graph would be difficult. It may be easier to implement special scheduling logic while executing the graph instead. I want to explore how this can be done in current pipetask and try to make it scheduling more configurable/extensible. Some refactoring (that we always wanted to do) of the internals will probably be needed."""
comments: "Another approach to this would be to have a custom script for this test package that creates a custom QuantumGraph, which could include the fact that (e.g.) the second visit's association job depends on the outputs of the first visit's association job.  That's still not quite possible without changes to the core code, because our QuantumGraph data structure assumes there are no dependencies between nodes corresponding to the same task label.    It's possible that the concrete association PipelineTask doesn't map well to that representation, of course - the problem here is that there is data flowing through the PPDB here that's not captured (for good reason, as we've discussed in the past!) in the QuantumGraph, and that blows up our execution and provenance model.    But even if that solution doesn't work, I think it's worth stepping back and thinking about this as a provenance problem as well as an execution-order problem, because I'm sure the provenance problem is going to come back to bite us if we don't.

summary: "Add relative symbolic link transfer mode to Gen 3 ingest"
description: """When converting gen 2 to gen 3 repositories, the current options are in-place conversion, hard links, or absolute symbolic links. There are cases (e.g., Git repositories) where links are desirable but neither hard links nor absolute paths are portable enough. A """"relsymlink"""" mode would be useful for these cases.    Because it's intended only for when very specific behavior is desired, """"relsymlink"""" probably does not make sense as an option in either the """"auto"""" or """"link"""" transfer modes."""

summary: "Please simplify the way ts_sal finds PYTHON includes"
description: """Presently ts_sal salgenerator appears to find Python.h by using {{/include/python$PYTHON_BUILD_VERSION}}. This is a bit of a problem for several reasons:  * It assumes that /include exists  * It is a bit confusing as it's not clear what PYTHON_BUILD_VERSION is being used for.    I suggest offering a new env variable PYTHON_INCLUDE (or similar) that is the full path to the directory containing Python.h. I think it would be much clearer what the env variable was used for.    You could fall back to PYTHON_BUILD_VERSION if PYTHON_INCLUDE was not defined, for backwards compatibility.    Two more small requests to consider (in an attempt to rationalize the env vars):  * Rename LSST_SDK_INSTALL to something a bit clearer. It appears to be the root directory of ts_sal, so perhaps TS_SAL_ROOT? If you do that then do you need SAL_HOME? It is a known subdirectory of ts_sal.  * Move your bin scripts (such as salgenerator) to the bin/ directory. That's what it's there for. Then we just have to get ts_sal/bin on our PATH and they can be found. The current location is surprising and deeply buried. At that point you can probably lose SAL_DIR."""

With these changes the 2to3 conversion can use """"auto"""" mode and that should do the right thing most of the time."""

description: """The scriptQueue has now been used on sky and is beginning to prove useful. It was also the first time we tried to use it in an operational setting and it provided insight into how to make it better.     This ticket is to rename the scriptQueue to Queue, and to rename scripts as """"Jobs""""    See comment for explanation.    jobsQueue was also suggested as one could imagine there will be other Queue's that might cause confusion.     """
comments: "One issue that constantly arose was how overloaded the word ""script"" is in the community. It's generally considered something that's often ""quick"" to make and does not adhere to any strict format. It also is often used for data reduction etc. It became confusing very quickly when someone would say, ""We can script that."" what one was referring to.     Renaming the scriptQueue to ""Queue"" and scripts to ""Jobs"" alleviates this confusion. The Queue does so much more than run unstructured scripts. It (along with salobj) provides the basis for the job to be written, command formatting, logging, passing meta-data to promote clarity, checkpoints, and providing more elegant interruption solutions than Ctrl+C! ""Jobs"" can then also be better defined to differentiate between production jobs (e.g. standard visit) and one-off's (e.g. try some weird test), which will come with different requirements in terms of required testing etc."

summary: "Add support for the MySQL-based empty chunk lists when ingesting new catalogs into Qserv"
description: """The current implementation of the Ingest System only supports the file-based *{{empty chunks lists}}*. Each catalog has a dedicated file. The files are located on a file system which is required to be seen (in the *{{read-only}}* mode) by each master node of a Qserv setup. This could be either a local or a network-based (NFS, GPFS, etc.) filesystem. In order to manage (populate, update, remove) the list the *{{Master Replication Controller}}* (providing the metadata services for the Qserv Catalogs Ingest workflows) is required to be run on a node which has the {{read-write}} access to a folder where the list are located. Depending on a setup of Qserv, it could be the same node where Qserv {{czar}} is setup, or be some other node which has a direct access to the folder. The current approach is have the empty file lists be deployed locally on each Qserv master node. The other issue is related to the multi-master extension of Qserv which is soon going to be deployed in production ({{lsp-int}}, {{lsp-prod}}) at {{NCSA}}. In such setup two (or as many as many Qserv master were configured - one per each Qserv {{czar}}) copies of the empty chunk list would have to be managed. The above explained ingest-lists """"affinity"""" requirement also complicates (makes it less flexible) the {{Kubernetes}}-based deployment scenarios for Qserv and its Replication/Ingest system. At the mean time the most recent versions of Qserv already support the lists as MySQL tables accessible by Qserv {{czar}} services. Potentially this allows to decouple a location of the lists from the hosts where Qserv `czar` and Replication/Ingest controllers run.    Hence a goal of this development is to add support for the empty chunk lists as MySQL tables. The REST API of the Ingest service needs to be extended with an option for choosing a desired persistent representation (a file system or a table) for the lists. The default value of the option should be the MySQL table. This could be changed if needed by passing an optional parameter to the service via the REST API.    The API extension will affect the following services:  ||method||service||role||  |{{POST}}|{{/ingest/chunk/empty}}|create or update an empty chunk list|  |{{PUT}}|{{/ingest/database/:name}}|publish a catalog (which also includes creating an empty chunk list)|"""
comments: "[~<USER> reviewed the proposed modifications and approved them in the PR."

We should have integration tests which spin up a Kafka cluster, try producing and consuming, and make sure everything works round-trip.    These tests should work on Linux. Getting isolated docker networking right on OSX can be pretty hard, so I think we should skip it; developers on OSX can use VirtualBox or we can add a GitHub workflow to trigger integration tests."""

summary: "Prototype a confluent schema registry instead of building our own registry"
description: """Today, we manage schema versions by committing them in the sample-avro-alert repository, and we expect them to be on the local filesystem of the producer or consumer. We load all schemas into memory using a custom SchemaRegistry class in sample-avro-alert's Python utility code. alert_stream's code relies on this.    This setup probably won't survive in production. It depends too much on synchronizing consumers and producers by managing their local state - we'd have to send out an email telling people to please refresh files on their machines in order to take advantage of a schema update.    In addition, our SchemaRegistry implementation is somewhat buggy: it doesn't canonicalize schemas, so minor whitespace and documentation changes would be breaking (this is DM-24293).    One alternative seems to be the [Confluent Schema Registry|https://www.confluent.io/confluent-schema-registry/]. This is in use today at LSST by SQuARE; the kafkit project provides access to the schema registry API directly, and the confluent_kafka consumer/producer library supports transparent ser/de if a schema registry is available. It would handle canonicalization for us, as well as fingerprinting of schemas.    Output of this work would be a working set of Docker containers that runs a Schema Registry alongside a Kafka broker, and Python code that produces and consumes the alert stream using that data. A good demo would be to show an update to the schema. Output should also include a DMTN summarizing the work and the result."""

summary: "Investigate Kafka compression of the alert stream"
description: """Kafka has support for compression schemes on a topic. Doing things very naively (ie, calling `gzip.compress` on a serialized alert stream packet) suggests that plain gzip compression can achieve a roughly 3:1 compression ratio.     Kafka supports snappy, gzip, and LZ4, and reserves a byte for communicating about compression of messages in a stream. We should investigate the CPU and memory cost of doing this on the producer and consumer, as well as the compression ratios we achieve when passing through a larger stream, since compression operates on blocks.    The output of this should be a brief DMTN."""

summary: "Set up React/Gatsby project for www.lsst.io search site front-end"
description: """The purpose of this ticket is to start work on the front-end for the reboot of [www.lsst.io|http://www.lsst.io/] by initializing a React/Gatsby.js app. We believe that Gatsby is the most appropriate front-end framework because it renders static pages that will deploy well on LSST the Docs. Gatsby will also let us create curated documentation features by using GitHub as a CMS/collaboration platform. React works well with the Algolia Instantsearch UI framework (Algolia is the software-as-a-service that the Ook app, see DM-24241, is populating with searchable records). Specific goals for this ticket are:   * Startup the Gatsby project   * Add code linting and formatting hooks   * Set up CSS build pipeline (global styles + styled-components?)   * Set up GitHub Actions-based CI.   * Create a demo page that integrates with Instantsearch.js"""
comments: "* Set up Gatsby site   * Added linting and formatting pre-commit hooks   * Added GitHub Actions-based CI   * Configured Styled Components for laying out the design within React components   * Set up layout for an Advanced Search page (there will be other search pages in the future)   * Integrated with the Algolia Instantsearch React library   * Designed result cards so that they hide details. summary: "Convert alert schemas into canonical form before fingerprinting them"
description: """The Avro spec [describes a """"canonical form""""|https://avro.apache.org/docs/1.9.2/spec.html#Parsing+Canonical+Form+for+Schemas] for schemas which should be used for fingerprinting. It does stuff like normalize whitespace and attribute order. The `SchemaRegistry` does a sort of fingerprinting today, but doesn't do any normalization, which means that it can't correctly handle minor changes to schemas like changes to their documentation.    This is probably pretty low priority. I'm not sure it will ever affect users... until it does, probably in very mysterious ways. It's a correctness thing."""

summary: "Prototype datastore changes to support dataset disassembly/assembly"
description: """After some discussion we have decided that it might be better for datastore to be in charge of assembly/disassembly of datasets. Currently registry creates 7 rows per raw with almost none of those components being relevant. We did it this way to allow pipelines to specify components directly. We very rarely want to do this though and it would be more efficient in database storage to have a single row for a dataset in registry and move [dis]assembly to the datastore layer. Then we can instead write a single row if we do not disassemble and multiple rows if we do disassemble (and no row at all for components that were not present).  This is similar to the original implementation of datastore.    This ticket is to check that the approach is feasible and also whether it can be implemented independently of registry to allow us to experiment with both approaches in parallel."""
comments: "While refactoring Registry code, I think I can restate this question from the ticket description:    bq. whether it can be implemented independently of registry    I think the ideal impact on Registry is that these changes make it so we never need to call {{insertDatasets}} with {{recursive=True}} (and hence can eventually remove that option), but we can still call {{insertDatasets}} explicitly on each component dataset and later call {{attachComponents}} to connect them to their parents.    It may also make sense to add logic to {{findDataset}} to - when given a component dataset type - look first for an ID for the component itself, and then fall back to the parent ID and somehow communicate that back to Butler.  It may be able to do that more efficiently than Butler could itself, but I think Butler could otherwise also do that fallback logic, and probably should at first." [SEP] "[~<USER> all tests are now passing with disassembly moved to datastore. Vestigial code still exists in butler itself for doing d

description: """By convention, the Python scripts in {{bin.src}} delegate all processing to code located in the {{python}} directory. A discussion of the manifold reasons for doing so can be found in [#dm-middleware|https://lsstc.slack.com/archives/C2JPT1KB7/p1585698155334100].    While this script organization is assumed by the [argparse topic type|https://developer.lsst.io/stack/argparse-script-topic-type.html], our developer guide does not actually say that scripts should be organized in this way. Add a section to the Python Style Guide explaining the convention and its rationale.    Since this would formalize a convention that already exists in the Stack, I believe there would not be serious objections and that no RFC is necessary."""

summary: "Add document describing how to create executable scripts to the dev guide"
description: """There currently is no document in the developer guide that describes how to create an executable script following our """"code in `python/script/`, nearly-empty executable in bin.src"""" convention. We have a [community post|https://community.lsst.org/t/new-tools-for-documenting-ad-hoc-scripts-in-the-lsst-science-pipelines/3784] with links describing how to document such a script, but no central location for someone writing a new script.    I'm not sure if this should go under `Python` or `DM Stack` in the dev guide."""
comments: "So it doesn't get lost, there's a discussion of all the advantages of the current convention on [#dm-middleware|https://lsstc.slack.com/archives/C2JPT1KB7/p1585698155334100]." [SEP] "   {quote}I'm not sure if this should go under `Python` or `DM Stack` in the dev guide.  {quote}  I'd vote for ""DM Stack"" since I think the solution will end up being deeply integrated with how the Stack (like Science Pipelines) is architected.    Other DM Python code, that uses setuptools for instance, would use a different way of writing executable scripts."

Write a design document which describes what this will actually consist of. That should probably be formatted as a DMTN (but you can also use Confluence if you find that easier/more convenient)."""
comments: "I happen to have some text describing the functionality in more detail:    A stand-alone alert-stream simulator will be developed. This simulator will be provided to those planning to propose to build community brokers, and will be run by them on their hardware. This simulator will produce a realistic (in terms of alert contents, alert format, and distribution mechanism) stream of alerts. Specifically, the alert stream simulator must:    Be capable of generating realistic streams of LSST transient alerts, at data rates expected of LSST.    Input   A static archive of alerts serialized as files.  A prescription for the rate and frequency which they should be provided to the alert stream.    Output  A stream of alerts that can be consumed locally or on other systems.    Adhere to the technical design of the LSST Alert Distribution System, as described in DMTN-093 and other design documentation provided by the LSST Project. This includes alert serialization using the Apache Avro format in a manner consistent wi

description: """As proposed in DMTN-082 the LSP users are generally interested in telemetry data at a frequency closer to the cadence of the observations.     The original proposal is that “all telemetry topics sampled with a frequency higher than 1Hz are (1) downsampled at 1Hz and (2) aggregated to 1Hz using general statistics like min, max, mean, median stdev”.     Commands and event topics should not be aggregated as they are typically low-frequency and can be read directly from the raw EFD data sources.    Another aspect of the aggregation is to resample the telemetry topics in a regular time grid to make it easier to correlate them.    The aggregator will produce a new set of aggregated telemetry topics in Kafka that can be consumed by a connector and stored in a specific format. In another ticket we'll use the parquet data format to store the aggregated topics.     In this ticket, we'll investigate and evaluate [Faust|https://faust.readthedocs.io/en/latest/], a Python asyncio stream processing library. Faust supports Avro serialization and multiple instances of a Faust worker can be started independently to distribute stream processing across nodes or CPU cores.    """
comments: "This ticket accounts for investigation work before adopting Faust. As a deliverable I would point to the aggregation example in the [kafka-aggregator repository|https://github.com/lsst-sqre/kafka-aggregator].     See also the README in that repo for preliminary documentation about the use of Faust in this project."

summary: "Rework API for providing data IDs to test butlers"
description: """Currently, the code for creating a [test Butler|https://pipelines.lsst.io/v/daily/modules/lsst.daf.butler/use-in-tests.html] requires that the user pass all data IDs at repository initialization time. This is both inconsistent with and much less flexible than the API for creating new datasets, making it awkward to create more than a single-ID repository.    The main advantage of the current API is that the user does not need to know which fields are mandatory for defining e.g. a Detector entry (some are obvious, like """"instrument"""", others are not, like """"full_name""""). The new API should also handle such fields automatically where possible."""

summary: "Run ap_pipe on HSC non-G COSMOS medium dataset"
description: """As DM-23430, but for all other bands."""
comments: "Status update! I have gotten mini-reruns to process pretty successfully for all filters now that DM-25746 is sorted. ""Pretty successfully"" means that ~80% of the dataIds processed without crashing. For the 5 filters x 3 visits x 103 CCDs = 1545 dataIds I tried, there were 325 failures, and they were pretty evenly distributed among all filters.    Most of those failures - 243 of them (75%) - are due to imageDifferenceTask falling over when it couldn't find a coadd PhotoCalib (which I think just means it can't find a coadd spatial match for a template at all, given that the previous thing it logs is e.g. ""apPipe.differencer.getTemplate WARN: deepCoadd_sub, tract=10055, patch=8 does not exist"").    The remaining errors are a smattering of things:    * 48 failures when processCcd's characterizeImageTask fails to measure the PSF (InvalidParameterError)   * 16 failures near the end of ap_association because the template point is outside the exposure box (i.e. DM-25262) (IndexError & KeyError)   * 5 failures w

summary: "Generate template coadds for HSC RC2 non-G bands"
description: """As DM-23429, but for all bands (not just G)."""
comments: "I used coaddDriver.py to create direct coadd templates for the COSMOS field, tract 9813, in HSC's R2, I2, Z, and Y bands. I followed a similar procedure as used for the HSC-G templates in DM-23429. For the Z and Y bands, the templates were from the RC2 dataset. However, the RC2 dataset has R and I filters rather than the newer R2 and I2 filters. For consistency, after brief discussion on Slack in #hsc-subaru, I decided to use some of the first non-RC2 visits (PDR2 instead, which will be the source of science images) for the R2 and I2 filters. All of the visits chosen had nominally good seeing (psfSigma always < 2.5 pixels, and more typically < 2.2 pixels). These are the visits used    *HSC-R2*   91554^91556^91558^91560^91560^91564^96850^96888^96936    *HSC-I2*   90770^90772^90774^90776^90778^90780^90782^90784    *HSC-Z*   17928^17908^17906^17926^17904^17902^17930^17962^17946^17944    *HSC-Y*   22602^22604^22644^22632^22628^22642^22626^22630^22664^11730^22662^22606^11736^22646^22660^11718^11732    The results

summary: "Improve startup speed of fgcmcal and add checkpointing for restarts"
description: """The recent large runs of {{fgcmcal}} on HSC data have shown that (a) in certain use cases the startup time is a factor of a few slower than it could/should be, and (b) certain files can be checkpointed to allow faster restart in case of transient file system failures, out of memory issues, etc.  I have a branch {{/u/<USER>fasterstart}} that implements these changes and will be cleaned up."""
comments: "This ticket implements a checkpoint safety barrier by regularly backing up the data every n runs. Very nicely implemented, and I have little to add. A few hangover lines of code have been commented out and can be removed (lines 438, 439 & 540 in fgcmBuildStars.py), but otherwise, all looks good!"

summary: "Explore performance of I/O of LUT linearizer in yaml format"
description: """As per DM-23044, ptc.py from cp_pipe is now able to persist different types of linearizers. One of them is LookupTable (see linearizer.py in ip_isr), with, for an LSSTCam-type device, will have a shape of (#nAmps, #DN) --->(16, 2**18).  This table, along with necessary metadata, is saved as a yaml file.  However, reading this file (~38Mb for the file in the example below) again takes a very long time (~ 3 mins), which is not ideal:     {code:python}  import yaml  import timeit   file = """"/project/shared/auxTel/rerun/<USER>PTC-auxTel2020MAR25-doLinearize-true/calibrations/linearizers/linearize-lut-det000_2020-03-27.yaml""""    def read_yaml ():      with open(file, 'r') as f:          dataLU = yaml.load(f, Loader=yaml.Loader)      return     %%timeit -n2 -r3  read_yaml ()    3min 18s ± 4.11 s per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 3 runs, 2 loops each)  {code}      """
comments: "What happens if you use the yaml.CLoader?" [SEP] "Way ahead of you Tim!    On my laptop (maybe fairer than NCSA, given that I think they are still having filesystem issues... and interactive performance there is certainly miserable), {{CLoader}} reduces the time from ~3 mins 45 sec to ~27 sec.    That's a nice improvement, but even 27 seconds seems like an awfully long time to read 37 MB." [SEP] "For completeness — {{CLoader}} gets me down to 37 sec on {{lsst-dev01}}." [SEP] "Setting team on this to Architecture, because I think it's an architectural/middleware issue. Of course, if [~<USER> simply wants Pipelines to choose our own binary blob format instead of YAML, we could do that..." [SEP] "I'm reviewing DM-23044 so I'm hoping that that ticket explains the structure of the YAML. It might be that YAML is not a good format here. In general it doesn't matter a lot if the text format takes a while to load since we are only meant to be doing that read during initial ingest (but see DM-24055 where I made a b

"""﻿The project is nearing the time when it will want to automatically ingest raw images into a friendly-user Gen3 butler repository. The DBB Endpoint Buffer Manager should be modified to be capable of ingesting raw images into Gen2, Gen3, and Rucio. Raw images will continue to arrive on the DBB gateway node via both the DBB Handoff Buffer Manager as well as the rsync raw image transfer service. The ingests into Gen2, Gen3 and Rucio should be able to work with raw images from either service. This will deprecate the cron job running on the gateway node to ingest rsync'd data into gen2 repos. Ingests will be configurable to make softlinks instead of move file into place. During friendly-user time, Gen2 butler will still be the production mechanism to serve raw images. Problems with Rucio or Gen3 ingestion should not cause delays or problems with Gen2 ingestion. Also during this time, Gen3 will track the file in a posix datastore along with Rucio tracking the file for replications. An assumption is that the raw image should be moved into the final storage location before ingesting into Rucio or any butler repository. These mean that all ingestions (Rucio, Gen2, and Gen3) should be independent. This epic should not be dependent upon whether Rucio or Gen3 ingestion is ready for deployment. This epic should assume that python code will be provided that:    * Will take a filename (including path) to ingest  * Will take a dictionary of configuration value(s) (may end up including name of other config file)  * Will throw an exception on error (Actual interface to be determined by design and prototyping)    This ticket had been created automatically  Primavera ID: A7830"""

summary: "Extend Qserv Replication system to allow exporting tables/chunks"
description: """h1. Objectives  The proposed functionality of the Qserv Replication System would allow an efficient mechanism for:  * exporting (ready to be ingested) data for external users, LSST sites, or Qserv instances of the same site  * backing up catalog data  * _in-place_ re-partitioning and re-ingesting catalogs if a change in the partitioning configuration was required    This functionality would be especially useful (or be the only option) if the original (source) of data used for ingesting a catalog is either not-available, or it's difficult to use for (re-)ingesting the catalog.    h1. Implementation details  Here is a summary of changes/additions to the existing code:  * extending system's Configuration service to allow specifying an internal (at workers) _export_ directory where the *{{CSV}}*/*{{TSV}}*-formatted dumps of the tables/chunks would be temporarily stored before returned them to clients/requestors  * adding the _export_ service to be run at each Replication System's worker  * adding the client API (library) for interactions with the workers _export_ services  * adding a binary applications build upon the API for retrieving the data from the _export_ services and writing them as local files  * extending the REST API of the *Master Replication Controller* for retrieving connection parameters of the above mentioned _export_ services needed to retrieve a content of a _regular_ table or a _chunk_ (or _chunks_) of a _partitioned_ table  * unit and/or integration tests where it may seem feasible"""
comments: "PR: https://github.com/lsst/qserv/pull/529" [SEP] "I showed it to my cat and it says that you have problem with abstractions and that replication is now more complex than the rest of Qserv and it refuses to understand it. I'm going to follow the lead and resign myself from reviewers as I have even more complex stuff in middleware to figure out.  " [SEP] "It looks good but I left some comments in the pull request. I'm removing my name from the list of reviewers."

summary: "Re-ingesting SDSS Stripe82 catalog (all tables) into LSP-INT"
description: """h1. Objectives  This ticket represents the next step in a broader effort of re-partitioning and re-ingesting all existing catalogs into {{lsp-int}}.     All catalogs will be partitioned with the following set of the partitioning parameters:    *NOTE*: the only change in this set compared to a configuration used for partitioning existing catalogs is in *{{num-sub-stripes}}* which was set to *{{12}}*. The main problem with this value is that it make the _overlap_ tables *{{x2}}* times bigger than the corresponding chunks. Reducing this number to *{{3}}* will make the _overlap_ tables *{{x2}}* times smaller then the corresponding chunk tables, thus reducing the amount of disk space taken by the catalogs and improving the overall performance of Qserv for the _shared scans_.    h1. Implementation notes  * Tables will be ingested into a new database: *{{sdss_stripe82_02}}*  """

summary: "Failure to flatten or filter chained collections in queries"
description: """https://lsstc.slack.com/archives/C2JPT1KB7/p1585184879129400"""
comments: "[~<USER>, here's the cleaned-up fix for the bug you discovered yesterday (just added a test).  I've rebased on master so you'll need to regenerate your repo in order to use it now.    The problem was that the {{...}}-expression code path for querying collections had a short-circuit that didn't obey the {{flattenChains}} and {{includeChains}} arguments, and hence the code where you saw the exception was saying ""don't give me chained collections, just their children"" but was getting the chained collections anyway.  The fix is to have that code path delegate to the same helper function (\{\{_yieldCollectionRecords}}) as the other ones.    (I'm hoping that with that description the review is pretty trivial, even if you don't have any background understanding of the surrounding code.)" [SEP] "Thanks for the quick fix!"

summary: "Investigate the possibility to improve the release process"
description: """Considering the actual release process, made of multiple steps, investigate is there is a way to improve the process. Possible ideas:   * remove unnecessary steps, if any   * split the release pipeline in smaller blocks   * promote existing builds to be a release"""
comments: "Summary on the proposed ideas:   * All steps are needed (if we build)   * Splitting the pipeline definition, we will not gain in speed   * Promote existing build, may require a full new implementation on top of what we have already    We could add some logic   * When we start the process, if the release candidate is exactly the same a weekly   ** This duplicate stuff in eups.lsst.codes, but should not be a problem   ** This requires to play a bit more with eups and see which possibilities it offers   * We could just do a git tag over the same tag   * But we need to generate src and binary pkgs, and this is what consumes time   * We could try to simplify this, seeing if it is possible just to do an eups copy of the existing package       * If the official release is then equal to the first release candidate we can do the same as above   * Or, we should build, exactly as we do now.   * All actual steps are required   * In this optic, we would introduce extra logic, that maybe not as beneficial as expected

summary: "Qserv fails to JOIN result sets of two (or many) tables which have a non-trivial overlap in column names"
description: """The latest version of Qserv is unable to handle result sets of a query representing a {{JOIN}} of two (and, presumably, many) tables. Here is an example of such query launched at NCSA agains the Qserv instance {{lsp-int}}:    This query fails due to the following complain:  {code}  ERROR 4110 (Proxy) at line 1: Query processing error: QI=569202: Failed to instantiate query: Error creating table:result_569202: Error applying sql: Error 1060: Duplicate column name 'qserv_trans_id' Unable to execute query: CREATE TABLE result_569202 (`jobId` INT(9),`qserv_trans_id` INT(11),`solution_id` BIGINT(20),`designation` TEXT,`source_id` BIGINT(20),`random_index` BIGINT(20),`ref_epoch` DOUBLE,`ra` DOUBLE,`ra_error` DOUBLE,`dec` DOUBLE,`dec_error` DOUBLE,`parallax` DOUBLE,`parallax_error` DOUBLE,`parallax_over_error` FLOAT,`pm  {code}  *NOTE* that the complain is about a special column *{{qserv_trans_id}}*. There is another column *{{source_id}}* present in both tables.  A workaround for this problem was to _explicitly_ specify columns of interest:  {code:sql}  SELECT source_id,lc.transit_id,lc.band,lc.time,lc.mag,lc.flux,lc.flux_error,    lc.flux_over_error,lc.rejected_by_photometry,lc.rejected_by_variability,    lc.other_flags,lc.solution_id  FROM gaia_dr2_01.gaia_source s,       gaia_dr2_01.light_curves lc  WHERE s.source_id=lc.source_id AND s.source_id=4038784423947097344;  {code}  This query was successful returning a desired result set:  {code:sql}  +---------------------+-------------------+------+--------------------+---------+---------+------------+-----------------+------------------------+-------------------------+-------------+--------------------+  | source_id           | transit_id        | band | time               | mag     | flux    | flux_error | flux_over_error | rejected_by_photometry | rejected_by_variability | other_flags | solution_id        |  +---------------------+-------------------+------+--------------------+---------+---------+------------+-----------------+------------------------+-------------------------+-------------+--------------------+  | 4038784423947097344 | 17493695788622223 | G    | 1713.2098570466237 | 15.2095 | 15543.3 |    44.1311 |         352.207 |                      0 |                       0 |        4097 | 369295551293819386 |  | 4038784423947097344 | 17497788065945061 | G    | 1713.2838568094814 | 15.2007 |   15670 |    31.8942 |         491.312 |                      0 |                       0 |           1 | 369295551293819386 |  | 4038784423947097344 | 19395005121172660 | G    | 1747.5907481256177 | 14.1615 | 40807.5 |    41.8696 |         974.634 |                      0 |                       0 |           1 | 369295551293819386 |  ...  {code}"""

summary: "Cannot do linearity corrections in Gen 3 DECam processing"
description: """{{IsrTask}} has code to construct a linearizer ({{ip.isr.linearize.LinearizeBase}}) from Butler datasets. However, this code cannot be used at present in Gen 3 because:  * {{IsrTask}} does not have a {{PrerequisiteInput}} for linearizer data  * it is not clear where this data should come from in a new DECam repository. See discussions on {{#dm-middleware}} on [March 16|https://lsstc.slack.com/archives/C2JPT1KB7/p1584401758440300] and [March 23|https://lsstc.slack.com/archives/C2JPT1KB7/p1584988007111300]. (In Gen 2, the linearizer is a virtual dataset handled by the {{CameraMapper}}, but everybody agrees that in Gen 3 it should be stored in the repository proper.)    Please provide a way to make the linearizer data available in Gen 3 DECam repositories, both in from-scratch repositories (see DM-21862) and in repositories converted from Gen 2."""

description: """Felis is our standard for representing metadata about tabular data stored in relational databases.  A similar system, TAP_SCHEMA v1.1, encodes similar information.    This issue is to explore how straightforward a direct mapping of TAP_SCHEMA to felis description files is."""
comments: "I have done the initial exploration here.  There is a more complete readme in the resulting [repository|https://github.com/SimonKrughoff/tap-schema-felis], but the high level summary is:    * I chose to start with the TAP_SCHEMA tables from gaia as I didn't see another obvious example.  * Much of the same information, column names, datatypes, units, etc. are stored in TAP_SCHEMA in a way that easily maps to felis.  * Felis is meant to also contain database specific information, for example the primary key of each table.  TAP_SCHEMA does not have a mechanism for storing this information specifically.    The takeaway is that a bulk of the translation can be automated, but I don't believe there is a way to completely automate it.  I think it will always require some human intervention unless TAP_SCHEMA is extended to encode extra information.    One note is that I was not able to look into the foreign key relationships as described in the TAP spec since gaia does not implement that aspect." [SEP] "[~<USER> will

description: """Per the screenshot, the allAxesInPosition event is not being sent out as expected. This event is needed to know when image acquisition can begin.    Note that the """"Sticking"""" issue reported in CAP-485 might be a result of this issue. """
comments: "The in-position mount event logic was refined to ensure that no state transitions were lost.    A producer-consumer model was put in place so that now transitions from every axis are buffered in FIFOs for later consumption by the lower priority mount task. Short description follows and VI captures uploaded for reference.    For each axis, the 1KHz high-priority task monitors the error value between the pointing kernel demand and the output of the trajectory generator. If the error is bigger than the ini file threshold for 150ms then the in-position flag goes low. If the error is smaller than the ini file threshold for 1s then the flag goes high. Whenever the flag changes its state, its new value gets buffered in a RT-FIFO.    The low-priority 20Hz mount task consumes the contents of all RT-FIFOS, and depending on the currently selected port declares if the mount is in position or not. Mount and axes transitions produce events that are published in SAL indicating if in-position or not.    The above replaces t

summary: "Standardize Gen3 instrument class names and location"
description: """Currently the gen3 instrument classes have no consistency in naming or module path. This adds to the confusion. Currently we have:    * lsst.obs.subaru.gen3.hsc.instrument.HyperSuprimeCam  * lsst.obs.decam.instrument.DarkEnergyCamera  * lsst.obs.lsst.gen3.instrument.LatissInstrument  * lsst.obs.lsst.gen3.instrument.Ts8Instrument  * lsst.obs.lsst.gen3.instrument.Ts3Instrument  * lsst.obs.lsst.gen3.instrument.UcdCamInstrument  * lsst.obs.lsst.gen3.instrument.PhosimInstrument  * lsst.obs.lsst.gen3.instrument.ImsimInstrument  * lsst.obs.lsst.gen3.instrument.LsstComCamInstrument  * lsst.obs.lsst.gen3.instrument.LsstCamInstrument    corresponding to instrument names of HSC, DECam, LATISS, LSST-TS8, LSST-TS3, LSST-UCDCam, PhoSim, LSST-ImSim, LSST-ComCam, lsstCam.    Instrument names have to be unique in Gen3 since telescope is not part of the data model. This is why TS8 is not TS8. lsstCam should probably be LSSTCam.    This ticket will try to clean things up a bit.    obs_cfht has no gen3 support at this time."""
comments: "We definitely need to get rid of the `gen3` submodules.    I'm personally torn on whether to have `Instrument` in the class name or not. Advantage: it makes it abundantly clear what the class is (it's an `Instrument`! whatever that is). Disadvantage: it makes the name longer in a way that may not actually benefit users in practice." [SEP] "The consensus on Slack naming is that the HSC and DECam names are fine but the LSST ones should drop ""Instrument"" but prefix ""Lsst"" when the name is not obvious (ie LATISS is unique on its own but TS8 is not). LsstTestStand8 was proposed but I think we decided that LsstTS8 is fine (I think the S should be upper case since it's a word boundary).    There wasn't much discussion on the instrument names themselves, but I think I will change lsstCam to LSSTCam." [SEP] "[~<USER> lots of reorganization but no code changes.    I've put the obs_subaru files at the python/lsst/obs/subaru/ level rather than using the python/lsst/obs/hsc/ convention." [SEP] "[~<USER> thanks for ag

summary: "Move gen3 generic curated calibrations ingest code to obs_base"
description: """In DM-23778 I updated writeCuratedCalibrations to support QE curves as well as defects. This is generic code that uses the pipe_tasks read_all function to read standardized calibration data from {{obs_x_data}} packages.  Since by definition these calibrations are standardized we should move this code to obs_base and ensure that all gen3 instruments call it if they have defined an _data package.  The relevant dataset types and curated calibrations would only be created/ingested if they exist in the _data package (which by definition means they are standardized).  The instrument subclasses should only be required to define their non-standard curated calibrations."""
comments: "A lot of this is already done on DM-21016. I do think we should decide how we are actually going to handle calibration products in gen3 and operations before we dive too far into this: I think there are quite a few open questions there, and they affect whether and how to work on this part of the code." [SEP] "I have a fairly solid idea in my head as to how we are meant to handle the obs_x_data standardized curated calibrations. I haven't put much thought into the other types of calibrations (curated or otherwise). This is generic code in gen2 and should be generic in gen3 so will need to move to obs_base. When is DM-21016 going to be ready for review?" [SEP] "I gave up on DM-21016 because there was not a coherent plan for how that data fit in with the rest of the calibrations, nor how to handle calibrations in general. If you want to take it up, please go ahead." [SEP] "I would *REALLY* like to see “solid ideas in heads” turned into documents before code or practice becomes solidified. I think this is e

summary: "Enable support for conda compilers in Jenkins"
description: """Right now, ci-scripts/ccutils.sh thinks it's setting up the Apple provided compiler, but the conda environment, which is activated later, includes the conda compiler ends up overriding that, and sconsUtils can pick that up just fine. This may likely to improperly tagged eups packages.    ci-scripts must first be modified to not die when specifying """"conda"""" compilers for certain jobs.    jenkins matrix should be modified so that a configuration for linux/mac+conda compilers is possible, but probably not the default.    eups packages should be verified to be tagged correctly.  """
comments: "These are the other responsibilities in ci-scripts:    jenkins_wrapper.sh is a wrapper over lsstswBuild.sh  * Verifies Jenkins params  * uses ccutils to verify compiler is setup correctly  * translates some jenkins params  * (unnecessary) checks major python versions  * Makes sure to use the Jenkins workspace's lsstsw      lsstswBuild.sh is a wrapper over rebuild.  * lsstswBuild prints out information about the build and it's environment (in colors)  * It has an extra option to do doxygen build and deployment (create_xlinkdocs.sh)"
